questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most appropriate morphological classification for this anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. Normocytic anemia (Option A) has an MCV between 80-100 fL. Macrocytic anemia (Option B) has an MCV greater than 100 fL. Megaloblastic anemia (Option D) is a subtype of macrocytic anemia characterized by impaired DNA synthesis. Pernicious anemia (Option E) is a specific cause of Vitamin B12 deficiency leading to macrocytosis, not microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a glycoprotein hormone primarily produced in response to hypoxia. Which of the following organs is the major site of its production in an adult?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney. Bone marrow (Option A) is the site where erythropoietin acts, not where it is produced. The liver (Option B) is the primary site in the fetus but only accounts for 10 percent in adults. The spleen (Option C) is involved in red cell destruction. The lung (Option E) is responsible for oxygen exchange but does not produce erythropoietin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory findings is the most characteristic feature of megaloblastic anemia on a peripheral blood film?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 nuclear lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemias caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. Target cells (Option A) are seen in thalassemias and liver disease. Schistocytes (Option B) represent fragmented cells seen in microangiopathic hemolytic anemias. Hypochromic microcytes (Option D) are seen in iron deficiency. Spherocytes (Option E) are seen in hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old male is diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia. Which of the following is the most common cause of this condition in adult males in clinical practice?
questions.explanation: In adult males and postmenopausal females, iron deficiency anemia is considered caused by chronic gastrointestinal blood loss (e.g., occult malignancy) until proven otherwise. Increased physiological demand (Option A) is common in pregnancy or infancy. Dietary deficiency (Option B) is rare in developed countries except in strict vegetarians. Malabsorption (Option C) occurs in Celiac disease but is less common than blood loss. Hookworm infestation (Option E) is a common cause globally but less frequent than malignancy-related GI loss in older adults in many regions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following screening tests is used specifically to evaluate the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) assesses the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways (Factors X, V, II, I). Bleeding time (Option A) assesses platelet function and vascular response. APTT (Option B) assesses the intrinsic and common pathways. Platelet count (Option D) evaluates the quantity of platelets. Clot retraction (Option E) is an older test for platelet function and fibrinogen level.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the context of reactive leukocytosis, a shift to the left in the white blood cell differential count refers to an increase in the proportion of which cells?
questions.explanation: A left shift refers to the release of immature neutrophil precursors (like band cells, metamyelocytes) from the bone marrow into the blood, usually during acute infection. Mature lymphocytes (Option A) increase in viral infections. Monocytes (Option C) increase in chronic infections like tuberculosis. Eosinophils (Option D) increase in parasitic infections or allergies. Plasma cells (Option E) are typically found in the bone marrow and their presence in blood is abnormal.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group O-negative is often referred to as the universal donor for packed red blood cell transfusions. This is because their red cells lack which of the following?
questions.explanation: O-negative red cells lack A, B, and Rh (D) antigens, meaning they will not be hemolyzed by the recipient's pre-existing anti-A, anti-B, or anti-D antibodies. Plasma antibodies (Option A) are present in Group O plasma (anti-A and anti-B), but packed cells contain minimal plasma. H substance (Option C) is the precursor for A and B antigens and is present on O cells. HLA antigens (Option D) are present on nucleated cells, not mature RBCs. Platelet receptors (Option E) are irrelevant to ABO grouping.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) is a hematopoietic growth factor used clinically. What is its primary therapeutic effect?
questions.explanation: G-CSF specifically stimulates the bone marrow to produce and release neutrophils into the circulation. Increasing platelet production (Option A) is the function of Thrombopoietin. Stimulating RBC maturation (Option B) is the function of Erythropoietin. Activating complement (Option D) is part of the innate immune response, not a G-CSF function. Suppressing the immune response (Option E) is the opposite of G-CSF's goal, which is to treat neutropenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by a benign increase in the number of circulating white blood cells above 11,000 per microliter?
questions.explanation: Leukocytosis is a benign, reactive increase in the total white blood cell count. Leukopenia (Option A) is a decrease in WBCs. Leukemia (Option B) is a malignant proliferation of WBCs. Lymphoma (Option D) is a malignancy of lymphoid tissue. Aplastic anemia (Option E) involves a decrease in all cell lines (pancytopenia).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Platelets play a vital role in hemostasis. What is the average lifespan of a platelet in the peripheral blood?
questions.explanation: Platelets circulate in the blood for approximately 7 to 10 days before being sequestered by the spleen. 120 days (Option A) is the lifespan of an erythrocyte. 1 to 2 days (Option C) is the lifespan of some activated granulocytes. 30 days (Option D) is not a standard lifespan for blood cells. 6 hours (Option E) is the approximate half-life of some neutrophils in the blood.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected iron deficiency anemia, which of the following sets of laboratory results would most likely confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is characterized by low serum iron, low ferritin (depleted stores), and high Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) as the liver produces more transferrin to capture iron. Low TIBC/High Ferritin (Option A) is typical of Anemia of Chronic Disease. High Serum Iron/High Ferritin (Option C) suggests hemochromatosis or iron overload. Low ferritin alone (Option D) is the earliest sign but the full diagnosis includes high TIBC and low iron. Normal TIBC/Normal Ferritin (Option E) excludes iron deficiency.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Normal Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction occurs when a patient receives ABO-incompatible blood. What is the primary immunologic mechanism responsible for the immediate cell lysis?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are Type II hypersensitivity reactions where naturally occurring IgM antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to the donor's RBC antigens, activating the classical complement pathway and causing intravascular hemolysis. Type I (Option A) involves mast cell degranulation. Type III (Option C) involves deposition of complexes in vessels. Type IV (Option D) is delayed and cell-mediated. Graft versus host (Option E) involves donor lymphocytes attacking recipient tissues.

options.B: Type II Hypersensitivity (Complement-mediated lysis via IgM)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old strict vegan female presents with numbness in her feet and a smooth, sore tongue. Laboratory tests show a Macrocytic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Strict vegans are at risk of Vitamin B12 deficiency as B12 is found primarily in animal products. Neurological symptoms (numbness) are specific to B12 deficiency and are not seen in folate deficiency. Iron deficiency (Option A) and Thalassemia (Option D) cause microcytic anemia. Folate deficiency (Option B) causes macrocytic anemia but lacks the subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neurological signs). Sideroblastic anemia (Option E) is usually microcytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Eosinophilia is most likely to be observed in a peripheral blood smear of a patient with which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is commonly associated with allergic disorders (like asthma, hay fever, drug reactions) and parasitic infestations. Acute MI (Option A) and bacterial infections (Option B) cause neutrophilia. Typhoid fever (Option D) is classic for causing leukopenia/neutropenia. Iron deficiency (Option E) affects red cells, not eosinophil count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 10-year-old boy presents with spontaneous joint bleeding (hemarthrosis). Laboratory tests reveal a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT) and a normal Prothrombin Time (PT). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) involves the intrinsic pathway, thus prolonging APTT while PT remains normal. Vitamin K deficiency (Option A), Warfarin (Option C), and Liver failure (Option D) affect multiple factors (II, VII, IX, X) and usually prolong both PT and APTT, with PT being affected earlier. DIC (Option E) typically prolongs both PT and APTT and shows low platelet counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is found to have microcytic anemia. The Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC count) is calculated to be 10. Which of the following conditions is most strongly suggested by this index?
questions.explanation: A Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC) of less than 13 suggests Thalassemia trait, while a value greater than 13 suggests Iron deficiency. In Thalassemia, the RBC count is often high despite a low MCV, lowering the index. Iron deficiency (Option A) has a higher index because the RBC count is lower. Chronic disease (Option C) is usually normocytic. B12 deficiency (Option D) is macrocytic. Lead poisoning (Option E) can cause microcytosis but doesn't specifically use the Mentzer Index for primary differentiation like Thalassemia does.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a peripheral blood smear interpretation, the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies is noted. These inclusions are most commonly seen following which of the following events?
questions.explanation: Howell-Jolly bodies are nuclear remnants in RBCs that are normally removed by the spleen. Their presence suggests asplenia or splenectomy. Bacterial infection (Option B) may show toxic granulations in neutrophils. Lead poisoning (Option C) shows coarse basophilic stippling. Iron overload (Option D) may show Pappenheimer bodies. Bone marrow transplant (Option E) itself doesn't cause these unless the spleen is non-functional.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) are used to treat anemia of chronic kidney disease. However, they are associated with which of the following serious adverse effects if the hemoglobin is raised too rapidly?
questions.explanation: ESAs can increase blood viscosity and cause hypertension, leading to a higher risk of stroke and myocardial infarction if hemoglobin targets are set too high. Leukemia (Option A) is not a direct complication of ESA therapy. ESA therapy helps renal-related anemia, it does not cause irreversible failure (Option C). It stimulates RBC production, so it does not cause hemolysis (Option D). Pneumonitis (Option E) is not a recognized side effect of ESAs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old patient with a history of deep vein thrombosis is on heparin. He suddenly develops a significant drop in his platelet count. Which of the following is the most likely underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: This describes Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT). It is caused by IgG antibodies against Heparin-Platelet Factor 4 complexes, which bind to and activate platelets, causing both thrombocytopenia and a prothrombotic state. Heparin does not suppress bone marrow (Option A) or megakaryocytes (Option D). Sequestration (Option B) and splenic filtration (Option E) are not the mechanisms for HIT.

options.B: Sequestration of platelets in the lungs
options.D: Direct toxic effect of heparin on megakaryocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of leukemias, the presence of the Auer rod in the cytoplasm of a blast cell is pathognomonic for which of the following?
questions.explanation: Auer rods are needle-like cytoplasmic inclusions consisting of crystallized granules found only in myeloid blasts, making them pathognomonic for AML. ALL (Option A) involves lymphoid blasts which never contain Auer rods. CLL (Option B) involves mature-looking lymphocytes. CML (Option D) is characterized by the Philadelphia chromosome and a full spectrum of maturing myeloid cells, but Auer rods are a feature of the "blast crisis" (transformation to AML). Multiple Myeloma (Option E) involves plasma cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following is the most likely classification of her anemia?
questions.explanation: An MCV below 80 fL is defined as microcytic, and since iron deficiency is the most common cause, it is typically hypochromic. Option A refers to megaloblastic anemia (MCV over 100). Option C occurs in acute blood loss or chronic disease. Option D is a rare or non-standard combination. Option E is incorrect as normocytic refers to a normal MCV (80-100 fL).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production of platelets from megakaryocytes?
questions.explanation: Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the key regulator of platelet production. Option A stimulates red blood cell production. Option B specifically increases neutrophils. Option D is a multi-lineage stimulator but not the primary factor for platelets. Option E stimulates both granulocytes and monocytes but is less specific than TPO for platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear examination reveals the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is most characteristic of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic diagnostic hallmark of megaloblastic anemia caused by Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency. Option A shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Option C shows pancytopenia with normal cell morphology. Option D shows target cells and microcytosis. Option E shows drepanocytes (sickle cells).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group O has which of the following antigens on their red blood cell surface?
questions.explanation: Blood group O is characterized by the absence of both A and B antigens on the erythrocyte surface. Option A belongs to group A. Option B belongs to group B. Option C belongs to group AB. Option E refers to the Rh system (Rh positive), which is independent of the ABO antigens.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of reactive leukocytosis characterized specifically by neutrophilia?
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections are the most common trigger for increased neutrophil counts (neutrophilia). Option A usually causes lymphocytosis. Option B and D typically lead to eosinophilia. Option E is a malignant proliferation, not a reactive process.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with petechiae and ecchymoses. The platelet count is 20,000/microL. What is the correct term for this laboratory finding?
questions.explanation: A reduction in platelet count below the normal range (150,000-450,000/microL) is termed thrombocytopenia. Option A is an increase in platelets. Option B is a decrease in white blood cells. Option D is a decrease in all three blood cell lines. Option E is an increase in red blood cell mass.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory parameters is used to measure the average size of a red blood cell?
questions.explanation: MCV measures the average volume (size) of red cells. Option A measures the average amount of hemoglobin per cell. Option B measures the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Option D measures the variation in red cell size (anisocytosis). Option E measures the rate of red cell production by the marrow.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During the investigation of iron deficiency anemia, which of the following is the most sensitive early laboratory indicator of depleted iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the first lab value to decrease in iron deficiency. Option A and E are late findings. Option C fluctuates and is less reliable than ferritin. Option D increases as a compensatory mechanism but is less specific than a low ferritin level.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is classified as a benign disorder of white blood cells?
questions.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis is a self-limiting, reactive lymphocytosis caused by the Epstein-Barr virus. Options A, B, D, and E are all malignant (neoplastic) disorders of the hematopoietic or lymphoid systems.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary site of erythropoietin production in an adult human?
questions.explanation: Approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A produces small amounts in adults but is the primary site in the fetus. Option B is where erythropoietin acts, not where it is made. Options C and E are lymphoid organs not involved in EPO production.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with chronic kidney disease presents with severe anemia. The physician prescribes recombinant erythropoietin. Which of the following is a well-known potential adverse effect of this therapy?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin therapy is frequently associated with an increase in peripheral vascular resistance, leading to hypertension. Option A is a common side effect of G-CSF (Filgrastim), not EPO. Option C is not a direct result of EPO therapy. Option D is incorrect as EPO does not typically suppress platelets. Option E is irrelevant to EPO therapy.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the pathophysiology of iron deficiency anemia, what is the role of Hepcidin?
questions.explanation: Hepcidin is the master regulator of iron; it degrades ferroportin, thereby inhibiting iron egress from storage sites and decreasing intestinal absorption. Option A is the opposite of hepcidin's effect. Option C is the function of ceruloplasmin/ferroxidase. Option D is an intracellular process. Option E describes transferrin.

options.A: It increases iron absorption from the duodenum
options.B: It inhibits iron release from macrophages and hepatocytes
options.C: It promotes the binding of iron to transferrin
options.D: It stimulates the synthesis of hemoglobin in erythroblasts
options.E: It acts as a primary carrier of iron in the blood

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old male presents with weakness and tingling sensations in his feet. Lab tests show macrocytic anemia. Which finding would most specifically suggest Vitamin B12 deficiency over Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: While both B12 and folate deficiency cause megaloblastic anemia, only B12 deficiency leads to subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neurological symptoms). Option A and B occur in both. Option D and E are common to all megaloblastic anemias.

options.D: Megaloblastic changes in the bone marrow

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with mucosal bleeding. Laboratory tests show a prolonged Bleeding Time, but the Platelet Count, Prothrombin Time (PT), and Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) are all normal. Which of the following conditions is most likely?
questions.explanation: Scurvy causes vascular fragility resulting in a prolonged bleeding time due to defective collagen, while clotting factors (PT/aPTT) and platelets remain normal. Options A and B would prolong aPTT. Options C and E would prolong PT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following findings on a peripheral blood smear is most suggestive of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Schistocytes (fragmented red cells) are the hallmark of mechanical destruction seen in microangiopathic hemolytic anemias like TTP or DIC. Option A suggests hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis. Option C suggests Thalassemia or liver disease. Option D suggests G6PD deficiency. Option E suggests asplenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An elderly patient presents with a very high white blood cell count (80,000/microL). To differentiate between a reactive Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following tests is most useful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in a reactive leukemoid reaction (due to mature functioning neutrophils) but low or absent in malignant cells of CML. Option A is high in both. Option B may show shifts in both, though CML has a more diverse spectrum. Options D and E are non-specific.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Immediately after a blood transfusion, a patient develops chills, fever, and low back pain. Hemoglobinuria is noted. What is the primary immunologic mechanism behind this acute hemolytic transfusion reaction?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are Type II hypersensitivity reactions where preformed host IgM antibodies against donor ABO antigens fix complement, leading to immediate intravascular hemolysis. Option A causes urticaria/anaphylaxis. Option C and D involve different timelines and mechanisms. Option E involves donor T-cells attacking the host.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected Aplastic Anemia, which of the following findings is most essential for confirming the diagnosis on bone marrow examination?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a dry tap or hypocellular marrow where hematopoietic elements are replaced by fat. Option A is seen in sideroblastic anemia. Option C is seen in megaloblastic anemia or MDS. Option D is pathognomonic for Acute Myeloid Leukemia. Option E defines myelofibrosis.

options.E: Extensive fibrosis of the marrow space

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents with severe macrocytic anemia. Laboratory studies show low serum folate and normal B12. What is the most likely microscopic finding in his bone marrow aspirate?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia (whether from B12 or folate deficiency) shows nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony where the nucleus matures slower than the cytoplasm due to impaired DNA synthesis. Option A describes iron deficiency. Option C is seen in chronic inflammation/TB. Option D describes multiple myeloma. Option E describes tuberculosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following patterns of laboratory results is most consistent with the Anemia of Chronic Disease?
questions.explanation: In Anemia of Chronic Disease, inflammation increases hepcidin, which traps iron in storage; thus, ferritin is high but serum iron is low. Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) is decreased as a negative acute phase reactant. Option B is the classic pattern for Iron Deficiency Anemia. Option C suggests Hemochromatosis/Sideroblastic anemia. Option D and E do not match recognized standard anemia patterns.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Normal Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which parameter is used to determine the average volume of a single red blood cell and is essential for the morphological classification of anemias?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) represents the average size of a red blood cell, measured in femtoliters (fL). It is the primary value used to classify anemia as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic. Option A (MCH) measures the average amount of hemoglobin per cell. Option C (MCHC) measures the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Option D (RDW) measures the variation in cell size (anisocytosis). Option E (Hct) is the percentage of total blood volume occupied by erythrocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, MCV of 70 fL, and low serum ferritin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is characterized by a microcytic (low MCV) and hypochromic pattern. Low serum ferritin is the most specific laboratory indicator of depleted iron stores. Options A and E are macrocytic anemias (high MCV). Option B is microcytic but usually shows high iron/ferritin. Option D is a normocytic, normochromic anemia with hypocellular bone marrow.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a glycoprotein hormone produced by the kidneys that stimulates the bone marrow to produce red blood cells. Option A (Thrombopoietin) is produced mainly in the liver to stimulate platelet production. Option B (G-CSF) is produced by macrophages and endothelium to stimulate neutrophil production. Options D and E are multi-lineage growth factors acting on early progenitor cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear of a patient shows hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is classically associated with which type of anemia?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia, caused by Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, reflecting impaired DNA synthesis in all hematopoietic lineages. Option A shows microcytosis. Option C shows drepanocytes (sickle cells). Option D shows target cells. Option E shows small, dark, round cells lacking central pallor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis characterized specifically by eosinophilia is most commonly associated with which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia (increased eosinophil count) is a typical response to parasitic infestations (e.g., helminths) and allergic reactions. Option A (Bacterial pneumonia) causes neutrophilia. Option B and E can cause a stress-induced neutrophilia. Option D (Viral infections) typically causes lymphocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group AB is characterized by having which of the following?
questions.explanation: Blood group AB individuals have both A and B antigens on their erythrocytes and possess no anti-A or anti-B antibodies in their serum. Option A describes group O. Option B describes the absence of antigens, typical of group O. Option D refers to the Rhesus factor, which is independent of ABO antigens. Option E is incorrect as there is no O antigen.

options.A: Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in serum
options.B: No antigens on the red blood cell surface
options.C: Both A and B antigens on the red blood cell surface
options.D: Only Rh antigen on the red blood cell surface

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the first-line laboratory investigation to evaluate the intrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: APTT is used to assess the intrinsic and common pathways of coagulation. Option A (PT) assesses the extrinsic and common pathways. Option B (BT) assesses platelet function and vascular response (primary hemostasis). Option D (TT) measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Option E is a specialized test for platelet dysfunction.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: According to the WHO classification, the diagnosis of acute leukemia requires a minimum blast percentage in the bone marrow of:
questions.explanation: A diagnosis of acute myeloid or lymphoblastic leukemia is generally made when blasts constitute 20 percent or more of the bone marrow nucleated cells. Percentages below this (Option A, B) may suggest myelodysplastic syndromes. Options D and E are significantly higher than the diagnostic threshold.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is primarily mediated by which of the following mechanisms?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions occur when preformed antibodies in the recipient (e.g., anti-A) bind to donor red cell antigens, triggering complement activation and intravascular hemolysis (Type II Hypersensitivity). Option A describes allergic/anaphylactic reactions. Option C involves tissue deposition of complexes. Option D is a delayed T-cell response. Option E occurs when donor T-cells attack an immunocompromised host.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which clinical condition is defined as a reduction in the number of all three blood cell lines (RBCs, WBCs, and Platelets)?
questions.explanation: Pancytopenia refers to a simultaneous decrease in erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets. Option A is a malignancy of WBCs. Option B is an increase in red cells. Option C is a decrease in WBCs only. Option E is a decrease in platelets only.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In differentiating Iron Deficiency Anemia from Anemia of Chronic Disease, which of the following laboratory findings specifically supports Anemia of Chronic Disease?
questions.explanation: In Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD), iron is sequestered in macrophages, leading to high or normal serum ferritin despite low serum iron. In Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA), ferritin is characteristically low. Option A and E are found in both. Option B is more common in IDA but can occur in late-stage ACD. Option D (High TIBC) is specific to IDA; ACD usually shows low TIBC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male with long-standing Vitamin B12 deficiency presents with staggering gait and loss of vibration sense. These neurological findings are due to the involvement of:
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes Subacute Combined Degeneration of the spinal cord, specifically affecting the posterior columns (loss of vibration/position sense) and lateral corticospinal tracts (spasticity). Options A, C, D, and E are not the primary sites of pathology in megaloblastic madness or spinal degeneration related to B12.

options.B: Posterior and Lateral columns of the spinal cord

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is suspected of having a Leukemoid reaction versus Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). Which laboratory test would be most useful in identifying a Leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation: A Leukemoid reaction is an exaggerated reactive leukocytosis where the LAP score is high (reflecting mature, functional neutrophils). In CML, the LAP score is characteristically low. Option B (Philadelphia chromosome) is the hallmark of CML. Option D (Auer rods) is diagnostic of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML). Options A and E are more suggestive of malignancy.

options.A: High blast count in peripheral blood

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During the investigation of a bleeding disorder, a patient is found to have a prolonged APTT, but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and normal Platelet Count. Which of the following is the most likely deficiency?
questions.explanation: Prolonged APTT with a normal PT indicates a defect in the intrinsic pathway (Factors VIII, IX, XI, or XII). Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A) fits this profile. Option A (Factor VII) would prolong the PT only. Options C and D would prolong both PT and APTT. Option E (vWF) often prolongs APTT but would typically show a prolonged Bleeding Time as well.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Recombinant Granulocyte-Macrophage Colony-Stimulating Factor (GM-CSF) is clinically used primarily to:
questions.explanation: GM-CSF and G-CSF are used to stimulate the bone marrow to produce white blood cells, reducing the duration of neutropenia following cytotoxic chemotherapy. Option A requires iron chelators. Option C requires anti-platelets. Option D requires Erythropoietin. Option E requires thrombolytics.

options.B: Accelerate recovery of neutrophil counts after chemotherapy
options.D: Increase hemoglobin in chronic renal failure

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following molecules acts as the master regulator of iron homeostasis by binding to ferroportin and causing its internalization and degradation?
questions.explanation: Hepcidin, produced by the liver, regulates iron by inhibiting ferroportin on enterocytes and macrophages, thus blocking iron absorption and release. Option A transports iron in blood. Option C suppresses hepcidin during erythropoiesis. Option D is an enzyme in heme synthesis. Option E is the protein shell of ferritin without iron.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old boy presents with spontaneous joint bleeds (hemarthrosis). Lab tests show prolonged APTT, normal PT, and normal Bleeding Time. Factor VIII assay shows 2 percent activity. What is the most accurate genetic description of this condition?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) is an X-linked recessive disorder, primarily affecting males. Autosomal dominant (Option A) is typical for some types of von Willebrand disease. Autosomal recessive (Option B) is seen in Factor X or XI deficiencies. Options D and E do not match the inheritance pattern of Hemophilia A.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC), which of the following sets of laboratory findings is most characteristic?
questions.explanation: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy characterized by the depletion of clotting factors (prolonging PT/APTT) and platelets, and the activation of fibrinolysis, which increases fibrin degradation products like D-dimers. Options A, C, D, and E do not reflect the comprehensive consumptive and fibrinolytic state of DIC.

options.A: Low PT, Low APTT, High Fibrinogen
options.B: High PT, High APTT, Low Platelets, High D-dimers
options.C: Normal PT, High APTT, High Platelets
options.D: High PT, Normal APTT, Normal Platelets
options.E: Low Platelets, Normal PT, Low D-dimers

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy showing more than 90 percent fat replacement and profound hypocellularity in a patient with normocytic anemia and reticulocytopenia is diagnostic of:
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a "dry tap" or hypocellular marrow where hematopoietic cells are replaced by fat cells. Option A (Myelofibrosis) shows increased collagen/fibrosis. Option C shows hypercellular marrow with megaloblasts. Option D shows hypercellular marrow with sheets of blasts. Option E shows plasma cell infiltration.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with microcytic anemia. The Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC count) is calculated to be 10. This value is most suggestive of which underlying condition?
questions.explanation: The Mentzer Index helps differentiate IDA from Thalassemia. A ratio < 13 suggests Thalassemia (due to a high RBC count relative to the low MCV), while a ratio > 13 suggests Iron Deficiency Anemia. Options D and E are macrocytic. Option C is usually normocytic and would not typically result in such a low index.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Based on the MCV, which morphological category does her anemia fall into?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. Common causes include iron deficiency and thalassemia. Distractor A is incorrect because macrocytic anemia has an MCV greater than 100 fL. Distractor C is incorrect because normocytic anemia has an MCV between 80 and 100 fL. Distractor D is incorrect because hyperchromia is not a standard morphological classification based on MCV. Distractor E is incorrect because megaloblastic anemia is a subtype of macrocytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production and differentiation of red blood cell precursors in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin is produced by the kidneys in response to hypoxia and specifically stimulates erythropoiesis. Distractor A is incorrect as G-CSF stimulates neutrophil production. Distractor B is incorrect as thrombopoietin stimulates platelet production. Distractor C is incorrect as IL-5 is specific for eosinophil differentiation. Distractor E is incorrect as stem cell factor acts on early multipotent progenitors, not just RBC precursors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group O is characterized by the presence of which of the following on their red blood cell surface?
questions.explanation: Group O individuals lack both A and B antigens on their RBCs, making them universal donors for RBCs. Distractor A refers to blood group A. Distractor B refers to blood group B. Distractor C refers to blood group AB. Distractor E refers to the Rh system (Rh positive), which is independent of the ABO antigens themselves.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with acute appendicitis. The peripheral blood film shows an increase in the total white cell count to 15,000/mm3 with an increase in neutrophils. This condition is termed as:
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is a reactive leukocytosis characterized by an increased neutrophil count, commonly seen in acute bacterial infections like appendicitis. Distractor A is incorrect as lymphocytosis is seen in viral infections. Distractor B is incorrect as eosinophilia is associated with allergies or parasites. Distractor D is incorrect as monocytosis is seen in chronic infections like TB. Distractor E is incorrect as basophilia is rare and usually associated with myeloproliferative disorders.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the normal range for Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) used to classify anemia as normocytic?
questions.explanation: Normocytic anemia occurs when the MCV is within the normal range of 80 to 100 fL. Distractor A and B are in the microcytic range. Distractor D and E are in the macrocytic range.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory investigations is considered a first-line test for assessing a suspected disorder of primary hemostasis?
questions.explanation: Primary hemostasis involves platelets and the vessel wall; thus, platelet count and bleeding time (or platelet function analyzers) are first-line. Distractors A, B, and D assess the coagulation cascade (secondary hemostasis). Distractor E is used to detect fibrin degradation products in DIC or thrombosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with chronic alcoholism presents with anemia. The peripheral smear shows macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia, characterized by macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes), is caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. Distractor A causes microcytic hypochromic anemia. Distractors C, D, and E do not typically produce hypersegmented neutrophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with chronic kidney disease is receiving recombinant human erythropoietin. Which of the following is a well-known adverse effect of this therapy?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin therapy is frequently associated with the development or worsening of hypertension and increased risk of thromboembolic events. Distractor A is wrong as it causes hypertension, not hypotension. Distractors B, D, and E are not standard side effects of EPO therapy.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The term leukopenia is defined as:
questions.explanation: Leukopenia is a decrease in the total WBC count below the normal range. Distractor A is leukocytosis. Distractor C is polycythemia. Distractor D is thrombocytopenia. Distractor E is not a recognized term but relates to polycythemia.

options.A: Increased total white blood cell count
options.B: Decreased total white blood cell count

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which antibodies are naturally present in the plasma of a person with blood group B?
questions.explanation: Individuals with blood group B have B antigens on RBCs and naturally occurring anti-A antibodies in their plasma. Distractor B is found in group A. Distractor C is found in group O. Distractor D is found in group AB. Distractor E is not naturally occurring; it develops after sensitization in Rh-negative individuals.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To differentiate between Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA) and Beta-Thalassemia Trait, which of the following laboratory findings is most suggestive of Iron Deficiency?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin is the most sensitive marker for iron stores; a decrease is diagnostic for IDA. Distractor A is incorrect because TIBC increases in IDA. Distractor B is incorrect because ferritin is low in IDA. Distractor D can be seen in both but is more classic for thalassemia. Distractor E is incorrect because a Mentzer index less than 13 suggests thalassemia, whereas greater than 13 suggests IDA.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient develops fever, chills, and hemoglobinuria immediately after starting a blood transfusion. This acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic reactions are usually due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies bind to donor RBCs and cause rapid intravascular hemolysis via the complement system. Distractor A describes an allergic reaction. Distractor B is not the primary mechanism of acute hemolysis. Distractor D describes a delayed hemolytic reaction. Distractor E describes Type III hypersensitivity (Serum sickness).

options.E: Deposition of immune complexes in joints

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow examination of a patient with suspected megaloblastic anemia is expected to show:
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia causes a hypercellular marrow due to ineffective erythropoiesis, with characteristic large precursors showing nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony. Distractor A is seen in aplastic anemia. Distractor C is seen in sideroblastic anemia. Distractor D is seen in myelofibrosis. Distractor E is seen in Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 6-year-old boy presents with painful swelling of the knee joint after minor trauma. Laboratory tests show a normal platelet count, normal PT, but a significantly prolonged aPTT. This pattern is classic for:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) involves the intrinsic pathway, thus prolonging aPTT while PT and platelet counts remain normal. Distractor A usually affects both bleeding time and aPTT. Distractor B affects platelet count. Distractor D prolongs both PT and aPTT. Distractor E affects all parameters including fibrinogen and D-dimers.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) differs from G-CSF in that GM-CSF:
questions.explanation: While G-CSF is lineage-specific for neutrophils, GM-CSF has a broader effect, stimulating granulocyte, monocyte, and even some megakaryocyte and erythroid progenitors. Distractor A is incorrect as it acts on precursors. Distractor B is incorrect as it is not lineage-specific for RBCs. Distractor D refers to EPO. Distractor E is incorrect as its main site of action is the marrow.

options.C: Stimulates a broader range of progenitors including eosinophils and monocytes
options.D: Is used primarily to treat chronic kidney disease anemia
options.E: Has no effect on the bone marrow

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with pale conjunctiva and jaundice. Her peripheral blood smear reveals numerous schistocytes and a decreased platelet count. The direct Coombs test is negative. What is the most likely underlying mechanism of her anemia?
questions.explanation: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) on a peripheral smear, combined with thrombocytopenia and a negative Coombs test, are hallmarks of MAHA (e.g., TTP or HUS). Distractor A would have a positive Coombs test and spherocytes. Distractor C (Thalassemia) shows target cells and microcytosis. Distractor D shows hypochromia without schistocytes. Distractor E (Hypersplenism) causes cytopenias but usually not schistocytes.

options.E: Sequestration of RBCs in the spleen

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old male presents with pancytopenia. A bone marrow biopsy reveals a markedly hypocellular marrow with the hematopoietic elements replaced by fat cells. There is no evidence of abnormal cells or fibrosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by pancytopenia and a dry/fatty bone marrow devoid of hematopoietic precursors. Distractor A involves marrow replacement by tumors or granulomas. Distractor B shows a hypercellular marrow. Distractor D shows hypercellular marrow with dysplastic changes. Distractor E shows hypercellular marrow with a predominance of myeloid cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An elderly patient presents with an MCV of 85 fL (Normocytic), but the peripheral smear shows a dimorphic population of RBCs (some small and pale, some large and oval). Which of the following is the most likely explanation?
questions.explanation: A dimorphic population occurs when two different types of anemia coexist (e.g., microcytic iron deficiency and macrocytic B12 deficiency). The average MCV may appear normocytic, but the RDW will be high. Distractor A typically shows a uniform normocytic population. Distractor C shows uniform microcytosis later on. Distractor D shows spherocytes. Distractor E shows normocytic cells with reticulocytosis.

options.B: Combined iron and Vitamin B12 deficiency
options.C: Early stage of iron deficiency anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a severe systemic infection has a WBC count of 45,000/mm3. To differentiate this reactive leukemoid reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which test would be most helpful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive states (leukemoid reaction) and low or absent in CML. Distractor A is high in both. Distractor B (left shift) occurs in both. Distractor D (hypercellularity) is common to both. Distractor E is non-specific.

options.B: Peripheral blood smear for band forms

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 28-year-old female with a history of heavy menstrual bleeding presents with easy bruising and epistaxis. Laboratory results: Platelet count 200,000/mm3 (Normal), PT 12s (Normal), aPTT 45s (Prolonged), and Bleeding Time 10 min (Prolonged). Which disorder is most likely?
questions.explanation: vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It typically causes a prolonged bleeding time (defective platelet adhesion) and may prolong aPTT (because vWF stabilizes Factor VIII). Distractors A and B have normal bleeding times. Distractor D affects PT. Distractor E affects both PT and aPTT but not bleeding time initially.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her lab reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following classifications best describes her anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV of less than 80 fL. 1. Macrocytic anemia involves an MCV greater than 100 fL. 2. Normocytic anemia falls within the 80 to 100 fL range. 3. Hyperchromic is a misnomer as cells cannot be over-saturated with hemoglobin beyond its physical capacity. 4. Megaloblastic anemia is a subtype of macrocytic anemia, not microcytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a peripheral blood smear examination, the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 nuclear lobes) is highly suggestive of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic morphologic hallmark of megaloblastic anemias caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. 1. Iron deficiency presents with microcytic hypochromic cells. 2. Thalassemia minor shows microcytosis and target cells. 3. Hereditary spherocytosis is characterized by small, dense red cells lacking central pallor. 4. Sickle cell anemia presents with crescent-shaped drepanocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 following an acute pyogenic bacterial infection. Which of the following reactive changes is most likely to be seen?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia (increase in absolute neutrophil count) is the standard reactive response to acute pyogenic bacterial infections. 1. Lymphocytosis is typically seen in viral infections like infectious mononucleosis. 2. Eosinophilia is associated with parasitic infections and allergic reactions. 3. Monocytosis is often seen in chronic infections like tuberculosis. 4. Basophilia is rare and usually associated with myeloproliferative neoplasms.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following blood groups is considered the universal donor for packed red blood cell transfusions because it lacks A and B antigens?
questions.explanation: Group O Rh negative blood lacks A, B, and D (Rh) antigens, minimizing the risk of an immediate immune reaction in any recipient. 1. Group A has A antigens. 2. Group B has B antigens. 3. Group AB is the universal recipient for cells because it lacks anti-A and anti-B antibodies. 4. Group O Rh positive can only be given to Rh positive recipients.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory investigation is used as the first-line screening test to evaluate the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. 1. aPTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways. 2. Bleeding time is an older test for platelet function. 3. Thrombin time specifically looks at the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. 4. D-Dimer is a marker of fibrinolysis, not a primary screening test for coagulation pathways.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary site of dietary iron absorption in the human gastrointestinal tract?
questions.explanation: Iron is primarily absorbed in the duodenum and the proximal jejunum. 1. The stomach helps acidify iron but is not the main site of absorption. 2. Terminal ileum is the primary site for Vitamin B12 absorption. 3. The ascending and sigmoid colons are involved in water and electrolyte reabsorption, not iron.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with petechiae and mucosal bleeding. The platelet count is 20,000/mm3. This clinical presentation is best classified as:
questions.explanation: Petechiae and mucosal bleeding are classic signs of platelet disorders (thrombocytopenia or platelet dysfunction). 1. Coagulation factor deficiencies typically present with deep tissue hematomas or hemarthrosis. 2. Vascular wall fragility (like scurvy) is less common and shows specific patterns. 3. Fibrinolytic defects lead to generalized oozing. 4. Vitamin K deficiency affects coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), not primarily the platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of iron deficiency anemia in adult males in developed countries?
questions.explanation: In adult males and post-menopausal females, iron deficiency anemia is considered gastrointestinal blood loss until proven otherwise. 1. Increased demand occurs in pregnancy and infancy. 2. Dietary deficiency is rare in adults in developed nations. 3. Malabsorption (Celiac disease) is a less common cause than bleeding. 4. Sweat contains negligible iron.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is clinically used to reduce the duration of neutropenia in patients receiving chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) stimulates the production of neutrophils and is used to manage chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. 1. Erythropoietin is used for anemia. 2. Interleukin-11 and Thrombopoietin are used to stimulate platelets. 3. Interleukin-5 is specific for eosinophil production and is not used for general neutropenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old vegetarian female presents with macrocytic anemia. On examination, she has lost vibration and position sense in her lower limbs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency presents with macrocytic anemia and neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration of the cord). 1. Folate deficiency also causes macrocytic anemia but does not cause neurological deficits. 2. Iron deficiency causes microcytic anemia. 3. Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency is associated with sideroblastic anemia (microcytic). 4. Anemia of chronic disease is usually normocytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient’s lab results show: Hemoglobin 9 g/dL, MCV 75 fL, Serum Ferritin 500 ng/mL (Normal: 20-250), and Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) 150 µg/dL (Normal: 250-450). What is the likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by microcytic or normocytic anemia with high ferritin (sequestration) and low TIBC. 1. Iron deficiency anemia shows low ferritin and high TIBC. 2. Sideroblastic anemia involves high iron/ferritin but TIBC is usually normal/low. 3. Thalassemia presents with microcytosis but ferritin and TIBC are usually normal. 4. Megaloblastic anemia is macrocytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction caused by ABO incompatibility, what is the primary mechanism of red cell destruction?
questions.explanation: ABO incompatibility leads to IgM antibodies binding to donor RBCs, activating the complement cascade and causing immediate intravascular hemolysis. 1. Type I is for allergic/anaphylactic reactions. 2. Splenic sequestration is more typical of Rh incompatibility or delayed reactions. 3. Delayed responses occur days later. 4. Graft versus host involves donor T-cells attacking the recipient.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old man presents with weight loss and a massive white cell count of 150,000/mm3. The peripheral smear shows a full spectrum of myeloid cells including myelocytes and metamyelocytes, with an increased number of basophils. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) typically presents with very high WBC counts, a full spectrum of myeloid maturation, and characteristic basophilia. 1. Leukemoid reactions have high WBC counts but lack basophilia and usually have high Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP). 2. AML and ALL are characterized by a preponderance of blasts (over 20%). 3. CLL involves a proliferation of mature-looking lymphocytes, not myeloid cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is diagnosed with a bleeding disorder. Laboratory tests reveal an increased Bleeding Time and an increased aPTT, but a normal PT and normal Platelet Count. Which condition is most likely?
questions.explanation: vWD is unique because it affects both platelet adhesion (increased bleeding time) and Factor VIII stability (increased aPTT). 1. Hemophilia A and B only have increased aPTT; bleeding time is normal. 2. ITP has a low platelet count. 3. Warfarin toxicity primarily increases the PT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with normocytic anemia. Bone marrow examination reveals increased iron stores within macrophages but absent iron in developing erythroid precursors (sideroblasts). This finding is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: In anemia of chronic disease, inflammatory cytokines (like hepcidin) sequester iron in macrophages, making it unavailable for erythropoiesis despite high total body stores. 1. Sideroblastic anemia shows iron in erythroid precursors (ringed sideroblasts). 2. Iron deficiency shows absent iron in both macrophages and sideroblasts. 3. B12 deficiency shows a hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic changes. 4. Hemolytic anemia shows erythroid hyperplasia and increased iron due to cell turnover.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A neonate born to an Rh-negative mother and Rh-positive father develops severe jaundice and anemia shortly after birth. Which of the following is true regarding the mechanism of this condition?
questions.explanation: Rh incompatibility is prevented by giving Anti-D (RhoGAM) to Rh-negative mothers after exposure to Rh-positive fetal blood to prevent sensitization. 1. IgM cannot cross the placenta; it is an IgG-mediated process. 2. It usually spares the first child as sensitization happens during the first delivery. 3. The direct Coombs test on the infant is positive (antibodies are on the RBCs). 4. It is an antibody-mediated (Type II) hypersensitivity, not T-cell mediated.

options.A: It is caused by maternal IgM antibodies crossing the placenta
options.B: It typically affects the first-born Rh-positive child
options.C: It is prevented by administering anti-D globulin to the mother at the time of delivery of the first child
options.D: The direct Coombs test on the infant's red cells will be negative
options.E: It is caused by fetal T-cells attacking maternal tissues

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with generalized bleeding after a traumatic car accident. Lab results: Decreased Platelet count, Increased PT, Increased aPTT, Decreased Fibrinogen levels, and presence of Fibrin Split Products. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy characterized by the consumption of platelets and clotting factors (high PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen) and secondary fibrinolysis (high FDPs/D-Dimer). 1. Hemophilia only affects aPTT. 2. Vitamin K deficiency increases PT/aPTT but fibrinogen and platelets are normal. 3. Heparin overdose increases aPTT but does not consume fibrinogen or platelets. 4. ITP only shows low platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old male presents with severe bone pain and a high fever. Lab tests show a WBC count of 35,000/mm3 with 80% blasts that are positive for Terminal Deoxynucleotidyl Transferase (TdT) by flow cytometry. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: TdT is a specific marker for lymphoblasts (found in 95% of ALL cases). 1. AML blasts are usually TdT negative and may show Auer rods or Myeloperoxidase (MPO) positivity. 2. CML is a mature myeloid disorder. 3. CLL is a mature B-cell disorder and is TdT negative. 4. Infectious mononucleosis shows atypical lymphocytes, not blasts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In differentiating between a Leukemoid reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of a Leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation: A Leukemoid reaction is an exaggerated WBC response to infection where the neutrophils are functionally normal and thus have high LAP scores. 1. Philadelphia chromosome (t9;22) is the hallmark of CML. 2. CML characteristically has a low LAP score because the neoplastic cells are functionally defective. 3. Splenomegaly and basophilia are common in CML but rare in simple leukemoid reactions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20 year old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her lab reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV less than 80 fL. Iron deficiency is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia. Option A and E are macrocytic anemias (MCV greater than 100 fL). Option C and D are typically normocytic anemias (MCV 80 to 100 fL).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a primary growth factor for red blood cell production. What is the major site of its synthesis in an adult?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A is the primary site in the fetus but contributes only 10 percent in adults. Options B, C, and E do not synthesize erythropoietin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear shows the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is highly characteristic of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemias caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency due to impaired DNA synthesis. Option A shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Option C shows a full spectrum of myeloid cells. Option D shows a left shift (band cells). Option E shows pancytopenia with normal cell morphology.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory parameters is the most sensitive and specific first-line indicator of depleted body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels correlate directly with total body iron stores and is the first parameter to decrease in iron deficiency. Option A fluctuates daily. Option B increases but is not specific. Option D decreases only in late stages of deficiency. Option E is a calculated value that decreases but is less specific than ferritin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with Blood Group O positive requires an emergency transfusion. Which of the following blood groups can safely donate packed red cells to this patient?
questions.explanation: Group O individuals have anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma; therefore, they can only receive Group O red cells. Group O negative is the universal donor and lacks the D antigen, making it safe for an O positive recipient. Options A, B, C, and E contain A or B antigens which would cause hemolysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5 year old boy presents with persistent bleeding after a minor cut. Lab tests show a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT) but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and normal bleeding time. Which factor deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Prolonged APTT with a normal PT indicates a defect in the intrinsic pathway (Factors VIII, IX, XI, or XII). Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) is a classic example. Option A affects the extrinsic pathway (prolongs PT). Options C and E prolong both PT and APTT. Option D would prolong bleeding time.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis with a shift to the left, characterized by an increase in band forms and metamyelocytes, is most commonly seen in:
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections trigger a neutrophilic leukocytosis and a left shift as the marrow releases immature forms. Option A typically causes lymphocytosis. Options C and D cause eosinophilia. Option E is a malignant proliferation of mature lymphocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemias, which of the following is categorized as a microcytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Thalassemia is a quantitative defect in globin chain synthesis resulting in microcytic hypochromic red cells. Option B can be microcytic but is less common than Thalassemia. Option C is macrocytic. Options D and E are usually normocytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which growth factor is specifically used clinically to reduce the duration of neutropenia in patients undergoing chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) specifically stimulates the production and function of neutrophils. Option A is for red cells. Option C and D stimulate platelet production. Option E acts on very early undifferentiated progenitors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most common cause of Vitamin B12 deficiency leading to megaloblastic anemia?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells, leading to a lack of intrinsic factor required for B12 absorption. Option A and B more commonly cause folate deficiency. Option D causes iron deficiency. Option E does not affect B12 levels.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old male with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with anemia. Lab results show: Hemoglobin 10 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, low serum iron, low TIBC, and high serum ferritin. What is the most likely mechanism?
questions.explanation: This is Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD). Chronic inflammation increases Hepcidin, which blocks iron release from macrophages, leading to low serum iron but high ferritin (stored iron). Option A is iron deficiency (low ferritin, high TIBC). Option C is megaloblastic. Option D is hemolysis. Option E is hemochromatosis.

options.A: Chronic blood loss from GI tract
options.B: Sequestration of iron in macrophages by Hepcidin
options.C: Failure of DNA synthesis in red cell precursors
options.D: Premature destruction of RBCs in the spleen

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To differentiate between Vitamin B12 deficiency and Folate deficiency, which of the following laboratory findings is specific to Vitamin B12 deficiency?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 is a cofactor for the conversion of methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA. In B12 deficiency, MMA levels rise. Folate deficiency does not affect MMA. Options A, B, C, and E are common to both deficiencies.

options.E: Megaloblastic changes in the bone marrow

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An adult patient receives an incompatible blood transfusion. Within minutes, he develops fever, chills, hypotension, and hemoglobinuria. This acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic reactions are Type II hypersensitivity reactions where preformed IgM antibodies (anti-A or anti-B) bind to donor RBCs, causing immediate intravascular hemolysis via the complement system. Option A describes anaphylaxis. Option C is Type IV. Option D is Type III. Option E describes post-transfusion purpura.

options.B: Preformed IgM antibodies and Complement activation
options.D: Immune complex deposition in small vessels

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old male has a WBC count of 50,000/mm3. The differential shows a marked increase in neutrophils, band forms, and a few myelocytes. The Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score is significantly elevated. This suggests:
questions.explanation: A high LAP score distinguishes a reactive leukocytosis (leukemoid reaction) from CML, where the LAP score is typically very low or zero. Option C would show over 20 percent blasts. Option D involves high RBC mass. Option E involves lymphocytes, not neutrophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is found to have a deficiency in von Willebrand Factor (vWF). Which combination of laboratory abnormalities would be most likely?
questions.explanation: vWF is required for platelet adhesion (affecting Bleeding Time) and also acts as a carrier protein for Factor VIII (affecting APTT). Option A is Factor VII deficiency. Option B is seen in healthy individuals. Option D is ITP. Option E is seen in DIC or severe liver disease.

options.B: Normal Bleeding Time and normal PT
options.C: Prolonged Bleeding Time and prolonged APTT

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the major regulator of platelet production. What is its mechanism of action on megakaryocyte progenitors?
questions.explanation: TPO binds to the c-Mpl receptor, which signals through the Janus Kinase (JAK2) and Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription (STAT) pathways to stimulate megakaryocyte growth. Option A, C, D, and E are incorrect signaling pathways for TPO.

options.A: Binding to a G-protein coupled receptor to increase cAMP
options.B: Binding to the c-Mpl receptor and activating the JAK2/STAT pathway
options.C: Activation of the intrinsic pathway of apoptosis
options.D: Direct inhibition of the EPO receptor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old pregnant woman presents with generalized bleeding and bruising. Lab results: Hemoglobin 9 g/dL, Platelets 40,000/mm3, prolonged PT, prolonged APTT, decreased Fibrinogen, and elevated D-dimers. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DIC is characterized by the consumption of platelets and clotting factors (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen) and secondary fibrinolysis (elevated D-dimers). Option A shows isolated low platelets. Option B only prolongs APTT. Option D prolongs PT/APTT but D-dimer is normal. Option E has normal PT and often normal/mildly prolonged APTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow examination in a patient with pancytopenia reveals a markedly hypocellular marrow with the replacement of hematopoietic elements by fat cells. There is no evidence of fibrosis or abnormal cells. What is the most likely primary defect?
questions.explanation: This describes Aplastic Anemia. The most common mechanism is T-cell mediated autoimmune destruction of hematopoietic stem cells. Option A shows a hypercellular megaloblastic marrow. Option C is myelophthisic anemia. Option D is seen in polycythemia vera (hypercellular). Option E shows ring sideroblasts and basophilic stippling.

options.B: Immune-mediated destruction of multipotent stem cells
options.C: Replacement of marrow by metastatic tumor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following adverse effects is most uniquely associated with the clinical administration of recombinant G-CSF?
questions.explanation: Medullary bone pain is the most frequent side effect of G-CSF therapy, likely due to the rapid expansion of hematopoietic cells within the marrow cavity. Options A, C, D, and E are not standard side effects of G-CSF.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral smear shows numerous schistocytes (fragmented red cells). Coagulation studies (PT and APTT) are completely normal. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with these findings?
questions.explanation: TTP and HUS cause Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA) with schistocytes but typically have normal coagulation profiles (PT/APTT). DIC also has schistocytes but features abnormal coagulation tests. Option C shows microcytes. Option D shows spherocytes. Option E shows microspherocytes and a positive Coombs test.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following indices is used to determine the average size of an individual red blood cell?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) measures the average volume of a red cell, typically ranging from 80 to 100 femtoliters. Option A (MCH) measures the average mass of hemoglobin per red cell. Option C (MCHC) measures the average concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Option D (RDW) measures the variation in red cell size (anisocytosis). Option E (Reticulocyte Count) measures the rate of red cell production by the bone marrow.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue and pica. Her peripheral blood smear shows small, pale red blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is the most common cause of microcytic (small) and hypochromic (pale) anemia due to decreased hemoglobin synthesis. Option A and B cause macrocytic anemia (large cells). Option D is typically normocytic with pancytopenia. Option E is a hemolytic anemia characterized by sickle-shaped cells, not primarily microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production of platelets?
questions.explanation: Thrombopoietin is the primary regulator of megakaryocyte differentiation and platelet production. Option A (Erythropoietin) stimulates RBC production. Option B (G-CSF) stimulates neutrophil production. Option D and E are early-acting factors that affect multiple lineages but are not specific for platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with Hemophilia A has a hereditary deficiency of which of the following coagulation factors?
