questions.explanation: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by a deficiency in Factor VIII. Option A is deficient in rare factor VII deficiency. Option C is deficient in Hemophilia B (Christmas Disease). Option D is deficient in Hemophilia C. Option E deficiency usually does not cause clinical bleeding.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, which blood group is known as the universal donor for red blood cell transfusions?
questions.explanation: Group O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens on the RBC surface, meaning it will not be attacked by antibodies in the recipient's plasma. Option C is the universal recipient. Option E can only be given to Rh-positive recipients. Options A and B have specific antigens that limit their use to matched recipients.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 nuclear lobes) on a peripheral blood smear are a diagnostic hallmark of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils occur in megaloblastic anemia (due to B12 or Folate deficiency) as a result of impaired DNA synthesis and disordered maturation. Option A shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Option C shows a full spectrum of myeloid cells. Option D shows a left shift (immature bands) and toxic granulations. Option E shows basophilic stippling.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is used to evaluate the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) specifically assesses the extrinsic pathway (Factor VII) and common pathway. Option B (aPTT) assesses the intrinsic and common pathways. Option A and E assess primary hemostasis (platelet function/number). Option D assesses the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is primarily synthesized and released by which organ in response to hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney. Option A (Liver) produces a small amount of EPO but is not the primary source in adults. Options B, C, and E do not synthesize erythropoietin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is a characteristic cause of reactive leukocytosis involving eosinophils?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia (reactive leukocytosis of eosinophils) is classically associated with parasitic infestations and allergic reactions. Option A usually causes neutrophilia. Option C usually causes lymphocytosis. Options D and E relate to iron status and do not typically alter WBC counts in a reactive manner.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary site of hematopoiesis in a healthy adult?
questions.explanation: In healthy adults, hematopoiesis is confined to the red bone marrow of the axial skeleton (skull, ribs, sternum, vertebrae, pelvis). Options A, B, and C are sites of hematopoiesis during embryonic or fetal development. Option E is where T-lymphocytes mature, not where primary hematopoiesis occurs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with total gastrectomy develops macrocytic anemia after three years. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Parietal cells in the stomach secrete Intrinsic Factor (IF), which is essential for Vitamin B12 absorption in the terminal ileum. Gastrectomy leads to B12 deficiency and megaloblastic anemia. Option A is related to green leafy vegetable intake. Option B causes microcytic anemia. Option D causes iron deficiency. Option E does not specifically cause macrocytic anemia.

options.B: Failure of iron absorption in the duodenum
options.D: Chronic blood loss from the surgical site
options.E: Rapid transit time through the jejunum

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory investigation of iron deficiency anemia, which of the following findings is the most sensitive and specific indicator of depleted total body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels correlate directly with total body iron stores; a low level is diagnostic of iron deficiency. Option A (Serum iron) can fluctuate due to inflammation. Option C (TIBC) increases in iron deficiency but is less specific than ferritin. Option D (Hemoglobin) is a late finding. Option E is calculated from iron and TIBC and can be low in other conditions like anemia of chronic disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is most commonly mediated by which type of immunological mechanism?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions occur when preformed antibodies (usually IgM or IgG) in the recipient react with antigens on donor RBCs, activating complement and causing intravascular hemolysis. This is Type II hypersensitivity. Option A is for allergies/anaphylaxis. Option C involves complexes like in SLE. Option D involves delayed hypersensitivity. Option E contradicts the nature of blood group incompatibility.

options.B: Complement-fixing antibodies reacting with donor RBCs (Type II)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 7-year-old boy presents with easy bruising and mucosal bleeding. Laboratory tests show a prolonged bleeding time but normal PT and aPTT. Which of the following is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Prolonged bleeding time with normal PT and aPTT points toward a defect in primary hemostasis (platelets). Option A and B would prolong the aPTT and PT respectively. Option D would prolong PT. Option E would typically prolong all tests (PT, aPTT, and bleeding time) and show low platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hematopoietic growth factors like G-CSF act by binding to specific receptors. Which of the following intracellular signaling pathways is primarily utilized by these receptors?
questions.explanation: Most hematopoietic growth factor receptors (like those for EPO and G-CSF) belong to the Type I cytokine receptor family and signal via the Janus Kinase (JAK) and Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription (STAT) pathway. Options A, C, D, and E are utilized by other types of receptors (G-protein coupled or enzyme-linked) but not typically by hematopoietic growth factor receptors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To distinguish between a reactive leukemoid reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following laboratory findings is most helpful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive leukemoid reactions (due to increased enzyme activity in functional neutrophils) but low or absent in CML (as the neoplastic cells are biochemically defective). Option A is seen in both. Option B can occur in both. Option D is more common in reactive states but not definitive. Option E is common in CML but can occur in severe infections.

options.B: Total white blood cell count greater than 50,000/mm3
options.D: Presence of toxic granulations in neutrophils

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Recombinant Erythropoietin is frequently used to treat anemia associated with chronic kidney disease. Which of the following is a potential serious adverse effect of this therapy?
questions.explanation: Exogenous Erythropoietin can increase blood viscosity and peripheral resistance, leading to hypertension and thrombotic events. Option A is an adverse effect of cyclophosphamide. Option C is a cause of renal failure, not a side effect of EPO. Option D is associated with bleomycin. Option E is associated with phenytoin.

options.B: Hypertension and increased risk of thromboembolism

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear shows red cells with a central spot of pallor and a peripheral rim of hemoglobin, resembling a target (Target Cells). This finding is most commonly associated with which underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Target cells (codocytes) occur when there is an increase in the surface-to-volume ratio, common in Thalassemia (due to decreased hemoglobin) or liver disease (due to increased membrane cholesterol). Option A causes simple hypochromia. Option C produces schistocytes. Option D produces spherocytes. Option E is characteristic of G6PD deficiency.

options.B: Increased surface area to volume ratio (e.g., Thalassemia)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of malignant WBC disorders, how is an "Aleukemic Leukemia" defined?
questions.explanation: Aleukemic leukemia refers to a state where the bone marrow is packed with malignant cells (blasts), but the total peripheral WBC count is normal or low, and no blasts are seen on the peripheral smear. Option A describes a hyperleukocytic state. Option B describes myelofibrosis. Option D describes chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Option E is simply a treated state.

options.A: Leukemia with a WBC count exceeding 100,000/mm3
options.B: Leukemia where the bone marrow is fibrotic and non-productive
options.C: Leukemia where blast cells are present in the marrow but absent in the peripheral blood
options.D: Leukemia consisting only of mature lymphocytes
options.E: Leukemia that has successfully achieved clinical remission

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient suspected of having a bleeding disorder is found to have a normal platelet count, normal PT, and normal aPTT, but a significantly prolonged bleeding time. Deficiency of which of the following is most likely?
questions.explanation: von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. vWF is required for platelet adhesion to the subendothelium; hence its deficiency prolongs bleeding time. Since it also carries Factor VIII, aPTT may be slightly prolonged, but in mild cases, it remains normal. PT is always normal. Options A and C would prolong PT and aPTT. Option D would show normal standard tests but impaired wound healing. Option E is a pro-thrombotic state.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20 year old male undergoes a routine blood count. The lab report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 85 fL. How would this anemia be classified based on red cell indices?
questions.explanation: Normocytic anemia is defined by an MCV within the normal range of 80 to 100 fL. Debunking: A is incorrect because microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV less than 80 fL. B is incorrect because macrocytic anemia is defined by an MCV greater than 100 fL. D is incorrect because hyperchromic is not a standard morphological classification as the MCHC has a physiological ceiling. E is incorrect because hypochromic refers to MCH/MCHC (color), not the cell size (MCV).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is considered the most sensitive early laboratory marker for depleted iron stores in Iron Deficiency Anemia?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels reflect the body's total iron stores; a decrease is the earliest evidence of iron deficiency. Debunking: A is incorrect because hemoglobin remains normal during the initial stages of iron depletion. C is incorrect because while TIBC increases in iron deficiency, it is less specific than ferritin. D is incorrect because MCH changes occur only after microcytosis develops in later stages. E is incorrect because target cells are more characteristic of thalassemias or liver disease than simple iron deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5 year old child presents with perianal itching. Peripheral blood film reveals an absolute eosinophil count of 800/microliter. Which of the following conditions is the most likely cause of this reactive leukocytosis?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a hallmark of parasitic infestations (like Enterobius vermicularis) and allergic reactions. Debunking: A is incorrect because viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. B is incorrect because pyogenic infections cause neutrophilia. D is incorrect because leukemia involves malignant proliferation of blasts, not just a reactive eosinophil rise. E is incorrect because CML involves a shift to the left in the entire myeloid series, not isolated reactive eosinophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group B will possess which of the following combinations of antigens and antibodies?
questions.explanation: Landsteiner's Law states that the plasma contains the antibody corresponding to the antigen absent on the red cells. Group B has B antigen and anti-A antibodies. Debunking: A is the profile for blood group A. C is the profile for blood group AB. D is the profile for blood group O. E is physiologically impossible as antibodies against one's own antigens would cause auto-hemolysis.

options.C: Both A and B antigens and no antibodies
options.D: No antigens and both anti-A and anti-B antibodies
options.E: B antigen and both anti-A and anti-B antibodies

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production and maturation of neutrophils in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: G-CSF specifically stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of neutrophil progenitors. Debunking: A is incorrect because erythropoietin stimulates RBC production. B is incorrect because thrombopoietin stimulates megakaryocytes for platelet production. D is incorrect because IL-5 is the primary growth factor for eosinophils. E is incorrect because IL-11 primarily stimulates megakaryocytopoiesis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear showing hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 nuclear lobes) is most characteristic of which type of anemia?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic diagnostic feature of Megaloblastic Anemia caused by Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency. Debunking: A is incorrect because Iron deficiency shows microcytic hypochromic cells. B is incorrect because Aplastic anemia shows pancytopenia with normocytic cells. D is incorrect because Sickle cell anemia shows drepanocytes (sickle cells). E is incorrect because Thalassemia shows target cells and microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected bleeding disorder, which of the following tests is used to assess the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic pathway (Factor VII) and the common pathway. Debunking: A is incorrect because BT assesses platelet function and vascular integrity. C is incorrect because aPTT assesses the intrinsic pathway. D is incorrect because TT assesses the final step of fibrinogen conversion. E is incorrect because clot retraction time is an old test for platelet number/function.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary morphological finding in the bone marrow of a patient with Aplastic Anemia?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a "dry tap" or "empty marrow" replaced by adipocytes (fat). Debunking: A is incorrect because hypercellularity with blasts defines acute leukemia. C is incorrect because megaloblastic changes define B12/folate deficiency. D is incorrect because it is a secondary finding, not the primary diagnostic feature. E is incorrect because fibrous replacement defines myelofibrosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is diagnosed with Megaloblastic Anemia. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the pathophysiology of this condition?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 and Folate are co-factors for DNA synthesis; their deficiency leads to nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony. Debunking: A is the mechanism for Iron deficiency and Thalassemia. B is the mechanism for Hemolytic anemias. D is the mechanism for Aplastic anemia. E is the mechanism for Anemia of Chronic Disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following blood groups is considered the universal recipient for packed red blood cell transfusion?
questions.explanation: AB Rh-positive individuals have no anti-A, anti-B, or anti-D antibodies, allowing them to receive any RBC type. Debunking: A is incorrect because O Rh-negative is the universal donor. B is incorrect because group O has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies. C is incorrect because Rh-negative individuals may have anti-D antibodies if sensitized. E is incorrect because group A has anti-B antibodies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old female complains of fatigue and pica. Laboratory studies show Hb 9 g/dL, MCV 72 fL, and low serum iron. What is the most likely finding on her peripheral blood smear?
questions.explanation: Microcytic hypochromic cells with elongated "pencil cells" are characteristic of iron deficiency anemia. Debunking: A is incorrect because it describes megaloblastic anemia. C is incorrect because it describes acute blood loss or hemolysis. D is incorrect because it describes microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. E is incorrect because it describes hereditary spherocytosis.

options.B: Microcytic hypochromic red cells and pencil cells
options.C: Normocytic normochromic cells with many reticulocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old man with a history of recurrent infections is found to have a total leukocyte count of 45,000/microliter. The leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score is significantly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A leukemoid reaction presents with a high WBC count but is distinguished from CML by an elevated LAP score. Debunking: A is incorrect because CML typically has a very low LAP score. B is incorrect because AML shows a high blast count and suppressed normal hematopoiesis. D is incorrect because Infectious Mononucleosis typically shows lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes. E is incorrect because CLL shows a high count of small, mature lymphocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops fever, chills, and dark-colored urine. Laboratory results confirm hemoglobinuria. What is the most likely immune mechanism involved?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are Type II hypersensitivity reactions where preformed IgM antibodies (anti-A or anti-B) cause immediate intravascular hemolysis. Debunking: A is incorrect because Type I causes urticaria/anaphylaxis. C is incorrect because Type III involves systemic complex deposition (e.g., SLE). D is incorrect because Type IV is delayed and cell-mediated. E is incorrect because delayed reactions occur days later and are typically IgG-mediated extravascular hemolysis.

options.A: Type I hypersensitivity mediated by IgE
options.B: Type II hypersensitivity mediated by preformed IgM antibodies
options.C: Type III hypersensitivity mediated by immune complexes
options.D: Type IV hypersensitivity mediated by T-cells
options.E: Delayed hemolytic reaction mediated by IgG

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old patient receiving chemotherapy develops severe neutropenia (neutrophil count < 500/microliter). The oncologist prescribes Recombinant Human G-CSF. What is the most common side effect the patient should be warned about?
questions.explanation: G-CSF causes rapid expansion of marrow precursors, leading to medullary bone pain in up to 30% of patients. Debunking: A is incorrect as it is associated with Vancomycin. C and E are not standard side effects of G-CSF. D is incorrect as pulmonary edema is more associated with fluid overload or TRALI in blood transfusions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12 year old boy presents with swollen, painful joints after minor trauma. Laboratory results show a normal PT, a prolonged aPTT, and a normal platelet count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) causes isolated prolongation of aPTT (intrinsic pathway) with normal PT and platelet counts. Debunking: A is incorrect as vWD usually also has a prolonged bleeding time due to platelet dysfunction. C is incorrect as ITP presents with low platelet counts. D is incorrect as Vitamin K deficiency prolongs both PT and aPTT. E is incorrect as DIC shows abnormalities in all coagulation parameters and low platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow aspiration of a patient with suspected Megaloblastic Anemia reveals asynchrony of nuclear and cytoplasmic maturation. Which morphological feature best defines this asynchrony?
questions.explanation: In megaloblastic anemia, DNA synthesis is delayed (nucleus looks immature/large), but RNA/protein synthesis is normal (cytoplasm looks mature/hemoglobinized). Debunking: A describes normal late-stage erythropoiesis. C describes early erythroid precursors. D is incorrect because nucleoli are present in early blasts. E describes normal maturation of normoblasts.

options.A: Dense, pyknotic nuclei with scant cytoplasm
options.B: Large, immature-looking nuclei with mature, pink cytoplasm
options.C: Small, mature nuclei with basophilic cytoplasm
options.D: Absence of nucleoli in early-stage blasts
options.E: Clumping of chromatin in late-stage normoblasts

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55 year old smoker presents with a high hematocrit. To differentiate Polycythemia Vera from Relative Polycythemia, which of the following findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of Polycythemia Vera?
questions.explanation: JAK2 V617F mutation is present in >95% of cases of Polycythemia Vera and is a key diagnostic criterion. Debunking: A is incorrect because decreased plasma volume defines Relative Polycythemia (Gaisbock syndrome). B is incorrect because PV characteristically has low EPO. D is incorrect because normal oxygenation helps rule out secondary polycythemia but doesn't confirm PV like JAK2. E is incorrect because PV usually involves panmyelosis (WBC and platelets also increased).

options.E: Isolated increase in red cell mass

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the pathogenesis of Anemia of Chronic Disease, which molecule is upregulated by Interleukin-6 (IL-6) and leads to the sequestration of iron within the mononuclear phagocytic system?
questions.explanation: IL-6 stimulates the liver to produce Hepcidin, which degrades ferroportin, preventing iron release from macrophages. Debunking: A is incorrect because transferrin levels are typically decreased in this condition. C is incorrect because ferroportin is the target that is degraded/inhibited by hepcidin. D is incorrect because EPO response is blunted. E is incorrect because ferritin is an iron storage protein that is high, but not the primary signaling molecule.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with mucosal bleeding and a prolonged bleeding time. Laboratory analysis reveals a deficiency in Von Willebrand Factor (vWF). How does this deficiency primarily impact the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: vWF serves as a carrier protein for Factor VIII, protecting it from degradation; thus, vWF deficiency often leads to a secondary decrease in Factor VIII. Debunking: A describes heparin or antithrombin action. B is incorrect as vWF does not affect Factor VII. D describes Factor XIII deficiency. E describes warfarin action.

options.A: Inhibits the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin
options.B: Prevents the activation of Factor VII
options.C: Reduces the stability and half-life of Factor VIII
options.D: Directly inhibits the cross-linking of fibrin
options.E: Blocks the synthesis of Vitamin K-dependent factors

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 24 year old patient with Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is found to have needle-like, azurophilic cytoplasmic inclusions in the malignant myeloblasts. What is the composition of these inclusions?
questions.explanation: Auer rods are needle-like structures seen in AML composed of fused lysosomal granules; they can trigger DIC if released. Debunking: A describes Heinz bodies. B describes Basophilic stippling. D describes Howell-Jolly bodies. E describes Pappenheimer bodies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Morphologically, iron deficiency anemia is characterized as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency leads to reduced hemoglobin synthesis, resulting in smaller (microcytic) and paler (hypochromic) red cells. Option A and E are seen in megaloblastic states or liver disease. Option C is characteristic of acute blood loss. Option D is morphologically impossible as red cells cannot be over-saturated with hemoglobin.

options.A: Macrocytic normochromic [B]: Microcytic hypochromic [C]: Normocytic normochromic [D]: Microcytic hyperchromic [E]: Macrocytic hypochromic
options.B: Microcytic hypochromic [C]: Normocytic normochromic [D]: Microcytic hyperchromic [E]: Macrocytic hypochromic
options.C: Normocytic normochromic [D]: Microcytic hyperchromic [E]: Macrocytic hypochromic

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with frequent nosebleeds. Laboratory tests show a normal Platelet count and normal PT, but a prolonged PTT. Which of the following is the most likely deficiency?
questions.explanation: Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A) causes a prolonged PTT (intrinsic pathway) while PT (extrinsic) remains normal. Option A would prolong PT only. Option C would prolong both PT and PTT as it affects factors II, VII, IX, and X. Option D is a phospholipid component, not measured by standard PTT. Option E would prolong all coagulation tests including Thrombin Time.

options.A: Factor VII [B]: Factor VIII [C]: Vitamin K [D]: Platelet Factor 3 [E]: Fibrinogen
options.B: Factor VIII [C]: Vitamin K [D]: Platelet Factor 3 [E]: Fibrinogen

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the term used to describe the average volume of a single red blood cell?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) measures the average size/volume of RBCs. Option A (MCH) refers to the weight of hemoglobin per cell. Option B (MCHC) is the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed RBCs. Option D (RDW) measures variation in cell size. Option E (Hematocrit) is the ratio of the volume of red cells to the total volume of blood.

options.A: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin [B]: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration [C]: Mean Corpuscular Volume [D]: Red Cell Distribution Width [E]: Hematocrit
options.B: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration [C]: Mean Corpuscular Volume [D]: Red Cell Distribution Width [E]: Hematocrit
options.C: Mean Corpuscular Volume [D]: Red Cell Distribution Width [E]: Hematocrit

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following white blood cells shows a reactive increase in response to hay fever and parasitic infestations?
questions.explanation: Eosinophils are specifically involved in allergic reactions and defense against parasites. Option A increases in bacterial infections. Option B increases in chronic inflammation like TB. Option C is rare and associated with myeloproliferative disorders. Option E increases in viral infections.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group O possesses which of the following?
questions.explanation: Group O lacks A and B surface antigens, therefore the serum contains both anti-A and anti-B antibodies. Option A describes group AB. Option C describes group AB (universal recipient). Options D and E describe groups A and B respectively.

options.A: A and B antigens on RBCs [B]: Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in serum [C]: No antibodies in serum [D]: Only A antigen on RBCs [E]: Only B antigen on RBCs
options.B: Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in serum [C]: No antibodies in serum [D]: Only A antigen on RBCs [E]: Only B antigen on RBCs
options.C: No antibodies in serum [D]: Only A antigen on RBCs [E]: Only B antigen on RBCs
options.D: Only A antigen on RBCs [E]: Only B antigen on RBCs

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Prothrombin Time (PT) is the primary screening test used to assess which part of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic and common pathways (Factors VII, X, V, II, and I). Option A is measured by PTT. Option C and E are platelet functions assessed by bleeding time or platelet aggregometry. Option D involves plasmin activity, assessed by D-dimer or FDPs.

options.A: Intrinsic pathway [B]: Extrinsic pathway [C]: Platelet aggregation [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Platelet adhesion
options.B: Extrinsic pathway [C]: Platelet aggregation [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Platelet adhesion

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary function of megakaryocytes in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: Platelets are formed by the cytoplasmic fragmentation of megakaryocytes. Option A is the function of neutrophils/macrophages. Option B is the function of plasma cells. Option D is the function of RBCs. Option E is the function of mast cells and basophils.

options.A: Phagocytosis of bacteria [B]: Production of antibodies [C]: Production of platelets [D]: Transportation of oxygen [E]: Release of histamine
options.B: Production of antibodies [C]: Production of platelets [D]: Transportation of oxygen [E]: Release of histamine
options.C: Production of platelets [D]: Transportation of oxygen [E]: Release of histamine
options.D: Transportation of oxygen [E]: Release of histamine

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following values is used to distinguish between microcytic and normocytic anemia?
questions.explanation: An MCV below 80 fL is the standard definition for microcytic anemia. Option B defines hypochromia. Option C is a general definition of anemia. Option D indicates bone marrow response. Option E is a measure of red cell mass but does not classify cell size.

options.A: MCV less than 80 fL [B]: MCH less than 27 pg [C]: Hemoglobin less than 12 g/dL [D]: Reticulocyte count less than 2% [E]: Hematocrit less than 30%
options.B: MCH less than 27 pg [C]: Hemoglobin less than 12 g/dL [D]: Reticulocyte count less than 2% [E]: Hematocrit less than 30%
options.C: Hemoglobin less than 12 g/dL [D]: Reticulocyte count less than 2% [E]: Hematocrit less than 30%
options.D: Reticulocyte count less than 2% [E]: Hematocrit less than 30%

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old male presents with a sore throat and fever. His blood report shows a high lymphocyte count with many atypical lymphocytes. This finding is most suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Atypical lymphocytes (Downey cells) are classically seen in viral infections like Infectious Mononucleosis. Option A would show neutrophilia. Option B would show eosinophilia. Option D would show immature blasts rather than atypical mature lymphocytes. Option E does not cause lymphocytosis.

options.A: Pyogenic infection [B]: Parasitic infection [C]: Viral infection [D]: Acute myeloid leukemia [E]: Iron deficiency
options.B: Parasitic infection [C]: Viral infection [D]: Acute myeloid leukemia [E]: Iron deficiency
options.C: Viral infection [D]: Acute myeloid leukemia [E]: Iron deficiency

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) on a peripheral blood smear are a diagnostic hallmark of:
questions.explanation: Impaired DNA synthesis in Megaloblastic anemia (B12/Folate deficiency) leads to nuclear-cytoplasmic dyssynchrony, manifesting as hypersegmented neutrophils. Option A shows microcytic cells. Option C shows pancytopenia with normal cell morphology. Option D shows target cells. Option E shows sickle cells.

options.A: Iron deficiency anemia [B]: Megaloblastic anemia [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Thalassemia [E]: Sickle cell anemia
options.B: Megaloblastic anemia [C]: Aplastic anemia [D]: Thalassemia [E]: Sickle cell anemia
options.C: Aplastic anemia [D]: Thalassemia [E]: Sickle cell anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected Vitamin B12 deficiency, which bone marrow finding is most likely?
questions.explanation: Bone marrow in B12 deficiency is hypercellular due to erythroid hyperplasia, with characteristic megaloblastic (large, immature nuclei) changes. Option A is seen in aplastic anemia. Option B is seen in sideroblastic anemia. Option D is seen in myelofibrosis. Option E is seen in hemochromatosis or sideroblastic anemia.

options.A: Hypocellularity with increased fat [B]: Presence of ring sideroblasts [C]: Megaloblastic erythroid hyperplasia [D]: Replacement by fibrous tissue [E]: Excessive iron stores
options.B: Presence of ring sideroblasts [C]: Megaloblastic erythroid hyperplasia [D]: Replacement by fibrous tissue [E]: Excessive iron stores
options.C: Megaloblastic erythroid hyperplasia [D]: Replacement by fibrous tissue [E]: Excessive iron stores
options.D: Replacement by fibrous tissue [E]: Excessive iron stores

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which biochemical parameter is typically increased in the serum of a patient with iron deficiency anemia?
questions.explanation: In IDA, the body produces more transferrin to capture any available iron, thus increasing the TIBC. Options A, B, D, and E are all characteristically decreased in iron deficiency anemia.

options.A: Serum Ferritin [B]: Serum Iron [C]: Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) [D]: Transferrin Saturation [E]: Hemoglobin
options.B: Serum Iron [C]: Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) [D]: Transferrin Saturation [E]: Hemoglobin
options.C: Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) [D]: Transferrin Saturation [E]: Hemoglobin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group A positive mistakenly receives a transfusion of blood group B positive. What is the mechanism of the resulting acute hemolytic reaction?
questions.explanation: ABO incompatibility causes an immediate Type II hypersensitivity reaction where naturally occurring IgM antibodies against the foreign B antigen activate the complement system, causing intravascular hemolysis. Option A describes Type IV hypersensitivity. Option C is an anaphylactic transfusion reaction. Option D describes extravascular hemolysis seen in delayed reactions. Option E is the mechanism of febrile non-hemolytic reactions.

options.A: Delayed T-cell mediated response [B]: Immediate IgM-mediated complement activation [C]: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation [D]: Chronic IgG-mediated splenic sequestration [E]: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes
options.B: Immediate IgM-mediated complement activation [C]: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation [D]: Chronic IgG-mediated splenic sequestration [E]: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes
options.C: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation [D]: Chronic IgG-mediated splenic sequestration [E]: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes
options.D: Chronic IgG-mediated splenic sequestration [E]: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes
options.E: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is a key laboratory feature that distinguishes leukemoid reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)?
questions.explanation: Leukemoid reaction (reactive neutrophilia) has a high LAP score, whereas CML cells are functionally defective and have a very low or absent LAP score. Option C is specific to CML. Option D is much more common in CML. Options A and E are found in both.

options.A: Presence of increased mature neutrophils [B]: High Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [C]: Presence of the Philadelphia chromosome [D]: Massive splenomegaly [E]: Presence of immature myeloid cells
options.B: High Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [C]: Presence of the Philadelphia chromosome [D]: Massive splenomegaly [E]: Presence of immature myeloid cells
options.C: Presence of the Philadelphia chromosome [D]: Massive splenomegaly [E]: Presence of immature myeloid cells
options.D: Massive splenomegaly [E]: Presence of immature myeloid cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis for 10 years presents with fatigue. Her labs show Hb 9 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, low serum iron, low TIBC, and high serum ferritin. What is the most likely cause of her anemia?
questions.explanation: Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by normocytic/mildly microcytic cells, low iron, but high ferritin (as iron is trapped in macrophages by hepcidin) and low TIBC. Option A (IDA) would show high TIBC and low ferritin. Option C is macrocytic. Option D has very low MCV and normal/high iron. Option E presents with spherocytes and jaundice.

options.A: Occult gastrointestinal bleeding [B]: Anemia of Chronic Disease [C]: Folate deficiency [D]: Beta-Thalassemia minor [E]: Hereditary Spherocytosis
options.B: Anemia of Chronic Disease [C]: Folate deficiency [D]: Beta-Thalassemia minor [E]: Hereditary Spherocytosis
options.C: Folate deficiency [D]: Beta-Thalassemia minor [E]: Hereditary Spherocytosis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with petechiae, a prolonged bleeding time, but a normal platelet count. Which of the following conditions must be considered?
questions.explanation: Bernard-Soulier involves a qualitative defect in platelet adhesion (GpIb deficiency); the count may be slightly low or normal, but function (bleeding time) is abnormal. Option A has very low platelet counts. Option C has a normal bleeding time. Option D has low platelets and prolonged PT/PTT. Option E has severe thrombocytopenia.

options.A: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) [B]: Bernard-Soulier Syndrome [C]: Hemophilia B [D]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [E]: Aplastic Anemia
options.B: Bernard-Soulier Syndrome [C]: Hemophilia B [D]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [E]: Aplastic Anemia
options.C: Hemophilia B [D]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) [E]: Aplastic Anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following features on a peripheral blood film is most specific for Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA)?
questions.explanation: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) result from mechanical shearing of red cells as they pass through fibrin strands in small vessels. Option A is seen in liver disease or Thalassemia. Option C is seen in G6PD deficiency. Option D is seen after splenectomy. Option E is seen in lead poisoning.

options.A: Target cells [B]: Schistocytes [C]: Heinz bodies [D]: Howell-Jolly bodies [E]: Basophilic stippling
options.B: Schistocytes [C]: Heinz bodies [D]: Howell-Jolly bodies [E]: Basophilic stippling
options.C: Heinz bodies [D]: Howell-Jolly bodies [E]: Basophilic stippling

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy showing more than 20% myeloblasts is the primary WHO diagnostic criterion for:
questions.explanation: The presence of 20% or more blasts in the marrow or peripheral blood is the defining threshold for Acute Leukemia (AML). Option A has less than 10% blasts in its chronic phase. Option C usually has blast counts below 20%. Option D shows mature cells. Option E shows increased megakaryocytes but not myeloid blasts.

options.A: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia [B]: Acute Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Myelodysplastic Syndrome [D]: Leukemoid Reaction [E]: Essential Thrombocythemia
options.B: Acute Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Myelodysplastic Syndrome [D]: Leukemoid Reaction [E]: Essential Thrombocythemia
options.C: Myelodysplastic Syndrome [D]: Leukemoid Reaction [E]: Essential Thrombocythemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory investigation would most accurately reflect an effective bone marrow response to treatment in a patient with iron deficiency anemia?
questions.explanation: An increase in the reticulocyte count (usually peaking at 7-10 days) is the earliest sign that the bone marrow is successfully producing new RBCs after iron replacement. Option A and C reflect iron stores/transport, not marrow activity. Option D and E take much longer to normalize as they depend on the replacement of the entire RBC population (120 days).

options.A: Rise in Serum Ferritin [B]: Rise in Reticulocyte count [C]: Normalization of TIBC [D]: Decrease in RDW [E]: Rise in MCHC
options.B: Rise in Reticulocyte count [C]: Normalization of TIBC [D]: Decrease in RDW [E]: Rise in MCHC
options.C: Normalization of TIBC [D]: Decrease in RDW [E]: Rise in MCHC
options.D: Decrease in RDW [E]: Rise in MCHC

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her laboratory results show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification for her anemia?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV less than 80 fL. Since the patient has an MCV of 72 fL, she fits this category. Option A is incorrect because macrocytic anemia requires an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option B is incorrect because normocytic anemia features an MCV between 80-100 fL. Option D is incorrect as megaloblastic anemia is a sub-type of macrocytic anemia. Option E is incorrect because hyperchromic is not a standard morphological classification as the MCHC has a physiological ceiling.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a primary hematopoietic growth factor responsible for the production of red blood cells. Which organ is the major site of erythropoietin synthesis in an adult?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is synthesized by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A is incorrect because the liver is the primary site only during fetal life. Option B is incorrect as the spleen is involved in RBC destruction and fetal hematopoiesis. Option D is incorrect because the bone marrow is the site of action for erythropoietin, not its synthesis. Option E is incorrect as the thymus is involved in T-cell maturation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A laboratory report of a patient suspected of having iron deficiency anemia shows various iron studies. Which of the following parameters is considered the most sensitive and specific early indicator of depleted iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the first parameter to decrease in iron deficiency. Option A is incorrect because serum iron fluctuates daily and with infections. Option B is incorrect because TIBC increases later in the progression of deficiency. Option D is incorrect because anemia (low Hb) is a late manifestation of iron deficiency. Option E is incorrect because transferrin saturation decreases only after stores are significantly depleted.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old man presents with a high-grade fever and cough. The complete blood count shows a total leukocyte count of 18,000/mm3 with 85 percent neutrophils. Which term best describes this finding?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia refers to an absolute neutrophil count above the normal range (typically >7,000/mm3), often seen in bacterial infections. Option A is incorrect because lymphocytosis refers to increased lymphocytes, usually seen in viral infections. Option B is incorrect because neutropenia is a decrease in neutrophils. Option D and E are incorrect because they refer to increases in monocytes and eosinophils respectively, which are not the predominant cells in this neutrophil-rich count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the extrinsic pathway of coagulation, which laboratory test is used to monitor the pathway and the effects of Warfarin therapy?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic and common pathways (Factors VII, X, V, II, and I) and is used to monitor Warfarin. Option A is incorrect because bleeding time assesses platelet function/primary hemostasis. Option C is incorrect because aPTT monitors the intrinsic pathway and Heparin therapy. Option D is incorrect because Thrombin time measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Option E is incorrect as it is an older, less specific test for platelet function and fibrinogen.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear of a patient shows hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is a classical morphological hallmark of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are characteristic of megaloblastic anemias caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency due to impaired DNA synthesis. Option A is incorrect as IDA shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Option B is incorrect because aplastic anemia shows pancytopenia with normal cell morphology. Option D is incorrect because it shows spherocytes. Option E is incorrect as thalassemia shows target cells and microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A person with Blood Group AB positive is considered the universal recipient for packed red blood cells. What is the immunological basis for this?
questions.explanation: Individuals with blood group AB do not produce antibodies against A or B antigens, allowing them to receive blood of any type. Option A is incorrect because AB group RBCs have both A and B antigens. Option B is incorrect as this describes Blood Group O. Option D is incorrect because Rh status is separate from the ABO antibody presence in this context. Option E is incorrect because H antigen is the precursor for A and B antigens and is present in almost everyone except those with Bombay phenotype.

options.A: Absence of A and B antigens on RBCs
options.B: Presence of both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma
options.C: Absence of both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with small, pin-point non-blanching red spots (petechiae) on the legs and a low platelet count. Which of the following is a primary disorder of hemostasis consistent with these findings?
questions.explanation: ITP is characterized by low platelets leading to defects in primary hemostasis, which typically manifests as petechiae and mucosal bleeding. Options A and B are incorrect because hemophilias are secondary hemostasis disorders, usually causing deep tissue bleeds or hemarthrosis. Option D is incorrect because Vitamin K deficiency affects clotting factors (secondary hemostasis). Option E is incorrect because it is a pro-thrombotic condition, not a bleeding disorder.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis characterized by an increase in the number of eosinophils (Eosinophilia) is most commonly associated with which of the following?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is most commonly associated with allergic reactions or parasitic infestations (e.g., helminths). Option A is incorrect as bacterial infections cause neutrophilia. Option C is incorrect because viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. Option D is incorrect as chronic blood loss leads to iron deficiency anemia. Option E is incorrect because MI typically causes a transient neutrophilia due to tissue necrosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is diagnosed with Aplastic Anemia. What is the expected finding on bone marrow examination?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by marrow failure leading to pancytopenia; the marrow is replaced by fat (dry tap/hypocellularity). Option A is incorrect because hypercellularity with blasts is seen in acute leukemia. Option C is incorrect as it describes megaloblastic anemia. Option D is incorrect as it describes sideroblastic anemia. Option E is incorrect as it describes myelofibrosis.

options.B: Hypocellular marrow with increased fat spaces

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with a history of chronic alcoholism presents with fatigue. His CBC shows MCV 110 fL and his blood film shows macro-ovalocytes. Laboratory tests show elevated serum homocysteine but normal methylmalonic acid (MMA). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Both B12 and Folate deficiency cause megaloblastic anemia and elevated homocysteine. However, only B12 deficiency results in elevated methylmalonic acid because B12 is a cofactor for the conversion of methylmalonyl CoA to succinyl CoA. Option A is incorrect because MMA would be elevated. Option C, D, and E are incorrect because they typically present with microcytic or normocytic anemia, not macro-ovalocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During the progression of Iron Deficiency Anemia, certain biochemical changes occur before the hemoglobin drops. Which of the following happens during the stage of iron-deficient erythropoiesis?
questions.explanation: During iron-deficient erythropoiesis, serum iron drops and TIBC increases, resulting in a decrease in transferrin saturation. Option A is incorrect because ferritin decreases. Option B is incorrect because TIBC increases (more empty seats on the transferrin bus). Option D is incorrect because MCH eventually decreases. Option E is incorrect because FEP increases when iron is unavailable to bind with protoporphyrin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 19-year-old college student presents with sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. A peripheral smear shows numerous atypical lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm indenting the surrounding RBCs (Downey cells). What is the most likely cause of this reactive leukocytosis?
questions.explanation: EBV causes Infectious Mononucleosis, characterized by atypical lymphocytosis (Downey cells). Option A is incorrect as it causes neutrophilia. Option C is incorrect as it causes a chronic granulomatous response. Option D is incorrect because while it causes lymphocytosis, they are mature lymphocytes, not atypical Downey cells. Option E is incorrect as malaria causes hemolytic anemia and splenomegaly.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12-year-old boy presents with painful swelling of the knee joint following minor trauma. His aPTT is significantly prolonged, but his PT and bleeding time are normal. The aPTT corrects when mixed with normal plasma. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) presents with a prolonged aPTT that corrects with a mixing study, while PT and bleeding time remain normal. Option A is incorrect because vWD usually involves a prolonged bleeding time. Option C is incorrect because Vitamin K deficiency prolongs both PT and aPTT. Option D is incorrect because DIC involves abnormal PT, aPTT, and low platelets. Option E is incorrect because it affects bleeding time, not aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD) is often confused with Iron Deficiency Anemia. Which laboratory finding most reliably distinguishes ACD from IDA?
questions.explanation: In IDA, TIBC is high (the body wants more iron), whereas in ACD, TIBC is low or normal (iron is sequestered, not truly deficient in the body). Option A, B, and C can be seen in both conditions. Option E is a clinical sign of any anemia and is not helpful for differentiation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old man with a history of gastrectomy presents with numbness and tingling in his feet. His blood report shows macrocytic anemia. Which of the following pathological changes in the spinal cord is most likely associated with his condition?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency (often post-gastrectomy due to loss of Intrinsic Factor) causes Subacute Combined Degeneration of the spinal cord, specifically affecting the posterior and lateral columns. Option A is incorrect as it describes Polio or ALS. Option C is incorrect as it describes generalized dementia. Option D is incorrect as it describes meningitis. Option E is incorrect as it describes Alzheimer’s disease.

options.A: Degeneration of the anterior horn cells
options.B: Demyelination of the posterior and lateral columns

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with sudden onset of jaundice and dark-colored urine following a blood transfusion. An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is suspected. Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are typically Type II cytotoxic hypersensitivity reactions where preformed host IgM antibodies (anti-A or anti-B) bind to donor RBC antigens, causing complement activation and intravascular hemolysis. Option A is incorrect as it relates to anaphylaxis. Option C is incorrect as it relates to serum sickness/SLE. Option D is incorrect as it relates to delayed-type reactions like the Tuberculin test. Option E is incorrect as it involves donor T-cells attacking the host.

options.A: Type I Hypersensitivity mediated by IgE
options.B: Type II Hypersensitivity mediated by preformed IgM antibodies
options.C: Type III Hypersensitivity mediated by immune complexes
options.D: Type IV Hypersensitivity mediated by T-cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Sideroblastic anemia is characterized by the presence of ringed sideroblasts in the bone marrow. What is the biochemical defect responsible for this condition?
questions.explanation: Sideroblastic anemia results from a failure to incorporate iron into protoporphyrin to form heme, often due to an enzyme defect (e.g., ALAS). Iron then accumulates in the mitochondria around the nucleus. Option A is incorrect as it describes Thalassemia. Option C is incorrect as it describes Megaloblastic anemia. Option D is incorrect as it describes Hemolytic anemia. Option E is incorrect as it describes Anemia of Chronic Disease.

options.D: Increased destruction of RBCs in the spleen
options.E: Chronic sequestration of iron in macrophages

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 28-year-old female in her third trimester of pregnancy develops generalized bleeding from venipuncture sites. Laboratory tests show prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, and the presence of D-dimers. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DIC is characterized by widespread activation of coagulation leading to consumption of factors and platelets, resulting in prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen, and elevated fibrin degradation products like D-dimer. Option A is incorrect as PT/aPTT are normal. Option B is incorrect as it usually only affects bleeding time and sometimes aPTT, but not fibrinogen or D-dimers. Option D is incorrect as it only prolongs aPTT. Option E is incorrect as it is a hypercoagulable state.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of malignant disorders of white blood cells, the presence of the Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) is the defining molecular hallmark of which disease?
questions.explanation: CML is characterized by the t(9;22) translocation, resulting in the BCR-ABL fusion gene with constitutive tyrosine kinase activity. Option A is incorrect because though it has many translocations, t(9;22) is not its defining one. Option C is incorrect as it often involves deletions like 13q. Option D is incorrect as it is a B-cell lymphoma. Option E is incorrect because it is associated with t(8;14) involving the MYC gene.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20 year old female presents with lethargy. Her laboratory reports show a hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification of her anemia?
questions.explanation: An MCV less than 80 fL is the standard definition for microcytic anemia, typically associated with hypochromia in iron deficiency. Option A is incorrect because normocytic anemia has an MCV of 80 to 100 fL. Option C and D are incorrect because macrocytic/megaloblastic anemias have an MCV greater than 100 fL. Option E is incorrect as hemolytic anemias are usually normocytic unless there is significant reticulocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a routine blood examination, a child is found to have an absolute eosinophil count of 1500/microliter. Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with this finding?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a hallmark of parasitic infections and allergic reactions. Option A is incorrect because viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. Option B is incorrect because bacterial infections cause neutrophilia. Option D and E are incorrect because iron deficiency and blood loss affect the erythroid line, not typically the eosinophil count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old man requires an emergency blood transfusion. He is determined to have blood group O negative. Why is this blood group referred to as the universal donor?
questions.explanation: Universal donors must lack antigens that the recipient's antibodies could attack; O Negative lacks A, B, and D (Rh) antigens. Option A and C are only partially correct. Option B and E describe antibodies, which are usually removed or diluted during transfusion of packed cells and do not define the universal donor status of the cell itself.

options.A: It lacks A and B antigens on RBCs
options.B: It contains both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in plasma
options.C: It lacks Rh antigen on RBCs
options.D: It lacks A, B, and Rh antigens on RBCs
options.E: It contains Rh antibodies in plasma

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following hematopoietic growth factors is specifically responsible for stimulating the production of platelets from megakaryocytes?
questions.explanation: Thrombopoietin (TPO) is the primary regulator of platelet production. Option A stimulates RBCs. Option C stimulates neutrophils. Option D is a multilineage stimulator but not specific to platelets. Option E primarily stimulates eosinophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with petechiae and easy bruising. The platelet count is 20,000/microliter. What is the most appropriate term for this condition?
questions.explanation: Thrombocytopenia is defined as a reduction in platelet count below the normal range (150,000 to 450,000/microliter). Option A refers to low WBCs. Option B refers to high RBCs. Option D refers to a decrease in all three cell lines. Option E refers to an increased platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemia based on underlying mechanisms, which of the following is categorized as anemia of increased peripheral destruction?
questions.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis is a hemolytic anemia where RBCs are destroyed in the spleen. Options A, C, D, and E are all examples of anemias caused by diminished erythropoiesis (production defects).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory investigation is used to monitor the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade, specifically in patients on Warfarin therapy?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin time (PT) assesses the extrinsic pathway (Factor VII). Option A assesses platelet function. Option B assesses the intrinsic pathway. Option D assesses the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Option E is an obsolete test for platelet function.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the characteristic finding on a peripheral blood smear in a patient with untreated Megaloblastic anemia?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia (B12/Folate deficiency) typically shows large oval RBCs and neutrophils with 6 or more lobes. Option A is for iron deficiency. Option B is for microangiopathic hemolysis. Option D is for thalassemia or liver disease. Option E is for hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is a benign disorder of white blood cells characterized by an absolute increase in the number of lymphocytes?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytosis is defined as an increase in the absolute lymphocyte count, often seen in viral infections. Options A and B refer to other cell types. Options D and E refer to decreases in cell counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25 year old male with a known history of peptic ulcer disease presents with pallor. Laboratory studies confirm iron deficiency anemia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of iron deficiency in this patient?
questions.explanation: In adult males, iron deficiency is considered chronic blood loss (e.g., from a peptic ulcer) until proven otherwise. Option A is less common. Option B is usually seen in pregnancy. Option D is common in vegetarians but blood loss is more critical to rule out. Option E refers to thalassemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the pathogenesis of Anemia of Chronic Disease, which inflammatory mediator stimulates the hepatic synthesis of hepcidin?
questions.explanation: Interleukin 6 (IL-6) is the primary inducer of hepcidin during inflammation, which blocks iron release from macrophages. Option A is reduced in chronic disease. Options C, D, and E are involved in inflammation but do not directly stimulate hepcidin synthesis as potently as IL-6.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55 year old vegan presents with paresthesia in his feet and a staggering gait. A peripheral smear shows macrocytosis. Which of the following investigations would specifically confirm Vitamin B12 deficiency as opposed to Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is elevated in B12 deficiency but not in folate deficiency, making it a specific metabolic marker. Option A only checks folate. Option B and D show megaloblastic changes common to both. Option E is low in both due to ineffective erythropoiesis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient undergoes a blood transfusion and suddenly develops chills, fever, and lumbar pain within 15 minutes. Laboratory tests show hemoglobinuria. What is the immunological mechanism behind this acute hemolytic transfusion reaction?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are typically due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies fix complement on donor RBCs. Option A and D describe anaphylaxis (allergic). Option C describes graft-versus-host. Option E describes delayed hemolytic reactions.

options.B: Preformed IgM antibodies attacking donor RBCs
options.E: Delayed IgG response to minor antigens

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old female with Rheumatoid Arthritis has a hemoglobin of 10 g/dL. Iron studies show low serum iron, low Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC), and high serum ferritin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by low serum iron but high ferritin (adequate stores but trapped) and low TIBC (body doesn't want more iron). Option A would have high TIBC and low ferritin. Option C has normal iron studies. Option D has high iron and ring sideroblasts. Option E is macrocytic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following bleeding disorders is characterized by a prolonged bleeding time and a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT), while the Prothrombin Time (PT) remains normal?
questions.explanation: von Willebrand Disease (vWD) affects platelet adhesion (prolonging bleeding time) and carries Factor VIII (prolonging aPTT). Option A and B only prolong aPTT. Option D and E usually prolong both PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 10 year old boy presents with repeated episodes of hemarthrosis. Laboratory findings show a normal platelet count, normal PT, and a significantly prolonged aPTT. The aPTT is corrected when the patient's plasma is mixed with normal plasma. What is the most likely molecular defect?
questions.explanation: Hemarthrosis and isolated prolonged aPTT that corrects with mixing suggests a factor deficiency in the intrinsic pathway (Factor VIII or IX). Since mixing studies corrected it, it is a deficiency, not an inhibitor (antibody). Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A) is the most common. Option D would prolong PT. Option E would prolong both PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is suspected of having either Leukemoid Reaction or Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). Which of the following findings on a blood film and special stains most strongly supports a diagnosis of Leukemoid Reaction?
questions.explanation: A Leukemoid reaction is a high WBC count due to infection; the neutrophils are functional and thus have a high LAP score. CML neutrophils are dysfunctional and have a very low LAP score. Option A and E are characteristic of CML. Option D suggests Acute Leukemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy in a 60 year old male with severe pancytopenia shows a marrow cellularity of 10% with the remainder being fat cells. There is no evidence of fibrosis or abnormal cells. What is the most likely mechanism for his anemia?
questions.explanation: The description (hypocellular marrow replaced by fat) defines Aplastic Anemia, which is most commonly caused by T-cell mediated suppression of stem cells. Option A describes Myelophthisic anemia. Option B describes Megaloblastic anemia. Option D is iron deficiency. Option E is hemolysis.

options.A: Replacement of marrow by malignant cells
options.B: Ineffective erythropoiesis due to DNA synthesis defect
options.C: Immune-mediated suppression of hematopoietic stem cells
options.E: Accelerated destruction of RBCs in the reticuloendothelial system

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group A positive is accidentally transfused with B positive blood. Which of the following initial biochemical changes is most likely to occur in this patient's blood?
questions.explanation: In acute intravascular hemolysis, free hemoglobin is released into the plasma and binds to haptoglobin; thus, haptoglobin levels drop significantly. Option A is incorrect because haptoglobin is consumed. Option B is incorrect because unconjugated bilirubin increases. Option C is incorrect because serum free hemoglobin increases. Option E is incorrect as fibrinogen may decrease in associated DIC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following specific hematological findings differentiates Beta-Thalassemia Trait from Iron Deficiency Anemia in a patient with a microcytic blood picture?
questions.explanation: HbA2 > 3.5% is a diagnostic hallmark of Beta-Thalassemia trait. Option A, B, and E are common to both. Option D (increased RDW) is more characteristic of Iron Deficiency Anemia, while Thalassemia trait usually has a normal (narrow) RDW.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this microcytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia where the MCV is typically below 80 fL. Distractor A and B are incorrect because B12 and Folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). Distractor D is incorrect as aplastic anemia is usually normocytic. Distractor E is incorrect as alcoholism typically leads to macrocytosis.

options.A: Vitamin B12 deficiency [B]: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism
options.B: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism
options.C: Iron deficiency [D]: Aplastic anemia [E]: Chronic alcoholism

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for stimulating the production of neutrophils in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) specifically stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of neutrophil progenitors. Distractor A is incorrect because Erythropoietin stimulates RBC production. Distractor C is incorrect as Thrombopoietin stimulates platelet production. Distractor D is incorrect as IL-5 is specific for eosinophils. Distractor E is incorrect as IL-11 stimulates megakaryocytes.

options.A: Erythropoietin [B]: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [C]: Thrombopoietin [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Interleukin-11
options.B: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [C]: Thrombopoietin [D]: Interleukin-5 [E]: Interleukin-11

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old patient with Blood Group A negative requires an urgent blood transfusion. Which of the following blood groups can be safely administered to this patient?
questions.explanation: O negative is the universal donor and lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe for an A negative patient. Distractor A is incorrect because Rh positive blood can cause sensitization in an Rh negative patient. Distractor B and C are incorrect because the patient has anti-B antibodies. Distractor E is incorrect because the Rh antigen (positive) is present.

options.A: A positive [B]: B negative [C]: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive
options.B: B negative [C]: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive
options.C: AB negative [D]: O negative [E]: O positive

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a routine blood examination, a patient is found to have a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with 80 percent neutrophils. This reactive neutrophilic leukocytosis is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections typically trigger a release of neutrophils from the bone marrow, leading to neutrophilia. Distractor A is incorrect as viruses usually cause lymphocytosis. Distractor C and D are incorrect as they typically cause eosinophilia. Distractor E is incorrect as it is a malignant proliferation, not a reactive process.

options.A: Viral infections [B]: Acute bacterial infections [C]: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
options.B: Acute bacterial infections [C]: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
options.C: Parasitic infestations [D]: Allergic reactions [E]: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory parameter is the most sensitive indicator of early iron deficiency before anemia actually develops?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the first lab value to decrease in iron deficiency. Distractors A, C, and D are incorrect because hemoglobin, MCH, and hematocrit only decrease once the deficiency has progressed to overt anemia. Distractor E is incorrect as reticulocyte count is usually low or normal in iron deficiency and is not a specific diagnostic tool for it.

options.A: Hemoglobin levels [B]: Serum Ferritin [C]: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.B: Serum Ferritin [C]: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.C: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) [D]: Hematocrit [E]: Reticulocyte count

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by which of the following findings on a peripheral blood smear?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia alongside macro-ovalocytes. Distractor A is seen in iron deficiency. Distractor C is seen in Thalassemia or Liver disease. Distractor D indicates microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Distractor E is characteristic of Hereditary Spherocytosis.

options.A: Microcytic hypochromic cells [B]: Hypersegmented neutrophils [C]: Target cells [D]: Schistocytes [E]: Spherocytes
options.B: Hypersegmented neutrophils [C]: Target cells [D]: Schistocytes [E]: Spherocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with spontaneous skin petechiae and mucosal bleeding. The platelet count is 20,000/mm3. This condition is best classified as a disorder of:
questions.explanation: Primary hemostasis involves platelets and the vessel wall; thrombocytopenia (low platelets) is a classic primary hemostatic defect. Distractors A and B refer to coagulation factor deficiencies which typically present with deep tissue hematomas, not petechiae. Distractor D and E are less common causes of this specific presentation compared to low platelets.

options.A: Secondary hemostasis [B]: Coagulation factors [C]: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity
options.B: Coagulation factors [C]: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity
options.C: Primary hemostasis [D]: Fibrinolysis [E]: Vascular wall integrity

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is a classic clinical feature of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction due to ABO incompatibility?
questions.explanation: Acute intravascular hemolysis leads to the release of free hemoglobin into the blood, which is then excreted in urine (hemoglobinuria). Distractor A is incorrect because jaundice in acute reactions is not delayed. Distractor C is a feature of allergic reactions, not necessarily hemolysis. Distractor D is a complication of chronic transfusions. Distractor E is an increase in RBC mass, the opposite of hemolysis.

options.A: Delayed jaundice [B]: Hemoglobinuria [C]: Skin rashes [D]: Iron overload [E]: Polycythemia
options.B: Hemoglobinuria [C]: Skin rashes [D]: Iron overload [E]: Polycythemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemias, a Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) that is significantly low indicates that the cells are:
questions.explanation: MCHC measures the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells; low MCHC means the cells are pale or hypochromic. Distractor A refers to size (MCV). Distractor B refers to high MCHC (rarely seen except in spherocytosis). Distractor C refers to normal size. Distractor E refers to a specific type of macrocytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which malignant disorder of white blood cells is characterized by the presence of the Philadelphia chromosome (t9;22)?
questions.explanation: CML is specifically associated with the translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22, creating the BCR-ABL fusion gene. Distractors A, C, D, and E have different genetic associations or are classified differently (e.g., lymphomas).

options.A: Acute Myeloid Leukemia [B]: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
options.B: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia [C]: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia
options.C: Hodgkin Lymphoma [D]: Multiple Myeloma [E]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with chronic kidney disease presents with worsening anemia. He is prescribed recombinant Erythropoietin. What is the primary mechanism of action of this drug?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin acts on erythroid progenitors (CFU-E) in the bone marrow to stimulate their proliferation and differentiation into proerythroblasts.
