questions.question: Which of the following hematopoietic growth factors is primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Logic: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary hormone for red cell production, synthesized mainly in the kidneys. 1. G-CSF is produced by macrophages and endothelial cells to stimulate neutrophils. 2. Thrombopoietin is primarily produced in the liver to stimulate platelets. 3. Interleukin-3 is produced by T-lymphocytes. 4. Stem cell factor is produced by bone marrow stromal cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory investigation of a patient suspected of having Iron Deficiency Anemia, which test is considered the most sensitive indicator of depleted body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Logic: Serum Ferritin levels correlate directly with total body iron stores; low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency. 1. Serum Iron can fluctuate daily and with infections. 2. TIBC increases in deficiency but is not as specific as ferritin. 3. Hemoglobin levels only fall after stores are completely exhausted. 4. Transferrin saturation decreases in several conditions, including anemia of chronic disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood smear of a 30-year-old patient shows hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 nuclear lobes). This finding is highly suggestive of which type of anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic morphological hallmark of megaloblastic anemia, caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. 1. Iron deficiency presents with microcytic hypochromic cells. 2. Aplastic anemia shows a reduction in all cell lines but normal morphology. 3. Sickle cell anemia shows drepanocytes (sickle cells). 4. Thalassemia minor shows target cells and microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is a primary cause of reactive neutrophilic leukocytosis?
questions.explanation: Logic: Neutrophilia is a reactive response typically seen in acute pyogenic bacterial infections. 1. Viral infections usually cause lymphocytosis. 2. Parasitic infestations cause eosinophilia. 3. Allergic reactions cause eosinophilia. 4. Chronic lymphoid leukemia is a malignant proliferation, not a reactive process.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a blood group labeled as O-negative is often referred to as the universal donor. This is because their red blood cells lack:
questions.explanation: Logic: Universal donors must lack the antigens that the recipient's antibodies could attack. O-negative lacks A, B, and Rh (D) antigens. 1. Lacking only A and B would be O-positive. 2. Lacking only Rh would be A, B, or AB negative. 3. Lacking A, B, and H is the rare Bombay phenotype. 4. Universal donors are classified by cell antigens, not plasma antibodies (which they actually possess).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is used to monitor the extrinsic pathway of coagulation and is commonly prolonged in patients with Vitamin K deficiency?
questions.explanation: Logic: Prothrombin Time (PT) monitors the extrinsic and common pathways; Factor VII (extrinsic) is the first to decrease in Vitamin K deficiency. 1. APTT monitors the intrinsic pathway. 2. Bleeding time assesses platelet function and vessel wall integrity. 3. Platelet count measures the quantity of platelets. 4. Thrombin time measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Recombinant human Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) is clinically used primarily to treat which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: Logic: G-CSF specifically stimulates the production and maturation of neutrophils. 1. Anemia of renal failure is treated with Erythropoietin. 2. Thrombocytopenia is treated with Oprelvekin (IL-11) or thrombopoietin mimetics. 3. Polycythemia vera is a state of too many cells, not a deficiency. 4. Hemophilia A is treated with Factor VIII replacement.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops fever, chills, and lower back pain. Laboratory tests confirm hemoglobinuria. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Logic: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) occurs within minutes/hours due to ABO incompatibility, presenting with fever, back pain, and intravascular hemolysis (hemoglobinuria). 1. Allergic reactions present with hives/wheezing. 2. TRALI presents with respiratory distress. 3. Delayed reactions occur days later. 4. Febrile non-hemolytic reactions do not cause hemolysis or hemoglobinuria.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bleeding disorders caused by platelet deficiencies or dysfunction typically manifest as which type of bleeding?
questions.explanation: Logic: Platelet disorders (primary hemostasis defects) typically present with small capillary bleeds in skin (petechiae) and mucous membranes. 1. Hemarthrosis is characteristic of coagulation factor deficiencies (secondary hemostasis). 2. Deep muscle bleeds are seen in factor deficiencies like Hemophilia. 3. Retroperitoneal hemorrhage is usually seen in severe factor deficiencies. 4. Delayed bleeding is common in factor XIII deficiency or hemophilia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old man with a history of total gastrectomy 5 years ago presents with pallor and paresthesia in his lower limbs. A peripheral smear shows macro-ovalocytes. What is the most likely mechanism of his anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: Gastrectomy leads to loss of intrinsic factor, causing Vitamin B12 deficiency. B12 is essential for DNA synthesis; its deficiency leads to megaloblastic anemia. 1. Defective hemoglobin synthesis occurs in iron deficiency. 2. Stem cell failure occurs in aplastic anemia. 3. Increased destruction occurs in hemolytic anemias. 4. Chronic blood loss leads to microcytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected Iron Deficiency Anemia, laboratory results show: Low Serum Iron, Low Serum Ferritin, and High TIBC. What does the high TIBC represent in this context?
questions.explanation: Logic: In iron deficiency, the liver increases the production of transferrin to maximize the capture of any available iron, which manifests as a high Total Iron Binding Capacity. 1. Transferrin is not degraded more; it is synthesized more. 2. Albumin does not significantly bind iron. 3. Marrow utilization is low due to lack of iron, not causing high TIBC. 4. High TIBC means many sites are empty, not saturated.

options.B: Compensatory increase in transferrin synthesis by the liver
options.D: Decreased utilization of iron by the bone marrow

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 7-year-old boy presents with easy bruising. Lab tests show a prolonged Bleeding Time and a prolonged APTT, but a normal Platelet Count and normal PT. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Logic: von Willebrand Disease (vWD) causes prolonged bleeding time (defective platelet adhesion) and prolonged APTT (vWF stabilizes Factor VIII). 1. Hemophilia A/B have normal bleeding times. 2. Vitamin K deficiency prolongs PT first. 3. ITP would show a low platelet count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 19-year-old college student presents with sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Peripheral blood film reveals an absolute lymphocytosis with many large cells containing abundant basophilic cytoplasm that indents around surrounding red cells. These cells are:
questions.explanation: Logic: The description fits atypical lymphocytes (Downey cells) seen in Infectious Mononucleosis (EBV). They are reactive CD8+ T-cells. 1. Blasts have high N:C ratios and nucleoli, not abundant indenting cytoplasm. 2. Follicular lymphoma presents in older adults with different morphology. 3. Toxic granulation is seen in neutrophils, not lymphocytes. 4. Monocytes have different nuclear folding and vacuumed cytoplasm.

options.A: Blast cells indicating Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
options.B: Atypical T-lymphocytes reacting to EBV-infected B-cells
options.E: Monocytes in a chronic inflammatory state

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: When interpreting a bone marrow aspirate in a patient with megaloblastic anemia, which of the following is a characteristic finding?
questions.explanation: Logic: Megaloblastic anemia is characterized by "nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony," where the nucleus matures slowly (due to DNA synthesis defect) while the cytoplasm matures normally, resulting in large immature-looking nuclei. 1. Predominance of lymphocytes is seen in CLL. 2. Absence of megakaryocytes is seen in aplastic anemia. 3. Ringed sideroblasts are seen in sideroblastic anemia. 4. Fibrosis is seen in myelofibrosis.

options.E: Massive fibrosis of the marrow spaces

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Recombinant Erythropoietin therapy is often associated with a specific serious adverse effect in patients with chronic kidney disease, particularly if hemoglobin rises too rapidly. What is this effect?
questions.explanation: Logic: Rapid increases in red cell mass via EPO therapy increase blood viscosity and can trigger or worsen hypertension and increase the risk of stroke or myocardial infarction. 1. EPO does not cause ATN. 2. It causes hypertension, not hypotension. 3. It uses up iron, potentially causing iron deficiency, not overload. 4. It has no direct effect on bone density.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old female presents with severe anemia. Bone marrow biopsy shows a "dry tap" on aspiration, and the biopsy reveals the marrow has been replaced by metastatic breast carcinoma cells. This specific type of anemia is termed:
questions.explanation: Logic: Myelophthisic anemia occurs when the bone marrow is infiltrated and replaced by non-hematopoietic tissue, such as metastatic cancer or fibrosis. 1. Aplastic anemia is primary stem cell failure without infiltration. 2. Sideroblastic anemia involves defective heme synthesis. 3. Pernicious anemia is a specific type of B12 deficiency. 4. Hemolytic anemia involves the destruction of peripheral red cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The pathophysiology of an Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction primarily involves which of the following immunological mechanisms?
questions.explanation: Logic: AHTR is a Type II cytotoxic hypersensitivity where pre-formed IgM (usually) antibodies against donor A or B antigens bind to donor RBCs, leading to immediate complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis. 1. Type I involves allergies/anaphylaxis. 2. Type III involves serum sickness/SLE. 3. Type IV is delayed (like a TB test). 4. AHTR involves the classical pathway, not just alternative.

options.A: Type I hypersensitivity mediated by IgE
options.B: Type II hypersensitivity with complement activation and intravascular lysis
options.C: Type III hypersensitivity with immune complex deposition in joints
options.D: Type IV hypersensitivity mediated by sensitized T-lymphocytes
options.E: Non-specific activation of the alternative complement pathway

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a bleeding disorder has a prolonged APTT. A "mixing study" is performed where the patient's plasma is mixed 1:1 with normal plasma. The APTT fails to correct to the normal range. What does this result indicate?
questions.explanation: Logic: In a mixing study, if a factor is deficient, the normal plasma provides enough factor to correct the APTT. If an inhibitor (like an antibody) is present, it will inhibit the factors in the normal plasma as well, preventing correction. 1. Factor VIII/IX deficiencies would correct. 2. vWF deficiency affects bleeding time primarily and would correct if APTT is long. 3. Vitamin K deficiency involves multiple factors and would correct.

options.C: Presence of a coagulation inhibitor (e.g., Lupus anticoagulant)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In differentiating a Leukemoid Reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of a Leukemoid Reaction?
questions.explanation: Logic: A Leukemoid Reaction is a benign, extreme reactive leukocytosis where the neutrophils are mature and functional, thus having a high LAP score. CML cells are malignant and lack this enzyme, resulting in a low LAP score. 1. Philadelphia chromosome is diagnostic for CML. 2. Low LAP score is seen in CML. 3. Splenomegaly is more common in CML. 4. Immature cells can be seen in both, though more frequent in CML.

options.E: Presence of immature cells like myelocytes in the blood

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following indices is used to determine the average volume of a single red blood cell?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) directly measures the average size or volume of RBCs, helping classify anemias as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic. Option A (MCH) measures the average amount of hemoglobin per RBC. Option C (MCHC) measures the average concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Option D (RDW) measures the variation in RBC size (anisocytosis). Option E (Hct) is the percentage of total blood volume occupied by RBCs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old pregnant woman is diagnosed with anemia. Which of the following is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia worldwide?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is the most prevalent cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia globally, often due to chronic blood loss or increased demand (like pregnancy). Option A and B cause macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia, not microcytic. Option D usually causes transient changes but not microcytic anemia. Option E is a hemolytic anemia that is typically normocytic with increased MCHC.

options.C: Chronic blood loss leading to iron deficiency

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with Blood Group O possesses which of the following in their serum?
questions.explanation: Individuals with Blood Group O have no A or B antigens on their RBCs and therefore produce both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies in their serum. Option A and B describe individuals with Group B and Group A respectively. Option D describes Blood Group AB individuals. Option E refers to the Rh system, not the ABO system naturally occurring antibodies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood film shows hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). This finding is most characteristic of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia (due to B12 or folate deficiency) resulting from impaired DNA synthesis. Option A, D, and E are associated with microcytic or hemolytic changes, not hypersegmentation. Option C (Aplastic anemia) shows pancytopenia but usually lacks hypersegmented neutrophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following cells are the source of platelets in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: Platelets are cytoplasmic fragments derived from large multinucleated cells in the bone marrow called megakaryocytes. Option A (Reticulocytes) are immature RBCs. Option C (Lymphoblasts) are precursors for lymphocytes. Option D (Myeloblasts) are precursors for granulocytes. Option E (Erythroblasts) are precursors for red blood cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a severe pyogenic bacterial infection. Which change is most likely to be seen on the differential leukocyte count?
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections typically cause reactive neutrophilia, often accompanied by a left shift (increased immature bands). Option A (Lymphocytosis) is more common in viral infections. Option B (Eosinophilia) is seen in parasitic infections or allergies. Option D (Monocytosis) is seen in chronic infections like TB. Option E (Basophilia) is rare and often associated with myeloproliferative disorders.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following tests is used to evaluate the intrinsic pathway of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: aPTT measures the integrity of the intrinsic and common pathways. Option A (PT) measures the extrinsic pathway. Option C (Bleeding Time) evaluates platelet function and vessel wall integrity. Option D (Thrombin Time) measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Option E (D-Dimer) is a marker of fibrin degradation/clot lysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is defined as a reduction in the number of all three blood cell lines (RBCs, WBCs, and Platelets)?
questions.explanation: Pancytopenia refers to a simultaneous decrease in RBCs, WBCs, and platelets, typically seen in bone marrow failure. Option A refers only to low WBCs. Option B refers only to low platelets. Option C refers to an increase in RBCs. Option E refers specifically to a decrease in granulocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5 year old boy has a microcytic anemia with a normal RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width) and a very high RBC count. Which condition is most likely?
questions.explanation: Thalassemia trait typically presents with microcytosis but a relatively uniform cell size (normal RDW) and a high RBC count as a compensatory mechanism. Option A (Iron deficiency) usually has a high RDW. Option C and D can cause microcytosis but usually have normal or low RBC counts. Option E causes macrocytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction occurs within minutes of starting a blood transfusion. What is the primary underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic reactions are usually due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies in the recipient fix complement and cause intravascular lysis of donor RBCs. Option A describes an allergic/anaphylactic reaction. Option C describes a delayed transfusion reaction. Option D describes septic shock from transfusion. Option E is a chronic complication, not an acute reaction.

options.B: Preformed IgM antibodies attacking donor RBC antigens
options.D: Bacterial contamination of the blood bag

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12 year old boy presents with spontaneous joint bleeds (hemarthrosis). Lab tests show a prolonged aPTT but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and normal platelet count. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) involves the intrinsic pathway, leading to a prolonged aPTT, but PT and platelets remain normal. Option A typically has a prolonged bleeding time and variable aPTT. Option C affects Factors II, VII, IX, and X, prolonging both PT and aPTT. Option D would show a very low platelet count. Option E would show abnormalities in PT, aPTT, platelets, and D-dimers.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Recombinant Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) is clinically used primarily to treat which condition?
questions.explanation: G-CSF stimulates the production of neutrophils and is used to shorten the duration of neutropenia following chemotherapy or in drug-induced cases. Option A is treated with Erythropoietin. Option C is treated with thrombopoietin mimetics or steroids. Option D is a myeloproliferative disorder where growth factors would be contraindicated. Option E involves RBC destruction and would not be managed with G-CSF.

options.A: Severe anemia in chronic renal failure

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow biopsy of a 20 year old patient with severe anemia and bruising shows a marked increase in fat cells and a near-complete absence of hematopoietic cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a hypocellular bone marrow where hematopoietic elements are replaced by fat. Option A would show a marrow hypercellular with blasts. Option C would show extensive collagen deposition (fibrosis). Option D would show a hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic changes. Option E would show a cellular marrow, possibly with reduced iron stores.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis presents with normocytic anemia. Lab results show low serum iron, low Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC), and high serum ferritin. Which of the following explains these findings?
questions.explanation: Anemia of chronic disease (ACD) is caused by inflammation-induced hepcidin, which traps iron in macrophages (high ferritin) and reduces iron availability (low serum iron and low TIBC). Option A would show low ferritin and high TIBC. Option C would be macrocytic. Option D shows high serum iron and ring sideroblasts. Option E shows extremely high ferritin but also high serum iron and low TIBC, usually without anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 65 year old man has macrocytic anemia. Which of the following laboratory findings would most specifically distinguish Vitamin B12 deficiency from Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Methylmalonic acid (MMA) is elevated only in Vitamin B12 deficiency, as B12 is a cofactor for converting methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA. Option A (Homocysteine) is elevated in both B12 and Folate deficiencies. Option C is seen post-splenectomy. Option D and E are common to both types of megaloblastic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a WBC count of 60,000/uL. To differentiate a leukemoid reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following findings would strongly favor a leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive states (leukemoid reaction) but low or absent in CML. Option A (Philadelphia chromosome) is diagnostic of CML. Option C (Splenomegaly) and Option E (Basophilia) are classic features of CML. Option D (Myeloblasts) can be seen in both but are more concerning for leukemia.

options.D: Presence of myeloblasts in peripheral blood

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient has a suspected qualitative platelet disorder. Lab tests show normal platelet count but failure of platelets to aggregate in the presence of Ristocetin. This aggregation defect is not corrected by adding normal plasma. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bernard-Soulier Syndrome is a deficiency of GpIb (the ristocetin cofactor receptor). Because the receptor itself is missing, adding plasma (containing vWF) does not correct the defect. Option A (vWD) would show correction with normal plasma. Option B involves a defect in GpIIb/IIIa, causing failure to aggregate with ADP/Collagen but normal ristocetin response. Option D and E have different aggregation patterns and usually respond to ristocetin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient receiving high-dose Recombinant Erythropoietin (EPO) therapy for anemia of chronic kidney disease develops a sudden worsening of systemic blood pressure and headache. What is the most likely underlying mechanism of this adverse effect?
questions.explanation: A rapid increase in the red cell mass (hematocrit) increases blood viscosity and total peripheral resistance, leading to hypertension. Option A is not a primary recognized mechanism. Option C (Iron depletion) is a side effect but causes anemia, not hypertension. Option D would cause skin rashes or anaphylaxis. Option E is not the primary mechanism associated with EPO-induced hypertension.

options.B: Rapid increase in blood viscosity and peripheral resistance
options.D: Allergic reaction to the recombinant protein

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of less than 80 femtoliters (fL) is the primary diagnostic criterion for classifying an anemia as:
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV below 80 fL, commonly seen in iron deficiency. Point A: Normocytic anemia has an MCV of 80 to 100 fL. Point B: Macrocytic anemia is defined by an MCV greater than 100 fL. Point C: Megaloblastic anemia is a subtype of macrocytic anemia due to DNA synthesis defects. Point E: Hemolytic anemia is usually normocytic unless there is a high reticulocyte count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the most sensitive laboratory indicator of early iron deficiency before anemia actually develops?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels reflect total body iron stores and are the first to drop in deficiency. Point A: Hemoglobin drops only after stores are depleted (late stage). Point C: TIBC increases as iron stores fall but is less specific than ferritin. Point D: MCH measures the weight of hemoglobin in a single RBC and changes late. Point E: Hematocrit measures the volume percentage of RBCs and decreases late.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a peripheral blood smear, the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (five or more nuclear lobes) is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic feature of megaloblastic anemia caused by B12 or folate deficiency. Point A: Iron deficiency typically shows hypochromic microcytic cells. Point B: Aplastic anemia shows pancytopenia with normal cell morphology. Point D: Sideroblastic anemia is characterized by ring sideroblasts in the bone marrow. Point E: Thalassemia shows target cells and microcytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old male presents with a high-grade fever and a cough. His CBC shows a WBC count of 18,000/mm3 with 85 percent neutrophils. This reactive leukocytosis is most likely caused by:
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is the classic hallmark of acute bacterial infections. Point A: Viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. Point C: Parasitic infestations usually result in eosinophilia. Point D: Allergic reactions are associated with eosinophilia. Point E: Infectious mononucleosis (EBV) causes a dramatic increase in atypical lymphocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: According to the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood type O negative is considered the universal donor because their red blood cells lack:
questions.explanation: Type O negative cells have no A, B, or Rh (D) antigens, so they cannot be attacked by the recipient's antibodies. Point A: Lacking only A and B would still cause a reaction in an Rh-negative recipient. Point B: Lacking only Rh would still cause a reaction in Type A, B, or O recipients if A/B antigens were present. Point D: Type O individuals have anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma, but it is the lack of antigens on the RBCs that makes them donors. Point E: HLA antigens are primarily on WBCs and tissues, not the basis for ABO grouping.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The Prothrombin Time (PT) is a laboratory test used to evaluate the integrity of the:
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic and common pathways, specifically Factors VII, X, V, II, and I. Point A: Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT) evaluates the intrinsic pathway. Point C: Bleeding time or PFA-100 assesses platelet function. Point D: D-dimer and FDP tests evaluate fibrinolysis. Point E: Vitamin C is related to collagen synthesis, not the PT test.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hemophilia A is a common X-linked recessive bleeding disorder caused by the deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A is specifically due to a deficiency of Factor VIII. Point A: Factor IX deficiency is known as Hemophilia B (Christmas disease). Point B: Factor XI deficiency is Hemophilia C. Point D: Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder but is not Hemophilia. Point E: Factor VII deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive disorder.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the body, iron is primarily stored within macrophages and hepatocytes in the form of:
questions.explanation: Ferritin is the primary soluble storage form, while hemosiderin is the insoluble form. Point A: Transferrin is the transport protein for iron in the plasma. Point B: Ferroportin is the channel that allows iron to exit cells. Point D: Hemoglobin is the functional form of iron in RBCs. Point E: Hepcidin is a regulatory hormone that inhibits iron release.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old strictly vegetarian male presents with pallor and tingling sensations in his feet. Lab tests show macrocytic anemia. Which finding helps distinguish Vitamin B12 deficiency from Folate deficiency?
questions.explanation: Only Vitamin B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neuropathy). Point A: Both cause megaloblastic changes in the marrow. Point B: Both result in an MCV greater than 100 fL. Point D: Both show hypersegmented neutrophils. Point E: Both cause significant anemia with low hemoglobin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 7-year-old boy presents with spontaneous bruising and prolonged bleeding from minor cuts. His platelet count is normal, but his Bleeding Time is prolonged and aPTT is mildly elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: vWD is unique because it affects both platelet adhesion (prolonging Bleeding Time) and stabilizes Factor VIII (prolonging aPTT). Point A: Hemophilia A has a normal Bleeding Time. Point B: Hemophilia B also has a normal Bleeding Time. Point D: ITP would show a significantly low platelet count. Point E: Vitamin K deficiency primarily prolongs the PT first.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia, which of the following combinations of findings is typically observed?
questions.explanation: In iron deficiency, the body lacks iron (low serum iron/ferritin) and increases the capacity to bind it (high TIBC). Point A: This pattern (low iron, low TIBC, high ferritin) is typical of Anemia of Chronic Disease. Point C: This is typical of Hemochromatosis/Iron overload. Point D: This is not a standard clinical pattern. Point E: Low TIBC is not seen in pure iron deficiency.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 19-year-old student presents with sore throat, lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. Peripheral smear reveals 60 percent lymphocytes, many of which are large with abundant basophilic cytoplasm indenting the RBCs. These cells are:
questions.explanation: These are "atypical lymphocytes" seen in Infectious Mononucleosis, which are actually reactive T-cells. Point A: Lymphoblasts would have finer chromatin and nucleoli, suggesting leukemia. Point C: Normal lymphocytes are smaller with scanty cytoplasm. Point D: Monocytes have different nuclear shapes and lack the indentation feature. Point E: Plasma cells have an eccentric nucleus and a perinuclear hoff.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: The fundamental mechanism of an Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction due to ABO incompatibility is:
questions.explanation: Preformed IgM antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to donor RBCs, activating the complement system for intravascular hemolysis. Point A: This describes cell-mediated reactions like graft-versus-host disease. Point B: This describes an anaphylactic transfusion reaction. Point D: This is not the mechanism for acute ABO hemolysis. Point E: ABO hemolysis is strictly immune-mediated.

options.C: Preformed IgM antibodies causing complement-mediated lysis
options.D: Cytotoxic T-cell destruction of donor cells
options.E: Non-immune osmotic lysis of donor cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male has a WBC count of 120,000/mm3. The peripheral smear shows a full range of granulocytes, including myelocytes and metamyelocytes, with 2 percent blasts. The Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score is 0. This is most diagnostic of:
questions.explanation: CML and leukemoid reactions both show high WBCs, but CML has a characteristically low/absent LAP score. Point A: A leukemoid reaction (due to infection) shows a high LAP score. Point C: AML would require greater than 20 percent blasts in the marrow/blood. Point D: Polycythemia Vera presents primarily with high RBC mass. Point E: Essential Thrombocythemia presents primarily with extremely high platelet counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A bone marrow biopsy in a patient with suspected Aplastic Anemia is expected to show:
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia involves global suppression of hematopoiesis, leading to "dry" or fatty marrow. Point A: This is typical of B12 or folate deficiency. Point B: This describes Myelofibrosis. Point D: This describes Myelophthisic anemia. Point E: This is a feature of sideroblastic anemias.

options.B: Extensive replacement of marrow by collagen (fibrosis)
options.C: Markedly hypocellular marrow replaced by fat cells
options.D: Replacement of marrow by metastatic epithelial cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with generalized oozing from IV sites. Labs show: Prolonged PT, Prolonged aPTT, Decreased Fibrinogen, and a Platelet count of 30,000/mm3. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DIC is a consumption coagulopathy where all factors and platelets are used up, leading to low fibrinogen and low platelets. Point A: Hemophilia has normal PT, normal platelets, and normal fibrinogen. Point B: vWD has normal platelets and normal PT. Point D: Warfarin prolongs PT but does not typically lower platelets or fibrinogen. Point E: While liver failure lowers factors, DIC is the best fit for acute consumption including platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the pathogenesis of Anemia of Chronic Disease, the molecule Hepcidin contributes to anemia by:
questions.explanation: Hepcidin prevents the export of iron from cells by inducing the degradation of ferroportin. Point A: Hepcidin actually blunts the EPO response. Point B: Hepcidin decreases iron absorption. Point D: This is the mechanism of Thalassemia. Point E: This describes hemolytic anemias.

options.B: Increasing the absorption of iron from the duodenum
options.C: Degrading ferroportin, thereby sequestering iron in macrophages
options.D: Directly inhibiting the synthesis of globin chains
options.E: Shortening the lifespan of circulating erythrocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a normal platelet count of 250,000/mm3 has a significantly prolonged Bleeding Time. Which of the following conditions most likely explains this discrepancy?
questions.explanation: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase, causing a qualitative platelet defect (normal count, abnormal function). Point A: ITP causes quantitative deficiency (low count). Point C: Hemophilia affects secondary hemostasis (normal bleeding time). Point D: Vitamin K deficiency affects coagulation factors (normal bleeding time). Point E: Factor XII deficiency prolongs aPTT but does not cause a clinical bleeding tendency or affect bleeding time.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her laboratory reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following conditions is the most likely cause of this finding?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is a classic cause of microcytic anemia, defined by an MCV less than 80 fL. Option A and B cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). Option D and E typically present as normocytic anemia, although spherocytosis can sometimes show a slightly decreased MCV with increased MCHC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male presents with high-grade fever and a productive cough. His peripheral blood film shows an absolute neutrophil count of 15,000/microliter with the presence of toxic granulations. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Reactive neutrophilia with toxic granulations is a hallmark of acute bacterial infections. Option A usually causes lymphocytosis. Option B and D are associated with eosinophilia. Option E is a malignant process that would show a shifted left differential including myelocytes and blasts, rather than just reactive changes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, which blood group is considered the universal donor for packed red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Group O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens on the red cell surface, meaning it will not be lysed by the recipient's antibodies in an emergency. Option C is the universal recipient. Options A, B, and E possess antigens that could trigger reactions in incompatible recipients.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a hemoglobin of 8 g/dL and an MCV of 115 fL. The peripheral smear shows hypersegmented neutrophils. Which investigation is most important to differentiate the possible causes?
questions.explanation: Macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL) with hypersegmented neutrophils is diagnostic of megaloblastic anemia, caused by B12 or Folate deficiency. Option A is for microcytic anemia. Option B is for thalassemias. Options D and E are primarily for hemolytic anemias.

options.C: Serum Vitamin B12 and Folate levels

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the primary clinical manifestation of a qualitative or quantitative platelet disorder?
questions.explanation: Platelet disorders (primary hemostasis defects) typically present with superficial bleeds like petechiae, purpura, and epistaxis. Options A, B, D, and E are characteristic of coagulation factor deficiencies (secondary hemostasis defects).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Prothrombin Time (PT) is a laboratory screening test used primarily to evaluate which pathway of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. Option A is measured by Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT). Option D is measured by bleeding time or platelet aggregometry. Option E is evaluated by D-dimer or FDPs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a healthy adult, what is the normal range for the Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV)?
questions.explanation: The standard reference range for MCV is 80-100 fL. Values below 80 define microcytosis (Option A and B), and values above 100 define macrocytosis (Option D and E).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A child is found to have worms in his stool. Which leukocyte count is most likely to be elevated on his complete blood count?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a classic response to parasitic infestations and Type I hypersensitivity reactions. Option A responds to bacteria. Option B responds to viruses. Option C is seen in chronic inflammation. Option E is rare but seen in myeloproliferative disorders.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hemophilia A is characterized by the deficiency of which clotting factor?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder caused by Factor VIII deficiency. Option C refers to Hemophilia B (Christmas disease). Option D refers to Hemophilia C. Option A and E are other rare factor deficiencies but not called Hemophilia A.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the laboratory diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia, which of the following patterns of Iron studies is typically observed?
questions.explanation: In iron deficiency, serum iron is low, and the body increases Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) to compensate. Ferritin (storage iron) is the first to drop. Option A describes Anemia of Chronic Disease. Option C describes Hemochromatosis or iron overload.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with chronic alcoholism presents with anemia. Laboratory tests show an MCV of 110 fL. Peripheral smear reveals macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemias (caused by B12 or folate deficiency, common in alcoholics) result from impaired DNA synthesis, leading to delayed nuclear maturation while cytoplasm grows. Option A causes microcytic anemia. Option B causes hemolytic anemia. Option D causes aplastic anemia. Option E leads to iron deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To distinguish between a Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following tests is most useful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive processes (Leukemoid reaction) and low in CML. Option A can be high in both. Option B might show few blasts in both, though more in CML. Options D and E are non-specific.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient develops sudden chills, lower back pain, and hypotension 15 minutes after starting a blood transfusion. What is the most likely mechanism of this reaction?
questions.explanation: This is an Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction (AHTR), usually due to ABO incompatibility where IgM antibodies activate complement. Option A describes an allergic/anaphylactic reaction. Option C describes febrile non-hemolytic reaction. Option D is sepsis. Option E describes a delayed reaction occurring days later.

options.B: Pre-formed IgM antibodies against donor RBC antigens
options.C: Cytokine release from donor white cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following peripheral blood smear findings is most characteristic of Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA)?
questions.explanation: Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) are the hallmark of MAHA (e.g., DIC, TTP, HUS). Option A is seen in hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis. Option C is seen in Thalassemia or liver disease. Option D is specific for Sickle Cell Anemia. Option E is seen in G6PD deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old patient with Chronic Kidney Disease is being treated with recombinant Erythropoietin for anemia. Which of the following is a recognized adverse effect of this therapy?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin therapy is strongly associated with the development or worsening of hypertension and increased risk of thromboembolic events. Option A is a side effect of G-CSF (Filgrastim). Option B is not a direct result of EPO. Option D and E are associated with other drugs like bleomycin or phenytoin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the investigation of Megaloblastic Anemia, a bone marrow biopsy typically reveals which of the following features?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia shows a hypercellular marrow because the marrow is trying to compensate for ineffective erythropoiesis. The precursors show nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony. Option A is aplastic anemia. Option C is sideroblastic anemia. Option D is myelofibrosis.

options.E: Replacement of marrow by metastatic cells

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12-year-old boy presents with a history of recurrent epistaxis and prolonged bleeding from minor cuts. His laboratory results show: Normal Platelet count, Prolonged Bleeding Time, Prolonged aPTT, and Normal PT. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Von Willebrand Disease (vWD) causes prolonged bleeding time (defective platelet adhesion) and prolonged aPTT (vWF stabilizes Factor VIII). Options A and B have normal bleeding times. Option D would prolong PT. Option E has a normal aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Regarding Reactive Leukocytosis, which of the following cytokines is specifically responsible for the eosinophilia seen in allergic disorders and parasitic infections?
questions.explanation: IL-5 is the key cytokine for eosinophil differentiation, activation, and survival. Option A and B are general pro-inflammatory cytokines. Option D activates macrophages. Option E is a major neutrophil chemoattractant.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with macrocytic anemia and neurological symptoms including loss of vibration and position sense. Which of the following antibodies, if present, would specifically point towards Pernicious Anemia as the cause?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune destruction of parietal cells or neutralization of Intrinsic Factor (IF). Antibodies against IF are highly specific for this diagnosis. Option A and D are for SLE. Option C is for ITP. Option E is for Celiac disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old student undergoes a routine complete blood count. The lab report shows a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following terms best describes this finding?
questions.explanation: The normal range for MCV is typically 80 to 100 fL. A value below 80 fL is defined as microcytosis, often seen in iron deficiency anemia. Debunking distractors: A is incorrect as macrocytosis refers to MCV greater than 100 fL. C is incorrect as normocytosis is the normal range (80-100 fL). D refers to variation in cell size, not the absolute size. E refers to variation in cell shape.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Hematopoiesis is regulated by specific growth factors. Which cytokine is primarily responsible for stimulating the production and differentiation of red blood cell precursors in the bone marrow?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO), primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney, is the main stimulator of erythropoiesis. Debunking distractors: A stimulates platelet production. B is specific for eosinophil production. D stimulates neutrophil production. E is a multi-lineage growth factor but not the primary specific regulator for RBCs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old pregnant female presents with fatigue. Her peripheral blood smear shows small, pale red blood cells with an increased area of central pallor. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is characterized by a microcytic hypochromic blood picture due to impaired hemoglobin synthesis. Debunking distractors: A and B cause macrocytic anemia (large cells). D causes normocytic normochromic anemia with pancytopenia. E shows spherocytes which lack central pallor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of white blood cell disorders, an increase in the absolute neutrophil count above the normal range is termed as:
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is the term for an increased absolute neutrophil count, typically seen in acute bacterial infections. Debunking distractors: A is a general decrease in total WBCs. B is a decrease in neutrophils. C is an increase in lymphocytes. E is an increase in eosinophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient requires an emergency blood transfusion, and there is no time for cross-matching. Which blood group is considered the universal donor for packed red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Group O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens on the red cell surface, making it safe for transfusion into most recipients in emergencies. Debunking distractors: A is the universal recipient. C contains Rh antigen which can cause sensitization in Rh-negative patients. B and E contain antigens that would react with anti-A or anti-B antibodies in specific recipients.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old boy presents with a sore throat and fever. Lab tests show a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with 80% neutrophils. This condition is best described as:
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections (like a sore throat) typically trigger a reactive neutrophilia, an increase in neutrophils to combat the pathogen. Debunking distractors: A is common in viral infections. B is a decrease in WBC count. D is a malignant proliferation, usually involving much higher counts and immature forms. E is an increase in monocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which growth factor is clinically used to reduce the duration of neutropenia in patients receiving cancer chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: Granulocyte Colony Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) stimulates the production of neutrophils and is used to treat chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. Debunking distractors: A is for anemia. C and D are used to stimulate platelets. E is a plasma protein, not a hematopoietic growth factor.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A medical student is examining a blood film and identifies a large neutrophil with a six-lobed nucleus. This finding is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (5 or more lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemias caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. Debunking distractors: A shows microcytic hypochromic cells. B shows normocytic cells. D usually shows a left shift (immature forms) and toxic granulation. E shows basophilic stippling.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory tests is the most appropriate first-line investigation for assessing the extrinsic pathway of coagulation?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic and common pathways of coagulation. Debunking distractors: A assesses platelet function/vessel wall. B assesses the intrinsic pathway. D assesses the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. E measures the quantity of platelets.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Blood grouping is based on the presence of specific antigens on the surface of erythrocytes. A person with blood group B will have which antibodies in their plasma?
questions.explanation: According to Landsteiner's Law, individuals possess antibodies in their plasma against the ABO antigens they lack on their red cells. Debunking distractors: B would cause hemolysis of the person's own cells. C is found in group O. D is found in group AB. E is only found in Rh-negative individuals after sensitization.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55-year-old chronic alcoholic presents with numbness in his feet and fatigue. Lab results show MCV 115 fL and low hemoglobin. Peripheral smear reveals macro-ovalocytes. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 and Folate are essential for DNA synthesis. Deficiency leads to megaloblastic anemia where nuclear maturation lags behind cytoplasmic maturation. Debunking distractors: A describes iron deficiency or sideroblastic anemia. C refers to hemolytic anemias. D refers to aplastic anemia. E leads to microcytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In distinguishing between Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA) and Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD), which of the following lab findings is characteristic of IDA but NOT ACD?
questions.explanation: In IDA, TIBC is high as the body tries to compensate for low iron. In ACD, TIBC is usually low or normal because iron is sequestered in stores. Debunking distractors: A, B, and D can be seen in both. E is a non-specific symptom of any anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old male with a history of massive trauma receives multiple units of blood. Shortly after, he develops fever, chills, and hemoglobinocyturia. This acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions occur when preformed antibodies (usually IgM in ABO incompatibility) bind to donor RBCs and activate the complement system, leading to intravascular hemolysis. Debunking distractors: A refers to allergic reactions. C refers to graft-versus-host disease. D refers to extravascular hemolysis (often delayed). E refers to septic transfusion reactions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a very high WBC count of 60,000/mm3. To differentiate a leukemoid reaction from Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), the Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score is performed. Which result favors a leukemoid reaction?
questions.explanation: LAP score is high in reactive states (leukemoid reaction) because the mature neutrophils are functionally active. It is low in CML. Debunking distractors: A favors CML. C is the genetic hallmark of CML. D and E can occur in both, though blasts are rare in simple leukemoid reactions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During the investigation of a bleeding disorder, a patient's Prothrombin Time (PT) is found to be prolonged, but the Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT) is normal. This suggests a deficiency in which factor?
questions.explanation: Factor VII is the only factor unique to the extrinsic pathway. A deficiency results in prolonged PT and normal APTT. Debunking distractors: A, B, and D are part of the intrinsic pathway (prolonged APTT). E is part of the common pathway (both PT and APTT prolonged).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old patient with rheumatoid arthritis has a normocytic anemia. Lab tests show low serum iron, low TIBC, and high serum ferritin. What is the role of Hepcidin in this condition?
questions.explanation: Anemia of Chronic Disease is mediated by Hepcidin (induced by IL-6), which blocks ferroportin, preventing iron release from macrophages and decreasing gut absorption. Debunking distractors: A is the opposite of hepcidin's effect. B is not a function of hepcidin (ACD actually has blunted EPO response). D is incorrect as transferrin decreases. E is not related to hepcidin.

options.A: It increases iron absorption from the duodenum
options.B: It stimulates erythropoietin release from kidneys
options.C: It inhibits iron release from macrophages
options.D: It increases transferrin synthesis in the liver
options.E: It promotes DNA synthesis in erythroid blasts

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with petechiae and mucosal bleeding. Platelet count is 20,000/mm3. PT and APTT are normal. Bone marrow shows increased megakaryocytes. What is the most likely pathogenesis?
questions.explanation: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) involves IgG antibodies against platelet glycoproteins (like GpIIb/IIIa), leading to splenic destruction. The marrow shows increased megakaryocytes as a compensatory response. Debunking distractors: A occurs in hypersplenism. C describes aplastic anemia (marrow would be hypocellular). D describes DIC or TTP (PT/APTT would be abnormal in DIC). E is Bernard-Soulier syndrome.

options.A: Sequestration of platelets in the spleen
options.C: Suppression of stem cells by chemicals

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A lab report shows the following: Hemoglobin 7 g/dL, RBC count 2 million/mm3, Hematocrit 21%. Calculate the Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) and classify the anemia.
questions.explanation: MCV = (Hct x 10) / RBC count (in millions). MCV = (21 x 10) / 2 = 105 fL. Since 105 > 100, it is macrocytic. Debunking distractors: A, C, D, and E are mathematically incorrect based on the formula.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient diagnosed with megaloblastic anemia is treated with folate. A week later, the blood film shows many small, dark-blue staining granules within the red cells (basophilic stippling). What does this indicate?
questions.explanation: While basophilic stippling is classic for lead poisoning, fine stippling is also seen in brisk reticulocytosis following treatment of nutritional anemias, representing residual RNA. Debunking distractors: A is possible but less likely in a post-treatment context. C is incorrect as reticulocytes show a positive response. D and E do not typically present this way immediately after folate therapy.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 28-year-old woman in her second pregnancy is Rh-negative, and her first child was Rh-positive. She did not receive Rhogam after her first delivery. Which of the following describes the mechanism of the potential hemolytic disease of the newborn in her current Rh-positive fetus?
questions.explanation: Sensitization in the first pregnancy leads to maternal anti-D IgG production. These small IgG molecules cross the placenta and coat fetal Rh-positive RBCs, leading to extravascular hemolysis. Debunking distractors: A refers to IgM, which is too large to cross the placenta. C is not the mechanism for HDN. D is not relevant to intrauterine hemolysis. E is incorrect as the hemolysis is primarily extravascular (splenic).

options.C: Direct lysis of fetal RBCs by maternal T-cells
options.E: Complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis in the fetus

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old chronic kidney disease patient presents with severe pallor. Lab results show a normocytic normochromic anemia. Which hematopoietic growth factor deficiency is most likely responsible for this condition?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin is primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. In chronic kidney disease, its production fails, leading to anemia. Distractor A is incorrect as Thrombopoietin regulates platelet production and is mainly produced in the liver. Distractor C (G-CSF) stimulates neutrophil production and is used in neutropenia. Distractor D (IL-3) is a general stimulator of multiple lineages but not the primary cause of renal anemia. Distractor E (SCF) acts on very early undifferentiated stem cells rather than specific erythroid maturation.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following indices is used to define microcytic anemia in a patient?
questions.explanation: Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) measures the average size of red blood cells; a value below 80 fL defines microcytosis. Distractor A (MCH) reflects the average weight of hemoglobin per cell, used to define hypochromia. Distractor B (MCHC) is the concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. Distractor D (RDW) measures the variation in RBC size (anisocytosis). Distractor E (Reticulocyte count) measures bone marrow erythropoietic activity, not cell size.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 22-year-old pregnant woman presents with fatigue. Her blood report shows Hemoglobin 9 g/dL, MCV 72 fL, and increased Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC). Which of the following is the most sensitive lab investigation to confirm iron deficiency?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific marker for iron deficiency as it directly reflects total body iron stores. Distractor A (Serum iron) fluctuates daily and can be low in chronic inflammation. Distractor C (Percent saturation) is helpful but less sensitive than ferritin. Distractor D (Electrophoresis) is used for thalassemias or sickle cell disease, not iron deficiency. Distractor E (Bone marrow) is the gold standard for stainable iron but is invasive and rarely used as a first-line confirmative test for simple IDA.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with a history of gastrectomy presents with paresthesia and megaloblastic anemia. Which finding on the peripheral blood smear is most characteristic of this condition?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia (B12 or Folate deficiency) is characterized by hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). Distractor A (Target cells) are seen in thalassemias and liver disease. Distractor B (Schistocytes) indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Distractor D (Rouleaux) is typical of Multiple Myeloma or high protein states. Distractor E (Bite cells) are found in G6PD deficiency due to splenic removal of Heinz bodies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An absolute increase in the number of circulating neutrophils above the normal range is termed as:
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is the correct term for an increase in the absolute neutrophil count. Distractor A (Leukemoid reaction) is an extreme elevation of WBCs mimicking leukemia, usually due to infection. Distractor C (Agranulocytosis) is a severe reduction in granulocytes. Distractor D (Left shift) refers to the presence of immature neutrophil precursors in the blood. Distractor E (Neutropenia) is a decrease in neutrophil count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 3-year-old child presents with petechiae and mucosal bleeding. Lab tests show a prolonged Bleeding Time (BT) but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT). The platelet count is severely reduced at 15,000/mm3. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
questions.explanation: Petechiae and mucosal bleeding with a low platelet count and prolonged BT indicate thrombocytopenia, which is a quantitative platelet disorder. Distractor A is incorrect because secondary hemostasis defects (clotting factors) usually cause deep tissue hematomas, not petechiae. Distractor B and C are incorrect because PT and aPTT would be prolonged in those cases. Distractor E is incorrect because qualitative dysfunction refers to poor platelet function despite a normal or near-normal count (e.g., Glanzmann thrombasthenia).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is specifically used to monitor the extrinsic pathway of coagulation?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin Time (PT) assesses the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. Distractor A (aPTT) monitors the intrinsic and common pathways. Distractor B (BT) assesses platelet function and vascular integrity. Distractor D (Thrombin Time) specifically evaluates the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. Distractor E is an older, non-specific test for platelet function.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops fever, chills, and hypotension. Red-colored urine is noted. This reaction is most likely mediated by:
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is usually due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies in the recipient's plasma cause intravascular hemolysis. Distractor A describes an allergic/anaphylactic reaction. Distractor C describes a graft-versus-host or delayed reaction. Distractor D describes a febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction. Distractor E describes delayed hemolytic reactions or Rh incompatibility issues, which are typically extravascular and less acute.

options.B: Preformed IgM antibodies against donor RBCs
options.D: Accumulation of cytokines in stored blood

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood film shows microcytic hypochromic cells with many "pencil cells." The serum iron is low, TIBC is high, and serum ferritin is 5 ng/mL. Which of the following bone marrow findings would be expected?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia (indicated by pencil cells and low ferritin) leads to a depletion of iron stores in the bone marrow, visible as an absence of Prussian blue staining in macrophages. Distractor A is for B12/Folate deficiency. Distractor C defines Acute Leukemia. Distractor D is characteristic of Sideroblastic anemia. Distractor E is characteristic of Myelofibrosis.

options.B: Absence of stainable iron in macrophages

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is found to have the "Bombay Phenotype." Which of the following is true regarding their blood grouping?
questions.explanation: Bombay phenotype individuals lack the H substance (fucose) due to a deficiency in the H gene (genotype hh), meaning they cannot produce A or B antigens. Distractor B is incorrect as H is the precursor for A and B. Distractor C is incorrect because they have Anti-H antibodies and will react against O-type blood (which has H antigen). Distractor D is incorrect as they possess Anti-A, Anti-B, and Anti-H. Distractor E is the reverse of the actual genetics.

options.A: They lack the H antigen on their RBCs
options.B: They have A and B antigens but lack H
options.C: They can safely receive O-negative blood
options.D: Their plasma lacks Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies
options.E: They have the H gene but lack the ABO gene

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old patient presents with a WBC count of 60,000/mm3. The peripheral smear shows a spectrum of granulocytic cells including myelocytes and metamyelocytes. The Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score is very low. This favors a diagnosis of:
questions.explanation: CML and Leukemoid reactions both show high WBC counts with immature forms, but a low LAP score is characteristic of CML, whereas a high LAP score suggests a reactive Leukemoid reaction. Distractor A is incorrect because LAP is high in infection. Distractor C is incorrect as it would show a "blast crisis" or more than 20 percent blasts. Distractor D involves RBC elevation mainly. Distractor E is the opposite of the clinical finding.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with macrocytic anemia has a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 115 fL. To differentiate between Vitamin B12 deficiency and Folate deficiency, which of the following lab findings specifically indicates Vitamin B12 deficiency?
questions.explanation: Both B12 and Folate deficiency cause high homocysteine, but only Vitamin B12 deficiency leads to elevated Methylmalonic Acid because B12 is a co-factor for the conversion of methylmalonyl CoA to succinyl CoA. Distractor A is found in both. Distractor C is a general feature of ineffective erythropoiesis in both. Distractor D suggests splenic dysfunction or megaloblastic change but isn't specific to B12. Distractor E is seen in both.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Reactive leukocytosis with a "left shift" in the neutrophil lineage is most commonly associated with:
questions.explanation: Acute bacterial infections typically cause neutrophilia with an increase in immature forms (bands/metamyelocytes), known as a left shift. Distractor A usually causes lymphocytosis. Distractor C and D cause eosinophilia. Distractor E is a malignant proliferation of lymphocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with hemophilia A is expected to have which of the following coagulation profiles?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A is a deficiency of Factor VIII, which is part of the intrinsic pathway monitored by aPTT. Since the extrinsic pathway (PT) is unaffected, the PT remains normal. Distractor A would suggest Factor VII deficiency. Distractor B suggests a common pathway defect or multiple deficiencies (like Vitamin K deficiency). Distractor D and E suggest platelet or vascular disorders.

options.D: Normal PT, Normal aPTT, Prolonged BT

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old male with a history of recurrent epistaxis and heavy bleeding after dental extraction shows a normal platelet count, prolonged Bleeding Time, and a slightly prolonged aPTT. His PT is normal. Platelet aggregation with Ristocetin is absent. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: vWD is characterized by prolonged BT (platelet adhesion defect) and often prolonged aPTT (vWF stabilizes Factor VIII). Absent aggregation with ristocetin is a classic finding. Distractor A (Hemophilia B) would have a normal BT. Distractor B also has absent ristocetin aggregation but features giant platelets and no aPTT prolongation. Distractor D has normal ristocetin aggregation but abnormal aggregation with ADP/Collagen. Distractor E presents with low platelet counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a case of severe eosinophilia (absolute eosinophil count > 5000/mm3), which of the following conditions is a common non-neoplastic cause?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is most commonly caused by allergic disorders (like asthma, hay fever) and parasitic infections. Distractor A (TB) often shows monocytosis. Distractor C and D relate to RBC disorders. Distractor E (Typhoid) is famous for causing leucopenia and eosinopenia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by which of the following iron study patterns?
questions.explanation: In chronic disease, hepcidin sequesters iron in macrophages. This results in low serum iron and TIBC (as the body tries to downregulate transport) but high ferritin (stored iron). Distractor B describes Iron Deficiency Anemia. Distractor C describes Sideroblastic anemia or Hemochromatosis. Distractor D/E are not standard patterns for ACD.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Normal Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 70-year-old male with weight loss and back pain is found to have normocytic anemia. Rouleaux formation is seen on peripheral smear. What would be the most expected finding on bone marrow examination?
questions.explanation: Rouleaux formation and anemia in an elderly patient with bone pain strongly suggest Multiple Myeloma, characterized by malignant plasma cell infiltration of the bone marrow. Distractor A is for AML. Distractor C is for Aplastic anemia. Distractor D is for CLL or Lymphoma. Distractor E is for Gaucher disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A woman who is Rh-negative is pregnant with her second Rh-positive fetus. She did not receive prophylaxis during her first pregnancy. The fetus is at risk of Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN). This is caused by donor:
questions.explanation: HDN occurs when maternal IgG antibodies (produced after sensitization in the first pregnancy) cross the placenta and attack fetal Rh-positive RBCs. Distractor A is incorrect because IgM cannot cross the placenta. Distractor B is about mucosal immunity. Distractor D is reverse. Distractor E is the effect of the disease (kernicterus), not the cause.

options.C: Maternal IgG antibodies crossing the placenta

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia is defined as microcytic when the Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) falls below which of the following values?
questions.explanation: The normal range for MCV is typically 80 to 100 fL. Values below 80 fL define microcytic anemia. Option A: 100 fL is the upper limit, above which anemia is macrocytic. Option B: 90 fL is within the normal normocytic range. Options D and E: While these are microcytic, 80 fL is the standard diagnostic threshold.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In adult males in developed countries, what is the most common underlying cause of iron deficiency anemia that must be ruled out?
questions.explanation: In adult men and postmenopausal women, iron deficiency is considered caused by occult gastrointestinal bleeding (e.g., malignancy) until proven otherwise. Option A: This is more common in growing children or pregnant women. Option B: This is rare in developed countries with balanced diets. Option D: Conditions like Celiac disease are less common than blood loss. Option E: This is a major cause in developing tropical regions, not globally standardized for adult males in developed areas.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old female presents with fatigue. Her peripheral blood smear shows macrocytic red cells and hypersegmented neutrophils. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) is a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia, caused by Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency. Option A: Iron deficiency causes microcytic hypochromic anemia. Option C: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy and collagen defects. Option D: Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency can cause sideroblastic anemia (microcytic). Option E: Vitamin K deficiency causes bleeding disorders, not anemia morphology changes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is a characteristic cause of reactive eosinophilia?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is typically associated with allergic reactions (asthma, hay fever) and parasitic infestations. Option A: Bacterial infections typically cause neutrophilia. Option B: Viral infections often cause lymphocytosis. Options D and E: These relate to red cell disorders, not reactive changes in white cell counts.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the ABO blood grouping system, an individual with blood group O has which of the following on their red cell membranes?
questions.explanation: Blood group O is characterized by the absence of both A and B antigens on the RBC surface. Option A: This defines group A. Option B: This defines group B. Option C: This defines group AB. Option E: This refers to the Rh system (Rh positive), which is independent of the ABO system.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory test is the most sensitive first-line indicator of depleted total body iron stores?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels correlate with total body iron stores and are the first to decrease in iron deficiency. Option A: Hemoglobin only drops in the late stage of iron deficiency anemia. Option B: Serum iron can fluctuate due to inflammation. Option D: TIBC increases in iron deficiency but is less specific than ferritin. Option E: Hematocrit measures red cell mass, not iron stores directly.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which white blood cell type is the first to respond and increase in number during an acute pyogenic bacterial infection?
questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the primary cells of acute inflammation and provide the first line of defense against bacteria. Option A: Lymphocytes increase in viral infections or chronic inflammation. Option B: Monocytes are associated with chronic inflammation. Option D: Basophils are rare and involved in immediate hypersensitivity. Option E: Eosinophils respond to parasites and allergies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bleeding disorders caused by platelet a-granule deficiency or dysfunction primarily affect which stage of hemostasis?
questions.explanation: Platelets are responsible for primary hemostasis (the formation of a platelet plug). Deficiencies in granules impair this initial response.
