Option A: Secondary hemostasis involves the coagulation cascade. Option C: Stabilization is done by Factor XIII and fibrin. Option D: Fibrinolysis is the breakdown of clots. Option E: Vasoconstriction is a neurogenic/endothelin-mediated vascular response.

options.B: Formation of the temporary platelet plug

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the term used to describe the variation in the size of red blood cells seen on a peripheral smear?
questions.explanation: Anisocytosis refers to variation in RBC size (often reflected by an increased RDW). Option A: Poikilocytosis refers to variation in shape. Option C: Polychromasia refers to the bluish tint of immature RBCs (reticulocytes). Option D: Hypochromia refers to increased central pallor (low hemoglobin). Option E: Microcytosis refers to small size specifically.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old man with chronic renal failure has a hemoglobin of 8 g/dL. What is the most significant factor contributing to his anemia?
questions.explanation: Chronic kidney disease leads to a loss of the peritubular cells that produce EPO, resulting in normocytic normochromic anemia. Option A: While possible, it is not the primary mechanism. Option B: Malabsorption is not a feature of renal failure. Option D: Uremia can shorten RBC life, but EPO deficiency is the dominant factor. Option E: B12 deficiency is not directly caused by renal failure.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with iron deficiency anemia, which of the following sets of laboratory results is expected?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is characterized by low iron, low ferritin (stores), and high TIBC (the body produces more transferrin to capture any available iron). Option A: This profile fits Anemia of Chronic Disease. Option C: This fits Hemochromatosis. Option D: Ferritin is usually the first to drop; iron would eventually follow. Option E: Low TIBC usually points toward inflammation or protein loss.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, Low Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A peripheral blood film shows the presence of Dohle bodies, toxic granulations, and cytoplasmic vacuoles in neutrophils. These findings are most suggestive of:
questions.explanation: These are reactive changes in neutrophils occurring during severe sepsis or inflammation (leukemoid reaction features). Option A: CML shows a full spectrum of myeloid cells but not usually toxic changes. Option B: AML is characterized by blasts. Option D: This shows hypersegmented neutrophils. Option E: This is a genetic condition showing bilobed neutrophils without toxic granules.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions due to ABO incompatibility are primarily mediated by which mechanism?
questions.explanation: ABO incompatibility involves pre-formed IgM antibodies binding to donor RBCs, activating the complement system and causing intravascular hemolysis (Type II Hypersensitivity). Option A: This causes urticaria or anaphylaxis. Option C: This involves tissue deposition of complexes (e.g., SLE). Option D: This is delayed (e.g., TB skin test). Option E: This is an attack by donor T-cells on the recipient.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT) but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and normal Platelet Count. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: APTT measures the intrinsic and common pathways. Factor VIII (Hemophilia A) is part of the intrinsic pathway. Option A: Factor VII is part of the extrinsic pathway and would prolong PT. Option C: Vitamin K deficiency affects II, VII, IX, and X, prolonging both PT and APTT. Option D: This is a platelet component. Option E: Prothrombin (Factor II) is in the common pathway and would prolong both tests.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow examination in a patient with a Mean Corpuscular Volume of 115 fL reveals hypercellularity with megaloblastic erythroid precursors showing nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony. What is the molecular basis for these findings?
questions.explanation: B12 and Folate are cofactors for DNA synthesis. Deficiency leads to delayed nuclear maturation while the cytoplasm grows normally (asynchrony). Option A: This occurs in Sideroblastic anemia or IDA. Option B: This occurs in Thalassemia. Option D: This describes ring sideroblasts. Option E: This occurs in Hereditary Spherocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To differentiate between a Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following findings strongly favors a Leukemoid Reaction?
questions.explanation: LAP is an enzyme found in mature, functional neutrophils. The score is high in reactive states (leukemoid) and low in malignant cells (CML). Option A: Seen in both. Option B: Common in CML, rare in reactive states. Option D: Diagnostic for CML. Option E: Characteristically seen in CML, not in reactive infections.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 4-year-old boy presents with spontaneous bleeding into the knee joints (hemarthrosis). Lab tests show: PT: Normal, APTT: Prolonged, Bleeding Time: Normal, Platelet Count: Normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemarthrosis is classic for coagulation factor deficiencies (secondary hemostasis). Normal BT/Platelets rule out platelet issues. Prolonged APTT with normal PT points to the intrinsic pathway (Factor VIII or IX). Option A: VWD usually presents with mucosal bleeding and often prolonged BT. Option B: ITP would show low platelets. Option D: This would prolong PT as well. Option E: DIC would show low platelets and prolongation of all tests.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the "Anemia of Chronic Disease" in terms of iron metabolism?
questions.explanation: Inflammation increases IL-6, which stimulates Hepcidin production. Hepcidin traps iron inside macrophages and prevents its release, making it unavailable for erythropoiesis despite high stores. Option A: Absorption is reduced, but the primary pathology is sequestration. Option C: This occurs in paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria. Option D: While bacteria use iron, it doesn't cause this specific anemia profile. Option E: This is not the mechanism of ACD.

options.A: Inability to absorb iron from the duodenum
options.B: Sequestration of iron in macrophages due to high Hepcidin
options.C: Loss of iron through the urinary tract
options.D: Massive utilization of iron by infecting bacteria

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a cross-match, the recipient's serum is mixed with the donor's red cells. If agglutination occurs in this "major" cross-match, what does it indicate?
questions.explanation: The major cross-match detects if the recipient's plasma/serum contains pre-formed antibodies that will attack the donor's cells. Option B: This is the "minor" cross-match, which is less clinically significant today as packed cells are used. Option C: Rh status is determined by typing, not cross-matching. Option D: Complement is present in all fresh serum but doesn't cause agglutination without antibodies. Option E: If they were identical, no agglutination would occur.

options.A: The recipient has antibodies against the donor's RBC antigens
options.B: The donor has antibodies against the recipient's RBC antigens
options.C: The recipient's RBCs lack the D antigen
options.D: The donor's plasma contains high levels of complement
options.E: The donor and recipient have the same blood group

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25 year old female presents with fatigue. Her lab reports show a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with this finding?
questions.explanation: Microcytic anemia is defined by an MCV less than 80 fL, with iron deficiency being the most common cause. Vitamin B12 and Folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). Aplastic anemia and acute blood loss typically present as normocytic anemia (MCV 80-100 fL).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Erythropoietin is a primary hematopoietic growth factor required for the proliferation of erythroid progenitors. Which organ is the primary site of its synthesis in an adult?
questions.explanation: In adults, approximately 90 percent of erythropoietin is secreted by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. The liver produces a small amount (10 percent). The bone marrow is the site of action, not production. The spleen and thymus do not produce erythropoietin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following cells is characteristically seen in the peripheral blood smear of a patient with Megaloblastic Anemia?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils (containing five or more nuclear lobes) are a hallmark of megaloblastic anemia alongside macro-ovalocytes. Target cells are seen in thalassemias and liver disease. Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Spherocytes are found in hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis. Sickle cells are specific to Sickle Cell Disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45 year old male presents with high-grade fever and cough. His total leukocyte count is 18,000/mm3 with 85 percent neutrophils. This reactive leukocytosis is most likely caused by which type of infection?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia (increased neutrophils) is the classic response to acute bacterial infections. Viral infections typically cause lymphocytosis. Parasitic infections and allergies cause eosinophilia. Fungal infections can cause various patterns but are less commonly associated with isolated acute neutrophilia than pyogenic bacteria.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of anemias based on underlying mechanisms, which of the following is classified as an anemia of diminished erythropoiesis?
questions.explanation: Thalassemia is an anemia of diminished erythropoiesis due to impaired globin synthesis. Options A, B, D, and E are all forms of hemolytic anemia, where the primary mechanism is increased destruction of red blood cells rather than decreased production.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a suspected bleeding disorder has a prolonged Bleeding Time but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT). This pattern most likely indicates a defect in:
questions.explanation: Bleeding time assesses platelet plug formation. Prolonged bleeding time with normal PT/aPTT points to platelet defects (quantitative or qualitative). Factor VIII and IX deficiencies (Hemophilia A and B) prolong aPTT. Factor VII deficiency prolongs PT. Prothrombin deficiency prolongs both PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: According to the International Society of Blood Transfusion, individuals with Blood Group O possess which of the following on their red cell membranes?
questions.explanation: Blood group O is characterized by the absence of both A and B antigens on the red cell surface. They have both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their serum. D antigen refers to the Rh system, which is independent of the ABO grouping.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) is clinically used primarily to treat which of the following conditions?
questions.explanation: G-CSF stimulates the production of granulocytes (neutrophils). It is standard therapy to recover neutrophil counts following myelosuppressive chemotherapy. Anemia is treated with erythropoietin. Thrombocythemia and Polycythemia are myeloproliferative disorders where growth factors are not indicated. ITP involves platelet destruction.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which red cell index represents the average weight of hemoglobin in an individual red blood cell?
questions.explanation: MCH measures the average content (weight) of hemoglobin per red cell. MCV measures the average volume (size). MCHC measures the average concentration of hemoglobin in a given volume of packed red cells. RDW measures the variation in red cell size (anisocytosis).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Deficiency of Vitamin K leads to an impaired synthesis of which group of coagulation factors?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K is essential for the post-translational gamma-carboxylation of glutamic acid residues on Factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X, as well as Protein C and S. Deficiency of Vitamin K results in inactive forms of these factors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old man is evaluated for anemia. Laboratory results show low serum iron, high Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC), and low serum ferritin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia is characterized by low ferritin (depleted stores) and high TIBC (the body's attempt to capture more iron). In anemia of chronic disease, ferritin is typically normal or high (as it is an acute phase reactant) and TIBC is low. Sideroblastic anemia usually shows high serum iron and ferritin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with atrophic glossitis and neurological symptoms including loss of vibration sense. Laboratory tests reveal macrocytic anemia and a positive test for anti-intrinsic factor antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
questions.explanation: Pernicious anemia is a specific form of megaloblastic anemia caused by autoimmune destruction of parietal cells or intrinsic factor (IF), leading to Vitamin B12 deficiency. Unlike folate deficiency, B12 deficiency causes neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord). The presence of anti-IF antibodies confirms the diagnosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: To differentiate between a Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following laboratory investigations is most useful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in a leukemoid reaction (a reactive process) but characteristically low or absent in CML (a malignant process). Both show high TLC and a left shift on peripheral smear. Bone marrow is hypercellular in both.

options.B: Peripheral smear showing shift to left

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5 year old boy presents with repeated painful swelling of the knee joints after minor trauma. His aPTT is significantly prolonged, while PT and bleeding time are normal. Which factor deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) presents with hemarthrosis and a prolonged aPTT (intrinsic pathway). PT (extrinsic pathway) and Bleeding Time (platelet function) remain normal. Factor VII deficiency would prolong PT. Factor X, Prothrombin, and Fibrinogen deficiencies would prolong both PT and aPTT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An acute hemolytic transfusion reaction occurs immediately after the start of an incompatible blood transfusion. This reaction is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are typically due to ABO incompatibility where preformed IgM antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to donor red cells, causing immediate intravascular hemolysis via complement activation. This is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction.

options.E: Release of cytokines by donor leukocytes

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old pregnant female presents with sudden onset of bruising and bleeding from IV sites. Lab results show Hb 9 g/dL, Platelets 40,000/mm3, prolonged PT, prolonged aPTT, and decreased fibrinogen levels. The peripheral smear shows schistocytes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DIC is characterized by the consumption of both platelets and coagulation factors, leading to prolonged PT/aPTT and low fibrinogen. Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia occurring as RBCs shear in fibrin webs. ITP only shows low platelets. TTP shows schistocytes and low platelets but typically has normal PT/aPTT. Hemophilia and vWD do not cause thrombocytopenia or schistocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with pancytopenia. A bone marrow biopsy is performed which reveals a markedly hypocellular marrow with the replacement of hematopoietic elements by fat cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is characterized by pancytopenia in the blood and a "dry tap" or hypocellular marrow replaced by fat. Megaloblastic anemia shows hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic changes. Myelofibrosis shows marrow replacement by collagen/fibrosis. AML shows hypercellular marrow packed with blasts. Iron deficiency does not typically cause pancytopenia or fat replacement.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55 year old patient with Rheumatoid Arthritis has an Hb of 10 g/dL and MCV of 82 fL. Serum iron is low, but serum ferritin is increased. What is the most likely underlying mechanism for this anemia?
questions.explanation: This is Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD). In chronic inflammation (like RA), Hepcidin levels rise, which blocks iron release from macrophages and decreases iron absorption. This leads to low serum iron despite high/normal storage iron (ferritin). Chronic blood loss leads to IDA (low ferritin). Impaired DNA synthesis is megaloblastic anemia.

options.A: Chronic blood loss from GI tract
options.B: Sequestration of iron in macrophages by Hepcidin
options.C: Autoimmune destruction of red cell precursors
options.D: Impaired DNA synthesis in the marrow

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 22 year old female presents with a history of excessive bleeding during menstruation and frequent epistaxis. Lab tests show a normal platelet count, prolonged Bleeding Time, and a mildly prolonged aPTT. Platelet aggregation studies show a failure to aggregate with Ristocetin. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It results in defective platelet adhesion (prolonged bleeding time and abnormal ristocetin cofactor assay) and often low Factor VIII levels (prolonged aPTT). Bernard-Soulier also has abnormal ristocetin studies but typically involves thrombocytopenia and giant platelets. Glanzmann has abnormal aggregation with ADP/Collagen but normal ristocetin studies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An Rh-negative mother is pregnant with her second child. The first child was Rh-positive. If the mother was not treated with anti-D gammaglobulin after her first pregnancy, the second Rh-positive fetus is at risk of:
questions.explanation: Hemolytic disease of the newborn (Erythroblastosis fetalis) occurs when maternal IgG antibodies (formed during the first Rh-positive pregnancy) cross the placenta and destroy fetal Rh-positive red cells. This causes severe anemia and hydrops fetalis. Leukemia, Hemophilia, and Aplastic crisis are not related to Rh-sensitization.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25 year old female presents with fatigue. Her blood report shows a Hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification of her anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: An MCV below 80 fL is defined as microcytic, and iron deficiency (the most common cause) typically results in decreased hemoglobin concentration per cell (hypochromic). Debunking: A is incorrect as macrocytic implies MCV over 100 fL. C is incorrect as normocytic implies MCV between 80 to 100 fL. D is incorrect because macrocytic cells are rarely hypochromic in standard classifications. E is incorrect because microcytic anemias almost always involve a parallel drop in MCHC, making them hypochromic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which hematopoietic growth factor is primarily responsible for the proliferation and differentiation of progenitor cells into mature erythrocytes?
questions.explanation: Logic: Erythropoietin is produced by the kidneys in response to hypoxia and specifically drives the production of red blood cells. Debunking: A is incorrect as G-CSF stimulates neutrophil production. B is incorrect as IL-11 is primarily involved in platelet production. D is incorrect as Thrombopoietin stimulates megakaryocytes to produce platelets. E is incorrect as Stem cell factor acts on early pleuripotent cells rather than the specific erythroid lineage.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An elderly male presents with chronic blood loss due to occult gastrointestinal bleeding. His peripheral smear shows pencil-shaped red cells and an MCV of 70 fL. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Logic: Chronic blood loss is the leading cause of iron deficiency in adults, characterized by microcytic anemia and specialized shapes like pencil cells. Debunking: A and B are incorrect because they cause macrocytic anemia (MCV over 100 fL). D is incorrect as it presents with normocytic pancytopenia and a hypocellular marrow. E is incorrect as it presents with spherocytes and usually a normal or slightly low MCV with increased MCHC.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group O negative requires an emergency transfusion. Which of the following blood groups can safely donate to this patient?
questions.explanation: Logic: Individuals with blood group O have anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma, so they can only receive type O blood. Being Rh negative, they must receive Rh negative blood to avoid sensitization. Debunking: A, B, and C are incorrect because the patient's anti-A and anti-B antibodies would cause immediate hemolysis. E is incorrect because the Rh-positive D antigen would react with the patient's potential anti-D antibodies (especially if previously sensitized).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following conditions is a characteristic cause of eosinophilia in a peripheral blood film?
questions.explanation: Logic: Eosinophilia is traditionally associated with allergic reactions and parasitic infections (helminths). Debunking: A is incorrect as viral infections usually cause lymphocytosis. B is incorrect as bacterial infections typically cause neutrophilia. D and E are incorrect as they affect the red cell lineage, not the eosinophil count.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of white blood cell disorders, which term is used to describe a total white cell count exceeding the normal upper limit?
questions.explanation: Logic: Leukocytosis is defined as an increase in the total number of white blood cells. Debunking: A is incorrect as leukopenia refers to a decrease in WBCs. C is incorrect as pancytopenia refers to a decrease in all three blood cell lines (RBC, WBC, Platelets). D is incorrect as it specifically refers to low neutrophils. E is incorrect as it refers to a severe lack of granulocytes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30 year old pregnant female is diagnosed with megaloblastic anemia. Which finding on her peripheral blood smear is most diagnostic for this condition?
questions.explanation: Logic: Megaloblastic anemia (due to B12 or Folate deficiency) is characterized by macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes). Debunking: A is incorrect as it describes iron deficiency. C is incorrect as target cells are seen in Thalassemia or liver disease. D is incorrect as schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. E is incorrect as spherocytes are seen in hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following is the first line laboratory investigation to assess the extrinsic pathway of coagulation?
questions.explanation: Logic: Prothrombin Time (PT) measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. Debunking: A is incorrect as APTT measures the intrinsic pathway. B is incorrect as BT measures platelet function and vessel wall integrity. D and E are incorrect as they are more specialized tests for fibrinogen and clot stability.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops fever, chills, and hemoglobinuria within minutes of the start. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Logic: Acute hemolytic reactions occur within minutes/hours due to ABO incompatibility, presenting with fever, chills, and intravascular hemolysis (hemoglobinuria). Debunking: A is incorrect as allergic reactions usually present with hives/urticaria without hemolysis. B is incorrect as delayed reactions occur days later. D is incorrect as febrile non-hemolytic reactions do not cause hemoglobinuria. E is incorrect as it is a chronic complication of multiple transfusions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 12 year old boy of Mediterranean descent presents with jaundice and splenomegaly. Lab results show Hb 7 g/dL, MCV 65 fL, and a high RBC count. Mentzer index (MCV/RBC) is less than 13. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Logic: Thalassemia presents with very low MCV but often a high RBC count (ineffective erythropoiesis), leading to a Mentzer index below 13. Debunking: A is incorrect because in iron deficiency, the RBC count is typically low, making the Mentzer index above 13. C is incorrect as it is macrocytic. D is incorrect as it is usually normocytic and diagnosed by Hb electrophoresis showing HbS. E is incorrect as it shows dimorphic smear and ring sideroblasts in the marrow.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 40 year old patient presents with generalized weakness and mucosal bleeding. A bone marrow biopsy shows a cellularity of 10 percent with the remaining space filled with fat cells. Which condition does this finding describe?
questions.explanation: Logic: Aplastic anemia is characterized by a hypocellular bone marrow where hematopoietic elements are replaced by fat. Debunking: A and B are incorrect as leukemias show hypercellular marrow with malignant cells. D is incorrect as myelofibrosis shows excessive collagen/fibrosis in the marrow. E is incorrect as megaloblastic anemia shows hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic changes in precursors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with chronic renal failure presents with normocytic normochromic anemia. What is the primary mechanism leading to this anemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: The peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney produce erythropoietin; in renal failure, this production drops, causing normocytic anemia. Debunking: A is incorrect as it describes the mechanism of anemia of chronic disease. C is incorrect as while it may occur in uremia, it is not the primary cause. D is incorrect as renal failure does not affect B12 absorption. E is incorrect as this would lead to microcytic anemia over time.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60 year old male smoker presents with a high Hemoglobin level of 19 g/dL. His serum erythropoietin levels are found to be significantly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this polycythemia?
questions.explanation: Logic: Secondary polycythemia occurs when hypoxia (from smoking/COPD) triggers increased erythropoietin production. Debunking: A is incorrect because Polycythemia Vera is a primary disorder where EPO levels are actually low. C is incorrect as dehydration causes relative polycythemia (decreased plasma volume) without increased EPO. D is incorrect as exogenous EPO would suppress endogenous production. E is incorrect as it involves platelets, not red cells.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 7 year old child presents with petechiae and epistaxis after a viral infection. Platelet count is 20,000/microL. PT and APTT are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Logic: ITP is a common post-viral condition in children involving isolated thrombocytopenia (low platelets) with normal coagulation profiles (PT/APTT). Debunking: A is incorrect as Hemophilia A affects APTT. B is incorrect as vWD usually involves prolonged bleeding time and sometimes APTT. D is incorrect as DIC would show abnormal PT, APTT, and low fibrinogen. E is incorrect as Vitamin K deficiency prolongs the PT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with a total WBC count of 50,000/microL shows a high Neutrophil Alkaline Phosphatase (NAP) score. The peripheral smear shows a shifted-to-the-left pattern with toxic granulations and Dohle bodies. What is the most likely interpretation?
questions.explanation: Logic: A leukemoid reaction (reactive leukocytosis to infection) presents with high NAP scores and toxic granulations, whereas CML presents with low NAP scores. Debunking: A is incorrect as CML typically has a low/absent NAP score. B is incorrect as ALL would show blasts, not a neutrophilic shift. D is incorrect as it primarily affects red cells. E is incorrect as it involves low cell counts (leukopenia).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory findings best distinguishes Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD) from Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA)?
questions.explanation: Logic: In IDA, ferritin (stored iron) is low. In ACD, iron is trapped in macrophages due to hepcidin, so ferritin levels are normal or high. Debunking: A, B, D, and E are incorrect because they can be found in both conditions, making them non-distinguishing. Low serum iron is common to both as iron is either missing (IDA) or sequestered (ACD).

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with microcytic anemia. Iron studies show: Low Serum Iron, High Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC), and Low Ferritin. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?
questions.explanation: Logic: The pattern of low iron, low ferritin, and high TIBC is classic for Iron Deficiency Anemia, commonly caused by chronic blood loss. Debunking: A is incorrect as lead poisoning shows basophilic stippling. B is incorrect as ACD has low TIBC and high ferritin. D is incorrect as sideroblastic anemia has high serum iron and ferritin. E is incorrect as Thalassemia trait usually has normal iron studies.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with macrocytic anemia and neurological symptoms (loss of vibration sense). Laboratory tests show increased levels of both Methylmalonic Acid (MMA) and Homocysteine. Which deficiency is most likely?
questions.explanation: Logic: Both B12 and Folate deficiency cause high homocysteine, but only B12 deficiency leads to high Methylmalonic Acid and neurological damage. Debunking: A is incorrect as MMA is normal in folate deficiency. C is incorrect as it causes microcytic anemia and no MMA elevation. D is incorrect as B6 deficiency causes sideroblastic (microcytic) anemia. E is incorrect as Vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) does not cause megaloblastic changes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 55 year old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents with severe anemia. Bone marrow shows erythroid hyperplasia with numerous Ring Sideroblasts. What is the biochemical defect in this condition?
questions.explanation: Logic: Sideroblastic anemia (often alcohol-induced) involves a defect in protoporphyrin synthesis, causing iron to accumulate in the mitochondria of RBC precursors (ring sideroblasts). Debunking: A is incorrect as it describes Thalassemia. C is incorrect as it describes Pernicious anemia. D is incorrect as it describes Megaloblastic anemia. E is incorrect as it describes Hereditary Spherocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 22-year-old medical student undergoes a routine blood test which shows an Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 88 fL. Which of the following morphological categories does this value fall into?
questions.explanation: The normal range for MCV is typically 80 to 100 fL, which defines normocytic red blood cells. Microcytic refers to an MCV less than 80 fL. Macrocytic refers to an MCV greater than 100 fL. Megaloblastic is a specific subtype of macrocytic anemia characterized by impaired DNA synthesis. Hypochromic refers to the hemoglobin content (MCHC), not the cell size.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with pica and spoon-shaped nails (koilonychia). Her peripheral blood smear shows small, pale red blood cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia typically presents as microcytic hypochromic anemia often associated with pica and koilonychia. Vitamin B12 and Folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anemia. Hereditary spherocytosis shows normocytic cells with increased MCHC. Aplastic anemia is usually normocytic and normochromic.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In the classification of white blood cell disorders, which of the following is considered a benign reactive change characterized by an absolute increase in the neutrophil count?
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia is a reactive leukocytosis where the absolute neutrophil count is increased, often in response to acute bacterial infections or inflammation. Lymphocytosis is an increase in lymphocytes (viral infections). Eosinophilia is seen in parasitic infections or allergies. Monocytopenia is a decrease in monocytes. Basophilia is rare and often associated with myeloproliferative neoplasms.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with blood group O negative requires an emergency transfusion. Which of the following blood groups can safely donate packed red cells to this patient?
questions.explanation: Individuals with blood group O negative have anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma and lack the Rh(D) antigen; therefore, they can only receive O negative blood. A, B, and AB groups contain antigens that would react with the recipient's antibodies. O positive blood contains the Rh(D) antigen which would trigger an immune response in an Rh-negative recipient.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which laboratory investigation is used as the first-line screening test to evaluate the extrinsic and common pathways of the coagulation cascade?
questions.explanation: Prothrombin time (PT) measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways (Factors X, V, II, I). APTT evaluates the intrinsic and common pathways. Bleeding time and platelet count assess primary hemostasis (platelet plug formation). Thrombin time specifically evaluates the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Megaloblastic anemia is primarily characterized by a defect in which of the following cellular processes?
questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia (due to B12 or folate deficiency) results from impaired DNA synthesis, leading to delayed nuclear maturation while cytoplasmic maturation progresses normally (nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony). Defective hemoglobin synthesis occurs in iron deficiency or thalassemias. Cell membrane defects occur in hereditary spherocytosis.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following findings on a peripheral blood smear is most suggestive of an active response to acute blood loss or hemolytic anemia?
questions.explanation: Reticulocytosis (increased immature RBCs) indicates that the bone marrow is actively responding to anemia by increasing production. Target cells are seen in liver disease or thalassemias. Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolysis. Howell-Jolly bodies suggest splenic dysfunction. Acanthocytes are seen in abetalipoproteinemia or liver disease.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary clinical use of recombinant Granulocyte-Macrophage Colony-Stimulating Factor (GM-CSF)?
questions.explanation: GM-CSF and G-CSF are used to stimulate the production of white blood cells (myeloid cells) following chemotherapy or bone marrow transplantation. Erythropoietin is used for renal anemia. Platelet disorders are not treated with GM-CSF. Hemophilia A requires Factor VIII replacement.

options.A: Treatment of chronic renal failure anemia
options.B: Acceleration of myeloid recovery after bone marrow transplant

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with 80 percent neutrophils and the presence of toxic granulations and Dohle bodies. This condition is best described as:
questions.explanation: Reactive neutrophilia in response to infection/inflammation is characterized by a high neutrophil count accompanied by toxic granulations and Dohle bodies (remnants of rough ER). Leukemia would show abnormal blasts or a massive increase in counts (>50,000). Agranulocytosis is a severe lack of neutrophils.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 65-year-old male with a history of total gastrectomy 5 years ago presents with numbness in his feet and a staggering gait. A blood count reveals an MCV of 115 fL. Which finding would you expect on the peripheral smear?
questions.explanation: Gastrectomy leads to loss of intrinsic factor and Vitamin B12 deficiency (Megaloblastic anemia). Macrocytic anemia (MCV > 100 fL) with hypersegmented neutrophils (more than 5 lobes) is the classic peripheral smear finding. Spherocytes and sickle cells are associated with hemolytic anemias. Hyposegmented neutrophils (Pelger-Huet anomaly) are seen in myelodysplastic syndromes.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old male is diagnosed with a bleeding disorder. Laboratory tests show a prolonged bleeding time but a normal PT and normal APTT. Which of the following is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Bleeding time evaluates the primary hemostatic plug (platelets and vessel wall). A prolonged bleeding time with normal PT/APTT suggests a platelet quantitative (thrombocytopenia) or qualitative defect. Hemophilia A (Factor VIII) and B (Factor IX) would prolong the APTT. Vitamin K deficiency and liver disease typically prolong the PT.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a blood transfusion, a patient suddenly develops high fever, hypotension, and red-colored urine. The transfusion is stopped immediately. What is the most likely immunological mechanism?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reactions are typically Type II hypersensitivity reactions where recipient IgM antibodies (anti-A or anti-B) bind to donor RBC antigens, activating the complement system and causing rapid intravascular hemolysis. IgE causes allergic/anaphylactic reactions. T-cell reactions are involved in GVHD.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Anemia of chronic disease is often distinguished from iron deficiency anemia by which of the following laboratory findings?
questions.explanation: Both conditions may show low serum iron. However, in iron deficiency, TIBC is high (as the body tries to find more iron), whereas in anemia of chronic disease, TIBC is low (due to inflammation-induced hepcidin sequestering iron). Serum ferritin is high or normal in chronic disease but low in iron deficiency.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Bone marrow aspiration in a 15-year-old girl with severe pancytopenia reveals a hypocellular marrow with most of the marrow space replaced by fat cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Aplastic anemia is defined by pancytopenia (low RBC, WBC, and platelets) and a hypocellular bone marrow where hematopoietic cells are replaced by fat. Myelofibrosis involves replacement by fibrous tissue. Megaloblastic and Iron deficiency anemias usually show hypercellular marrows as the body tries to compensate.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) is being differentiated from a patient with a severe leukemoid reaction. Which of the following findings would most strongly support a diagnosis of CML?
questions.explanation: The Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) is the hallmark of CML. A leukemoid reaction (reactive) typically shows a high LAP score and toxic granulations, whereas CML shows a very low LAP score. While splenomegaly can occur in CML, it is not as definitive as the cytogenetic marker.

options.D: History of a severe bacterial infection

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 50-year-old male with chronic kidney disease receives recombinant Erythropoietin (EPO) therapy. He subsequently develops a sudden, severe drop in red cell production but normal white cell and platelet counts. Bone marrow shows a complete absence of erythroid precursors. What is the most likely mechanism?
questions.explanation: Pure Red Cell Aplasia (PRCA) can occur as a rare adverse effect of recombinant EPO therapy due to the development of neutralizing anti-erythropoietin antibodies that cross-react with endogenous EPO. This results in an isolated loss of erythroid precursors in the marrow. Iron or folate deficiency would not cause a complete absence of precursors.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with suspected Von Willebrand Disease (vWD), the laboratory results show a prolonged bleeding time and a prolonged APTT. What is the reason for the prolonged APTT in this patient?
questions.explanation: Von Willebrand Factor (vWF) has two roles: it mediates platelet adhesion (evaluated by bleeding time) and it serves as a carrier protein for Factor VIII, protecting it from degradation. Therefore, a deficiency in vWF leads to a secondary decrease in Factor VIII levels, which prolongs the APTT.

options.C: vWF acts as a carrier and stabilizer for Factor VIII
options.D: vWF is required for the activation of Factor X
options.E: vWF directly activates the extrinsic pathway

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A clinician is evaluating a patient for Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA). Which combination of laboratory findings would confirm this diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MAHA is a mechanical hemolytic process (non-immune) characterized by fragmented RBCs called schistocytes on the peripheral smear. Because it is mechanical and not antibody-mediated, the Coombs test (DAT) is negative. Spherocytes with a positive Coombs test indicate autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following hematopoietic growth factors is specifically utilized to treat patients with chronic immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) by acting as a thrombopoietin receptor agonist?
questions.explanation: Eltrombopag and Romiplostim are thrombopoietin receptor agonists used to stimulate platelet production in chronic ITP. Filgrastim is G-CSF (neutrophils). Sargramostim is GM-CSF. Oprelvekin is IL-11, used for chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia but less commonly for ITP due to side effects. Erythropoietin acts on RBCs.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 25-year-old male with chronic kidney disease presents with severe pallor. Lab reports show a low hemoglobin level. Which hematopoietic growth factor is most likely deficient in this patient?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin is primarily produced by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. In chronic kidney disease, its production fails, leading to normocytic anemia. Debunking: A is involved in platelet production. C is specific for neutrophil production. D is a multi-lineage factor but not specific for red cell mass maintenance. E stimulates both granulocytes and monocytes.

options.A: Thrombopoietin [B]: Erythropoietin [C]: Granulocyte-colony stimulating factor [D]: Interleukin-3 [E]: Granulocyte-monocyte colony stimulating factor
options.B: Erythropoietin [C]: Granulocyte-colony stimulating factor [D]: Interleukin-3 [E]: Granulocyte-monocyte colony stimulating factor
options.C: Granulocyte-colony stimulating factor [D]: Interleukin-3 [E]: Granulocyte-monocyte colony stimulating factor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following indices is the most helpful in classifying an anemia as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic?
questions.explanation: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) measures the average size of red blood cells. A value below 80 fL indicates microcytic, 80-100 fL is normocytic, and above 100 fL is macrocytic. Debunking: A and B refer to the hemoglobin content and concentration within the cells (color). D measures the variation in red cell size (anisocytosis). E reflects the bone marrow's regenerative response.

options.A: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) [B]: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) [C]: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) [D]: Red cell distribution width (RDW) [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.B: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) [C]: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) [D]: Red cell distribution width (RDW) [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.C: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) [D]: Red cell distribution width (RDW) [E]: Reticulocyte count
options.D: Red cell distribution width (RDW) [E]: Reticulocyte count

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A person with blood group O negative is considered the universal donor because their red blood cells lack which of the following?
questions.explanation: Blood group O lacks A and B antigens, and Rh negative lacks the D antigen. Since no major antigens are present on the donor's red cells, the recipient's antibodies have nothing to attack. Debunking: A is incorrect because group O individuals have anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma. B and C are incomplete as both ABO and Rh status must be considered. E is incorrect because H substance is the precursor for A and B antigens and is present in group O.

options.A: Antibodies in the plasma [B]: Rh factor only [C]: A and B antigens only [D]: A, B, and Rh antigens [E]: H substance
options.B: Rh factor only [C]: A and B antigens only [D]: A, B, and Rh antigens [E]: H substance
options.C: A and B antigens only [D]: A, B, and Rh antigens [E]: H substance
options.D: A, B, and Rh antigens [E]: H substance

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 30-year-old female presents with fatigue and pica. Her peripheral film shows microcytic hypochromic red blood cells. What is the most common cause of this condition worldwide?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency is the most common cause of microcytic hypochromic anemia globally, often due to nutritional deficiency or chronic blood loss. Debunking: A and B cause macrocytic anemia. D usually causes normocytic anemia (Anemia of Chronic Disease). E causes pancytopenia with normocytic cells.

options.A: Vitamin B12 deficiency [B]: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Chronic inflammation [E]: Bone marrow aplasia
options.B: Folate deficiency [C]: Iron deficiency [D]: Chronic inflammation [E]: Bone marrow aplasia
options.C: Iron deficiency [D]: Chronic inflammation [E]: Bone marrow aplasia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a routine blood examination, a patient is found to have a total leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3. Which of the following terms correctly describes this finding?
questions.explanation: Leukocytosis is defined as an increase in the total white blood cell count above the normal reference range (usually >11,000/mm3). Debunking: A is a decrease in WBCs. C is a malignant proliferation of WBCs. D is an extreme elevation (>50,000) mimicking leukemia. E is a decrease in all three blood cell lines.

options.A: Leukopenia [B]: Leukocytosis [C]: Leukemia [D]: Leukemoid reaction [E]: Pancytopenia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following cells is the first to respond and increase in number during an acute pyogenic bacterial infection?
questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the primary cells of acute inflammation and pyogenic bacterial infections; an increase in their number is called neutrophilia. Debunking: A increases in viral infections. B increases in chronic infections like tuberculosis. C increases in parasitic infections and allergies. E is associated with immediate hypersensitivity reactions.

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is suspected of having a primary bleeding disorder. The laboratory reports a prolonged bleeding time but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT) and Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). Which of the following is the most likely culprit?
questions.explanation: Bleeding time is a measure of platelet function and primary hemostasis. Prolongation with normal PT/APTT points to a platelet problem or von Willebrand disease. Debunking: A, B, D, and E are coagulation factor deficiencies which would prolong PT or APTT.

options.A: Factor VIII deficiency [B]: Factor IX deficiency [C]: Platelet dysfunction [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Prothrombin deficiency
options.B: Factor IX deficiency [C]: Platelet dysfunction [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Prothrombin deficiency
options.C: Platelet dysfunction [D]: Factor VII deficiency [E]: Prothrombin deficiency

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary physiological stimulus for the release of Erythropoietin?
questions.explanation: The kidney senses low oxygen delivery (hypoxia) via hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha, which triggers EPO release. High altitude decreases oxygen saturation, stimulating this pathway. Debunking: A, C, and D do not directly stimulate EPO. E (high CO2) affects respiration but not EPO production directly.

options.A: Low blood pressure [B]: High altitude hypoxia [C]: Increased blood volume [D]: Leukocytosis [E]: Hypercapnia
options.B: High altitude hypoxia [C]: Increased blood volume [D]: Leukocytosis [E]: Hypercapnia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In megaloblastic anemia, what is the underlying molecular defect causing the characteristic large red cell precursors?
questions.explanation: Deficiencies in Vitamin B12 or Folate impair the synthesis of thymidine, a building block of DNA. This leads to nuclear-cytoplasmic asynchrony where the nucleus matures slowly while the cytoplasm grows. Debunking: A describes iron deficiency or thalassemia. D describes sideroblastic anemia. E describes hereditary spherocytosis.

options.A: Impaired hemoglobin synthesis [B]: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Increased RNA destruction [D]: Iron overload in mitochondria [E]: Defective cell membrane proteins
options.B: Impaired DNA synthesis [C]: Increased RNA destruction [D]: Iron overload in mitochondria [E]: Defective cell membrane proteins
options.C: Increased RNA destruction [D]: Iron overload in mitochondria [E]: Defective cell membrane proteins
options.D: Iron overload in mitochondria [E]: Defective cell membrane proteins

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following blood groups contains anti-A and anti-B antibodies in the serum but no A or B antigens on the red cell surface?
questions.explanation: Landsteiner's Law states that if an antigen is absent on the RBC, the corresponding antibody must be present in the serum. Group O has neither A nor B antigens, thus both antibodies are present. Debunking: A has anti-B. B has anti-A. C has neither antibody. E is a rare type lacking H substance.

options.A: Group A [B]: Group B [C]: Group AB [D]: Group O [E]: Group Bombay
options.B: Group B [C]: Group AB [D]: Group O [E]: Group Bombay
options.C: Group AB [D]: Group O [E]: Group Bombay

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 45-year-old male presents with iron deficiency anemia. Which of the following sets of laboratory findings is most characteristic of this condition?
questions.explanation: In iron deficiency, iron stores (Ferritin) are depleted first. Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC) increases as the body attempts to capture more iron. Serum iron eventually falls. Debunking: A describes Anemia of Chronic Disease. C describes Hemochromatosis/Iron Overload. D and E are inconsistent with the physiological patterns of iron kinetics.

options.A: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin [B]: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin [C]: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin [D]: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin [E]: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, High Ferritin
options.B: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, Low Ferritin [C]: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin [D]: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin [E]: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, High Ferritin
options.C: High Serum Iron, Low TIBC, High Ferritin [D]: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin [E]: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, High Ferritin
options.D: Normal Serum Iron, Normal TIBC, Low Ferritin [E]: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, High Ferritin
options.E: Low Serum Iron, High TIBC, High Ferritin

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 60-year-old male with a history of a total gastrectomy presents with paresthesia in his feet and a staggering gait. A peripheral smear shows macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Gastrectomy leads to loss of Intrinsic Factor, causing B12 malabsorption. B12 deficiency is unique among megaloblastic anemias for causing subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (neurological symptoms). Debunking: B causes identical blood findings but no neurological deficits. A and D are microcytic. E shows a dry tap/hypocellular marrow without hypersegmented neutrophils.

options.A: Iron deficiency anemia [B]: Folate deficiency anemia [C]: Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia [D]: Thalassemia minor [E]: Aplastic anemia
options.B: Folate deficiency anemia [C]: Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia [D]: Thalassemia minor [E]: Aplastic anemia
options.C: Vitamin B12 deficiency anemia [D]: Thalassemia minor [E]: Aplastic anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: An Rh-negative mother is giving birth to her second child, who is Rh-positive. The first child was also Rh-positive, and no prophylaxis was given. The newborn develops severe jaundice and hydrops fetalis. This reaction is primarily mediated by:
questions.explanation: Erythroblastosis fetalis occurs when maternal IgG antibodies (small enough to cross the placenta) against the D antigen coat fetal RBCs, leading to extravascular hemolysis. Debunking: A is Type I hypersensitivity. B (IgM) cannot cross the placenta. D and E are not the mechanisms for hemolytic disease of the newborn.

options.A: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation [B]: IgM-mediated complement activation [C]: IgG-mediated placental transfer and hemolysis [D]: IgA-mediated mucosal response [E]: Cytotoxic T-cell infiltration
options.B: IgM-mediated complement activation [C]: IgG-mediated placental transfer and hemolysis [D]: IgA-mediated mucosal response [E]: Cytotoxic T-cell infiltration
options.C: IgG-mediated placental transfer and hemolysis [D]: IgA-mediated mucosal response [E]: Cytotoxic T-cell infiltration
options.D: IgA-mediated mucosal response [E]: Cytotoxic T-cell infiltration

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During an investigation of a bleeding patient, the laboratory finds a prolonged Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT) but a normal Prothrombin Time (PT). This indicates a defect in which pathway?
questions.explanation: APTT measures the intrinsic (Factors XII, XI, IX, VIII) and common pathways. PT measures the extrinsic (Factor VII) and common pathways. A prolonged APTT with normal PT isolates the intrinsic pathway. Debunking: A would prolong PT. C would prolong both PT and APTT. D and E are not measured by these tests.

options.A: Extrinsic pathway [B]: Intrinsic pathway [C]: Common pathway [D]: Platelet aggregation [E]: Fibrinolysis
options.B: Intrinsic pathway [C]: Common pathway [D]: Platelet aggregation [E]: Fibrinolysis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 22-year-old medical student presents with a sore throat, fever, and cervical lymphadenopathy. A peripheral smear shows 60 percent lymphocytes, many of which are large with abundant "ballerina-skirt" cytoplasm indenting the RBCs. What is the most likely cause of this leukocytosis?
questions.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis (EBV) causes reactive T-cell lymphocytosis. These atypical lymphocytes are CD8+ T cells reacting against B cells. Debunking: A would show blasts with Auer rods. B would cause neutrophilia. D occurs in elderly patients with small, mature smudge cells. E causes eosinophilia.

options.A: Acute Myeloid Leukemia [B]: Staphylococcus aureus infection [C]: Infectious Mononucleosis [D]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia [E]: Parasitic infestation
options.B: Staphylococcus aureus infection [C]: Infectious Mononucleosis [D]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia [E]: Parasitic infestation
options.C: Infectious Mononucleosis [D]: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia [E]: Parasitic infestation

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Minutes after starting a blood transfusion, a patient develops chills, back pain, and dark-colored urine. Laboratory results show hemoglobinemia and a positive direct Coombs test. What is the most likely mechanism of this reaction?
questions.explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction is usually due to preformed IgM antibodies against A or B antigens in the recipient, causing rapid complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis. Debunking: A causes urticaria/anaphylaxis. B occurs days later and is usually extravascular. D causes high fever and septic shock. E presents with respiratory distress and pulmonary edema.

options.A: IgE-mediated Type I hypersensitivity [B]: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction [C]: Acute intravascular hemolysis due to ABO incompatibility [D]: Bacterial contamination of the blood unit [E]: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
options.B: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction [C]: Acute intravascular hemolysis due to ABO incompatibility [D]: Bacterial contamination of the blood unit [E]: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
options.C: Acute intravascular hemolysis due to ABO incompatibility [D]: Bacterial contamination of the blood unit [E]: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)
options.D: Bacterial contamination of the blood unit [E]: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient presents with a very high WBC count of 60,000/mm3. To differentiate between a Leukemoid Reaction and Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), which of the following tests is most useful?
questions.explanation: The LAP score is high in reactive states like a leukemoid reaction (normal/active neutrophils) but low in CML (malignant neutrophils lack the enzyme). Debunking: A and C are for anemias. D is for primary hemostasis. E is for hemoglobinopathies like sickle cell or thalassemia.

options.A: Reticulocyte count [B]: Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [C]: Serum Ferritin [D]: Bleeding Time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
options.B: Leukocyte Alkaline Phosphatase (LAP) score [C]: Serum Ferritin [D]: Bleeding Time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
options.C: Serum Ferritin [D]: Bleeding Time [E]: Hemoglobin electrophoresis

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with megaloblastic anemia, a bone marrow biopsy is performed. Which of the following findings is most consistent with this diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Despite the anemia in the blood, the marrow is hypercellular because it is trying to compensate, but the precursors (megaloblasts) are defective and undergo intramedullary apoptosis (ineffective erythropoiesis). Debunking: A is Aplastic Anemia. C is Sideroblastic Anemia. D is Myelofibrosis. E is Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia.

options.A: Hypocellularity with increased fat spaces [B]: Hypercellularity with megaloblastic erythroid hyperplasia [C]: Presence of ringed sideroblasts [D]: Myelofibrosis with "dry tap" [E]: Massive infiltration of lymphoblasts
options.B: Hypercellularity with megaloblastic erythroid hyperplasia [C]: Presence of ringed sideroblasts [D]: Myelofibrosis with "dry tap" [E]: Massive infiltration of lymphoblasts
options.C: Presence of ringed sideroblasts [D]: Myelofibrosis with "dry tap" [E]: Massive infiltration of lymphoblasts
options.D: Myelofibrosis with "dry tap" [E]: Massive infiltration of lymphoblasts

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old boy presents with spontaneous bruising and joint swelling (hemarthrosis). Lab tests show: PT: Normal, APTT: Prolonged, Bleeding Time: Normal, Platelet Count: Normal. Mixing the patient's plasma with normal plasma corrects the APTT. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) presents with deep tissue bleeding (hemarthrosis) and a prolonged APTT that corrects with normal plasma (indicating a deficiency, not an inhibitor). Debunking: A would likely prolong Bleeding Time. C would show a low platelet count. D and E would prolong both PT and APTT.

options.A: Von Willebrand Disease [B]: Hemophilia A [C]: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.B: Hemophilia A [C]: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.C: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) [D]: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
options.D: Vitamin K deficiency [E]: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient has an MCV of 72 fL, a low MCH, and a high RDW. The peripheral smear shows many "pencil cells" (elliptocytes). Which stage of iron deficiency is this patient likely in?
questions.explanation: Microcytosis (low MCV) and hypochromia (low MCH) with morphological changes like pencil cells only occur once iron deficiency is severe enough to cause manifest anemia. Debunking: A and B show normal red cell morphology as hemoglobin production is not yet severely compromised. D would show a dimorphic population (new normal cells vs old microcytic cells). E is the opposite of deficiency.

options.A: Pre-latent (depleted stores) [B]: Latent (iron-deficient erythropoiesis) [C]: Manifest Iron Deficiency Anemia [D]: Recovery phase after iron therapy [E]: Iron overload
options.B: Latent (iron-deficient erythropoiesis) [C]: Manifest Iron Deficiency Anemia [D]: Recovery phase after iron therapy [E]: Iron overload
options.C: Manifest Iron Deficiency Anemia [D]: Recovery phase after iron therapy [E]: Iron overload
options.D: Recovery phase after iron therapy [E]: Iron overload

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 20-year-old female presents with pallor and fatigue. Her lab reports show a Hemoglobin of 9 g/dL and a Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 70 fL. Which of the following is the most likely morphological classification for her anemia?
questions.explanation: An MCV less than 80 fL indicates microcytic anemia, and microcytic cells are typically hypochromic. Option A is incorrect because macrocytic refers to MCV over 100 fL. Option C refers to normal MCV (80-100 fL). Option D is a subtype of macrocytic anemia. Option E is incorrect because microcytosis is almost always accompanied by hypochromia in clinical practice.

options.A: Macrocytic normochromic [B]: Microcytic hypochromic [C]: Normocytic normochromic [D]: Megaloblastic [E]: Microcytic normochromic
options.B: Microcytic hypochromic [C]: Normocytic normochromic [D]: Megaloblastic [E]: Microcytic normochromic

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient with chronic kidney disease has severe anemia due to deficiency of a specific hematopoietic growth factor. This factor is primarily produced by which organ?
questions.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary growth factor for RBC production, secreted by the peritubular interstitial cells of the kidney in response to hypoxia. Option A is incorrect because the liver only produces a small fraction of EPO in adults. Option B is where EPO acts, not where it is produced. Option C and E are not involved in EPO production.

options.A: Liver [B]: Bone marrow [C]: Spleen [D]: Kidney [E]: Thymus

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: During a peripheral blood smear examination, the presence of hypersegmented neutrophils (5 or more lobes) is a hallmark finding of which condition?
questions.explanation: Hypersegmented neutrophils are pathognomonic for megaloblastic anemias (Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency) due to impaired DNA synthesis. Option A shows microcytes and pencil cells. Option B shows a lack of all cell lines. Option D shows drepanocytes (sickle cells). Option E typically shows schistocytes or spherocytes depending on the cause.

options.A: Iron deficiency anemia [B]: Aplastic anemia [C]: Megaloblastic anemia [D]: Sickle cell anemia [E]: Hemolytic anemia
options.B: Aplastic anemia [C]: Megaloblastic anemia [D]: Sickle cell anemia [E]: Hemolytic anemia
options.C: Megaloblastic anemia [D]: Sickle cell anemia [E]: Hemolytic anemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A lab technician is performing a blood grouping. The patient's RBCs agglutinate with both Anti-A and Anti-B sera. What is the blood group of this patient?
questions.explanation: Group AB individuals possess both A and B antigens on their RBCs, thus reacting with both antibodies. Option A would only react with Anti-A. Option B would only react with Anti-B. Option C (Group O) has no antigens and would not react with either. Option E refers to the Rhesus system, not the ABO system.

options.A: Group A [B]: Group B [C]: Group O [D]: Group AB [E]: Group Rh negative
options.B: Group B [C]: Group O [D]: Group AB [E]: Group Rh negative
options.C: Group O [D]: Group AB [E]: Group Rh negative

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which of the following laboratory parameters is the most sensitive early indicator of depleted iron stores in the body?
questions.explanation: Serum ferritin levels reflect total body iron stores and are the first to drop in iron deficiency. Option A fluctuates daily and is not an early marker. Option C increases as stores decrease but is less specific than ferritin. Options D and E only decrease in the late stage (anemia phase) of iron deficiency.

options.A: Serum iron [B]: Serum ferritin [C]: Total iron binding capacity (TIBC) [D]: Hemoglobin concentration [E]: Hematocrit
options.B: Serum ferritin [C]: Total iron binding capacity (TIBC) [D]: Hemoglobin concentration [E]: Hematocrit
options.C: Total iron binding capacity (TIBC) [D]: Hemoglobin concentration [E]: Hematocrit

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A 5-year-old child presents with a high-grade fever and sore throat. The differential leukocyte count shows a significant increase in neutrophils with a shift to the left. This reactive leukocytosis is most likely caused by:
questions.explanation: Neutrophilia with a left shift (immature forms) is the classic response to acute pyogenic bacterial infections. Option A usually causes lymphocytosis. Option B and D cause eosinophilia. Option E is a malignant disorder, not a reactive process.

options.A: Viral infection [B]: Parasitic infestation [C]: Acute bacterial infection [D]: Allergic reaction [E]: Chronic myeloid leukemia
options.B: Parasitic infestation [C]: Acute bacterial infection [D]: Allergic reaction [E]: Chronic myeloid leukemia
options.C: Acute bacterial infection [D]: Allergic reaction [E]: Chronic myeloid leukemia

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which growth factor is specifically used to reduce the duration of neutropenia in patients receiving cancer chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: G-CSF (Filgrastim) stimulates the production of neutrophils. Option A is for RBCs. Option B and D (IL-11) are for platelets. Option E acts on very early pluripotent cells and is not used specifically for neutropenia.

options.A: Erythropoietin [B]: Thrombopoietin [C]: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [D]: Interleukin-11 [E]: Stem Cell Factor
options.B: Thrombopoietin [C]: Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF) [D]: Interleukin-11 [E]: Stem Cell Factor

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: A patient is diagnosed with a bleeding disorder characterized by a prolonged Prothrombin Time (PT) but a normal Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (APTT). Which coagulation factor is likely deficient?
questions.explanation: PT measures the extrinsic pathway, of which Factor VII is the key component. APTT measures the intrinsic pathway. Option A, B, D, and E would all lead to a prolonged APTT (though Factor V is common to both, its deficiency usually prolongs both PT and APTT).

options.A: Factor VIII [B]: Factor IX [C]: Factor VII [D]: Factor XII [E]: Factor V
options.B: Factor IX [C]: Factor VII [D]: Factor XII [E]: Factor V
options.C: Factor VII [D]: Factor XII [E]: Factor V

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: Which blood type is known as the universal donor for packed Red Blood Cells?
questions.explanation: O negative blood lacks A, B, and Rh antigens, making it safe for almost all recipients in emergencies. Option A is the universal recipient for cells. Option B is the universal recipient for plasma. Option C contains Rh antigen and could cause reactions in Rh-negative patients. Option E contains A antigen.

options.A: AB positive [B]: AB negative [C]: O positive [D]: O negative [E]: A negative
options.B: AB negative [C]: O positive [D]: O negative [E]: A negative
options.C: O positive [D]: O negative [E]: A negative

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: What is the primary clinical manifestation of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction due to ABO incompatibility?
questions.explanation: Incompatible ABO transfusion leads to immediate intravascular hemolysis, causing free hemoglobin in the urine (hemoglobinuria), fever, and hypotension. Option A describes a delayed hemolytic reaction. Option C is a simple allergic reaction. Option D occurs after multiple chronic transfusions. Option E is a cellular immune complication.

options.A: Delayed jaundice [B]: Hemoglobinuria and fever [C]: Urticaria [D]: Iron overload [E]: Graft versus host disease

options.C: Urticaria [D]: Iron overload [E]: Graft versus host disease
options.D: Iron overload [E]: Graft versus host disease

questions.lo: HL-Pa-001. Blood cells, platelets and blood group
questions.question: In a patient with Megaloblastic Anemia, the underlying mechanism involves impaired DNA synthesis. Which of the following biochemical abnormalities is responsible for this?
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12 is a cofactor for the enzyme methionine synthase, which converts homocysteine to methionine; a block here traps folate in an unusable form, halting DNA synthesis. Option A describes Sideroblastic anemia. Option C describes hemolytic anemia. Option D describes Thalassemia. Option E describes Porphyria.

options.A: Failure of iron incorporation into heme [B]: Inadequate conversion of homocysteine to methionine [C]: Excessive destruction of RBCs in the spleen [D]: Defective synthesis of alpha-globin chains [E]: Failure of the protoporphyrin ring synthesis
options.B: Inadequate conversion of homocysteine to methionine [C]: Excessive destruction of RBCs in the spleen [D]: Defective synthesis of alpha-globin chains [E]: Failure of the protoporphyrin ring synthesis
options.C: Excessive destruction of RBCs in the spleen [D]: Defective synthesis of alpha-globin chains [E]: Failure of the protoporphyrin ring synthesis
